dimensions.boardOrientation: Light square on right corner (h1 = light)

layout.zones: Back rank (rank 1 & 8), centre (d4,e4,d5,e5), kingside (files e‑h), queenside (files a‑d)

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Cardboard, vinyl, wood, or silicone board
courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor: home, chess club, tournament hall, park (outdoor with weighted pieces)

physical.benefit: Improves hand‑eye coordination (when moving pieces quickly in blitz)
Reduces risk of dementia and Alzheimer's (cognitive reserve)
May lower blood pressure through stress reduction

mental.benefit: Enhances memory and concentration
Develops logical thinking and problem‑solving skills
Improves patience and emotional control
Reduces anxiety through structured thinking

warmup.stretching: Neck rotations, shoulder shrugs, wrist circles (5 min) – prevents stiffness during long games
warmup.pregameExercises: Solve 5 simple tactics puzzles (e.g., mate in 1), review a favourite opening line (5 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Review the game briefly (5 min), stand up and walk, deep breathing (2 min), eye relaxation (palming)

basicRules.rule: Game is played between two players, one controlling white pieces, the other black.
White moves first. Players alternate moves.
Objective: checkmate the opponent's king (king under attack with no escape).

Pieces move: king (1 square any direction), queen (any distance horizontally/vertically/diagonally), rook (any distance horizontally/vertically), bishop (any distance diagonally), knight (L‑shape: 2+1), pawn (forward 1, capture diagonally).
Special moves: castling (king + rook move together), en passant (pawn capture), pawn promotion (to queen, rook, bishop, or knight upon reaching last rank).

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: 2 players (singles). No team variant in standard chess.

breaksIntervals.standard: No scheduled breaks; players may leave board on their turn (but clock runs)
breaksIntervals.tournament: After 2‑3 hours, a 30‑min break may be scheduled (before time control) Allowed during opponent's turn (not recommended during critical moments)

formats.shortMatch: Blitz (3+2) or Bullet (1+0) – fast, intuitive play

rulesRegulations.substitutesAndRoles: No substitutes; each player plays alone. In team events (Olympiad), each board has a designated player.

playersRoles.role: Attacking player – seeks checkmate, sacrifices material
Positional player – builds small advantages, controls squares
Endgame specialist – excels with few pieces

scoringSystem.draw: 0.5 point each (by stalemate, threefold repetition, insufficient material, or mutual agreement)

rulesRegulations.howToWin: Deliver checkmate, or opponent resigns, or opponent runs out of time (in timed games)

illegalMove.penalty: In standard play: move retracted, alternative legal move required; in blitz: loss if illegal move completed
illegalMove.examples: Moving knight as bishop, leaving king in check, moving into check

touchMove.penalty: If you touch a piece, you must move it (if possible). If you adjust a piece, say "j'adoube" (I adjust)

clockViolation.penalty: Loss of game if flag falls (time runs out) – unless opponent cannot checkmate with remaining material

unsportsmanlikeConduct.penalty: Warning, forfeit of game, or expulsion (depending on severity)
unsportsmanlikeConduct.examples: Distracting opponent, offering draw repeatedly, using phone

fairPlay.rule: No outside assistance (books, engines, or other people)
No distracting noises or gestures
Record moves (in classical games) on scoresheet
Shake hands before and after game

judgementCriteria.arbiter: Enforces rules, handles disputes, watches for cheating
judgementCriteria.electronicBoards: Used in top tournaments to broadcast moves
judgementCriteria.antiCheating: Metal detectors, delayed live broadcast, analysis of engine correlation

commonInjuries.injury: Eye strain (dry eyes, headache, blurred vision)
Repetitive strain injury (wrist, forearm from mouse or clock pressing)
Carpal tunnel syndrome (excessive mouse use in online chess)

preventionMethods.method: Use 20‑20‑20 rule: every 20 min, look 20 feet away for 20 seconds
Adjust chair height so elbows at 90° and wrists straight
Take a 2‑minute break after every 30 min of play (stand, stretch, walk)
Use ergonomic mouse and wrist rest for online chess
Blink consciously and use lubricating eye drops if needed
Do neck stretches (chin tucks, side bends) between games

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Migraine or severe headache – bright screens and concentration worsen it
Acute conjunctivitis (pink eye) – risk of spreading in over‑the‑board events
Severe anxiety or panic disorder – timed pressure may trigger episodes
Recent concussion (within 2 weeks) – cognitive rest needed
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: If you have epilepsy triggered by flickering screens, avoid online blitz or use flicker‑free monitors

commonMistakes.mistake: Moving too quickly without calculating opponent's threats
Not developing pieces in opening (moving same piece multiple times)
Leaving pieces undefended (hanging pieces)
Failing to castle early – king remains exposed
Overlooking checks and captures
Playing too passively (no plan, just reacting)
Blundering in time pressure (flagging without thinking)

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 300‑500 ml water 1‑2 hours before; avoid excess (bathroom breaks disrupt focus)
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 100‑150 ml every 30 minutes (keep water bottle on table, allowed in most tournaments)
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Rehydrate with 400‑600 ml water or electrolyte drink
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Excessive caffeine before/during (jitters, frequent urination); sugary drinks (energy crash)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Dry mouth, headache, irritability, reduced concentration – take a sip and breathe

nutritionTips.preGame: 1‑2 hours before: complex carbs (oatmeal, whole grain toast) + protein (eggs, yogurt) + healthy fat (nuts) – avoid heavy meals
nutritionTips.preGameSnack: 30 min before: banana, dark chocolate (70%+), or a handful of almonds (brain fuel)
nutritionTips.duringGame: Only water or unsweetened tea; avoid sugar spikes (lollies, soda). In long games (>3h), small snacks like nuts or dried fruit allowed between moves
nutritionTips.postGame: Protein + carbs (chicken and rice, or Greek yogurt with berries) to replenish mental energy
nutritionTips.daily: Omega‑3 (fish, walnuts), antioxidants (blueberries), B vitamins (whole grains, eggs) for brain health

physicalBurnout.limit: Play serious games max 3‑4 days per week (classical time control)
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 days with no chess, or only light puzzles (10 min)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Physical exercise (walking, swimming) to clear mind

mentalBurnout.signs: Dreading study, making uncharacteristic blunders, loss of enjoyment
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, play casual variants (chess960, bughouse), reduce rating pressure

pieces.material: Plastic (most common), wood (e.g., boxwood), silicone, or marble

scoresheet.format: Paper scoresheet with move numbers (required in classical tournaments)

accessories.item: Chess bag (carry board, pieces, clock)
Pen (for recording moves)
Analysis board for post‑game

protective.item: None required. For physical safety: comfortable chair with back support
Anti‑glare glasses (if sensitive to lighting)
Wrist brace (if prone to repetitive strain)

safetyGear.firstAid: Eye drops, pain reliever (headache), hand sanitizer (for over‑the‑board hygiene)

coordination.desc: Not physical; mental coordination – integrate calculation, memory, and pattern recognition
coordination.drill: Solve tactical puzzles (Chess.com, Lichess) – 15 min daily

speedAgility.desc: Mental speed – quickly recognise threats and candidate moves
speedAgility.drill: Blitz games (3+0) or puzzle rush survival – trains rapid pattern recognition

focusReaction.desc: Maintain concentration for hours, avoid distractions
focusReaction.drill: Play 30‑minute games without looking away from board (use physical board)

strategy.opening: Control centre, develop pieces, king safety (castle)

xFactors.middlegame:
Create weaknesses, improve piece activity, launch attack King activation, pawn promotion, opposition
Evaluate when to trade pieces, when to sacrifice, when to simplify to a winning endgame Choose between candidate moves based on safety, activity, and long‑term plan

dailyPlan.day: Tactics (20 puzzles) + Openings (15 min) + One rapid game (15+10)
Endgame study (30 min: king + pawn vs king) + Analysis of master game (30 min)
Blitz games (10 x 3+0) for pattern recognition – but not for deep learning
Positional exercises (30 min) + Play through annotated game (30 min)
Simulate tournament conditions: one classical game (90+30) with analysis afterwards
Solve advanced tactics (10 puzzles) + Review personal games from week
Rest or light puzzles only (10 min) + physical activity

drillsForBeginners.drill: Mate with king+queen vs king (practice 10 times)
Place knight to fork two pieces – 10 positions
Play 5 games focusing only on centre control, development, castling (ignore tactics)
Given a position, find all checks, captures, and threats – 10 positions

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Learn piece moves, checkmate patterns, basic tactics (forks, pins) – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Study openings (e4/e5), endgame basics (king+pawn), play 30+0 games – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Add positional concepts (weak squares, pawn structure), analyse own games, join local club – 4x per week + weekend tournament
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Maintain with daily puzzles, weekly classical game, opening repertoire refinement

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 4 sec, hold 2 sec, exhale 6 sec – before each move in critical positions
Close eyes, imagine the board and move sequences (3‑4 moves deep) – 5 min daily
Before each game: set up pieces, deep breath, remind yourself of opening plan

pressureHandling.tip: When low on time, play “safe” moves (avoid complications) and rely on intuition
If opponent offers a draw in a winning position, take 10 seconds to recalculate before deciding
Use positive self‑talk: “I have a plan, I can execute it”
After a blunder, don't dwell – look for counterplay or swindles

sportsmanship.rule: Resign only when defeat is certain (don't waste time)
Offer draw only on your move, before pressing clock
Don't distract opponent (tapping fingers, humming)
Congratulate opponent after loss; analyse together if both agree

howToJoinSport.step: Learn rules and basic tactics (free on Chess.com, Lichess, or local library)
Buy a basic chess set ($15‑30) or use free online platforms
Join a local chess club (meetup.com or USCF/FIDE affiliates)
Play rated games online to get a starting rating (e.g., Lichess rapid)
Enter your first tournament: scholastic, open, or novice section at local club
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Online free; club membership $20‑50/year; tournament entry $20‑50; USCF/FIDE membership $20‑60/year

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Patience – waits for opponent to make mistake
Objectivity – evaluates positions without wishful thinking
Resilience – recovers after bad losses
Curiosity – studies openings and endgames continuously
Time management – allocates clock wisely

opening.strategy: Control centre, develop knights before bishops, castle quickly
Pawn chains, positional manoeuvring, slow build‑up
Allow opponent to occupy centre, then attack with pieces (e.g., Nimzo‑Indian)

middlegame.strategy: Open files, sacrifice pieces, bring queen and rooks
Prevent opponent's plans (e.g., move before they do)
Advance pawns on opposite side to create weaknesses

endgame.strategy: Bring king to centre, use as attacking piece
Create and promote a passed pawn
Kings face off with one square between – crucial in pawn endgames

specialSituations.situation: Winning position – trade pieces, simplify, avoid unnecessary risks
Losing position – create complications, set traps, hope for opponent blunder

terminologies.term: Check – king is under attack
Checkmate – king under attack with no legal escape (game ends)
Stalemate – player to move has no legal moves but king not in check (draw)
Fork – single piece attacks two or more enemy pieces
Pin – piece cannot move without exposing a more valuable piece behind it
Skewer – attack on a valuable piece that forces it to move, exposing a less valuable piece behind
Discovery – moving a piece reveals an attack from another piece behind it
Sacrifice – give up material for a positional or attacking advantage
Zugzwang – any move worsens player's position (often in endgames)
Blunder – very bad move that loses material or the game
Increment – extra time added after each move (e.g., 3+2 means 3 min + 2 sec per move)
Flag fall – time runs out on a player's clock
Touch move – rule requiring player to move a piece they touch
En passant – special pawn capture when opponent moves two squares from start
Castling – move with king and rook (king moves two squares towards rook)
Promotion – pawn reaching last rank becomes queen, rook, bishop, or knight
Algebraic notation – standard move recording (e.g., Nf3, exd5, O‑O, Qh8=Q)
Elo rating – numerical measure of player strength (average 1200, grandmaster 2500+)
Blitz – fast time control (3‑5 min per player)
Bullet – very fast (1‑2 min per player)
Rapid – medium (10‑60 min per player)
Classical – long (60+ min per player)
Gambit – opening where a pawn is sacrificed for development (e.g., King's Gambit)
Endgame – stage with few pieces left
Middlegame – after development, before endgame
Opening – first 10‑15 moves of the game
FIDE – International Chess Federation (World Chess governing body)
USCF – United States Chess Federation

dimensions.olympicPool: 50m long x 25m wide (10 lanes, each 2.5m wide)
dimensions.shortCourse: 25m long x 20m wide (8 lanes, each 2.5m wide)

layout.lanes: Lane ropes (cable with floats) separating lanes; lane numbers 0‑9 or 1‑8 At one end (or both ends for backstroke) – height 0.5‑0.75m above water End walls with touchpads (electronic timing) and backstroke turn indicators (flags 5m from wall)

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Ceramic tile, concrete with non‑slip coating, or PVC liner
courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor (50m or 25m pool) or outdoor (seasonal) – must have filtration, lighting, and spectator seating

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance and lung capacity (VO2max)
Full‑body, low‑impact workout (joint‑friendly)
Burns 400‑700 calories per hour (vigorous intensity)
Strengthens muscles: shoulders (deltoids), back (latissimus), core, legs (kick)
Improves flexibility (shoulder rotation, ankle plantarflexion)

mental.benefit: Reduces stress and anxiety (rhythmic breathing, water immersion)
Improves focus and discipline (lap counting, pace control)
Boosts mood through endorphin release

warmup.landStretching: Dynamic: arm circles, trunk twists, leg swings, shoulder rotations, ankle rotations (10 min)
warmup.waterWarmup: Easy swimming (200‑400m mixed strokes), kickboard drills (100m), pull buoy (100m), gradually increase intensity

cooldown.postRace: Easy swimming (200m very slow), float or treading water (2 min), static stretches on deck (hamstring, quad, shoulder, triceps) (30 sec each)

basicRules.rule: Swimmers race in assigned lanes; must stay in lane throughout race (except lane crossing is DQ).
Four competitive strokes: freestyle (front crawl), backstroke, breaststroke, butterfly.
Individual Medley (IM): butterfly → backstroke → breaststroke → freestyle (order fixed).
Starts: from starting block (freestyle, breaststroke, butterfly) or in water (backstroke).
False start: leaving before starting signal – one false start per race allowed (some rules: immediate DQ).

numberOfPlayers.individual: 1 swimmer per lane (up to 8‑10 lanes in finals)
numberOfPlayers.relay: 4 swimmers per team (each swims one leg)

breaksIntervals.betweenRacesIndividual: Same swimmer may have 20‑60 min between events
breaksIntervals.relayChangeover: Next swimmer may start when previous touches wall (0.0 sec reaction time allowed)

substitutesAndRoles.substitution:
In relays, a substitute can replace a swimmer before the race (not during). In individual events, no substitutes.
Sprinter (50‑100m) – explosive power, start and turn specialist Distance swimmer (400‑1500m) – endurance, pacing, efficiency IM specialist – versatile in all four strokes

rulesRegulations.timeBased: Fastest time wins; ties broken by swim‑off (if needed) or shared medal
rulesRegulations.howToWin: First swimmer to touch the wall after completing the prescribed distance (all strokes legal). In timed finals, fastest time wins.

dqCauses.cause: False start (after one warning in some rules – FINA: immediate DQ if start before signal)
Stroke violation (e.g., breaststroke kick in butterfly, non‑simultaneous arm pull in breaststroke)
Turn violation (e.g., not touching wall with two hands in breaststroke/butterfly)
Backstroke: turning onto stomach before final touch
Relay: early takeoff (swimmer leaves before teammate touches wall)

fairPlay.rule: Acknowledge opponents after race (handshake or nod)
No splashing intentionally to distract
Respect lane etiquette – do not draft illegally (drafting allowed, but not crossing lane)

judgementCriteria.officials: Referee, starter, stroke judges (2‑4 per side), turn judges, finish judges
judgementCriteria.electronicTiming: Touchpads at both ends (swimmer stops clock by pressing pad), backup manual times
judgementCriteria.videoReview: Used for relay takeoff violations and touchpad disputes (FINA events)

commonInjuries.injury: Swimmer's shoulder (rotator cuff tendinitis, impingement) – most common
Swimmer's knee (breaststroker's knee – medial collateral ligament strain)
Lower back pain (hyperextension from butterfly or poor technique)
Swimmer's ear (otitis externa – infection from water)

preventionMethods.method: Proper stroke technique – avoid crossing midline in freestyle (reduces shoulder impingement)
Strengthen rotator cuff (external rotation with bands, scapular stability)
Dry‑land warm‑up including shoulder circles and dynamic stretching
Use earplugs and dry ears after swimming (alcohol/vinegar drops to prevent infection)
Gradual volume increase (no more than 10% per week) to avoid overuse
Hydrate before and during (cramps often due to dehydration)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, productive cough) – risk of drowning or myocarditis
Open wounds or skin infections (risk of infection for self and others, pool contamination)
Uncontrolled asthma (cold water may trigger bronchospasm) – use rescue inhaler if cleared
Contagious ear infection (can spread in water)
Recent seizure disorder not controlled (risk of drowning)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have heart condition, epilepsy, or severe allergies to chlorine

commonMistakes.mistake: Holding breath instead of rhythmic breathing (causes CO2 buildup, fatigue)
Crossing midline with hand entry (freestyle – leads to shoulder injury, zigzag)
Lifting head too high to breathe (drops hips, increases drag)
Incomplete kick (breaststroke: narrow frog kick, not wide)
Rushing turns (losing momentum, illegal touch)
Over‑gliding (breaststroke – loss of speed)
Poor start reaction (delay off blocks)

hydrationImportance.beforeRace: Drink 400‑600 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (even though in water, you sweat)
hydrationImportance.duringRace: Not possible during short races; between events drink 150‑200 ml water or sports drink
hydrationImportance.afterRace: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink if heavy training (1+ hour)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dark urine, dry mouth, dizziness – stop and hydrate, inform coach

nutritionTips.preRace: 2‑3 hours before: pasta, rice, banana, oatmeal (carbs) – avoid heavy fat/protein (slows digestion)
nutritionTips.preRaceSnack: 30 min before: energy gel, banana, or rice cake (easily digestible)
nutritionTips.betweenEvents: Small snacks: dried fruit, granola bar, or sports drink (maintain blood sugar)
nutritionTips.postRace: Within 30 min: protein shake, chocolate milk, or eggs + toast (muscle repair) + carbs to replenish glycogen
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (red meat, spinach) for oxygen transport; omega‑3 (fish) for joint health; adequate calories for high training volume

physicalBurnout.limit: High volume training (>5km/day) max 5 days per week; lower volume (3km) can be 6 days
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 1 full rest day per week; active recovery (light swimming, yoga)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Dry‑land strength (2‑3x/week), cycling, running (low impact cross training)

mentalBurnout.signs: Dreading practice, plateauing performance, irritability, lack of motivation
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break (active recovery), set new goals (e.g., technique focus instead of time), vary workouts (drills, fins, paddles)

swimsuit.competitive: Jammers (men) or one‑piece knee‑skin or bodysuit (women) – technical fabric (polyester, PBT)

goggles.features: Anti‑fog, UV protection, comfortable seal, adjustable strap

equipment.swimCap: Silicone or latex – reduces drag, keeps hair out, protects goggles
equipment.pullBuoy: Foam float between legs to isolate arms
equipment.fins: Short fins (for technique and speed work)

accessories.item: Mesh equipment bag, water bottle, towel, pool buoy (lane rope training)

protective.item: Earplugs (prevents swimmer's ear – especially for cold water or prone individuals)
Nose clip (optional – prevents water inhalation, especially for backstroke/butterfly)
Bright swim cap (for open water, not necessary for pool but helpful for visibility)
Lifejacket (non‑swimmers – not for competitive swimming)

safetyGear.firstAid: Antiseptic ear drops (for ear infections), bandages (for minor cuts – waterproof), ice packs, rescue tube (lifeguard)

coordination.desc: Breathing timing with arm recovery (freestyle: breathe to side when arm exits water)
coordination.drill: Side‑kick with one arm extended, breathe every 3 strokes – 4 x 25m

speedAgility.desc: Quick turnover (stroke rate), explosive starts, fast turns
speedAgility.drill: 10 x 25m sprint with 30 sec rest; turn drills (crossover or flip turns) 20 reps

focusReaction.desc: React to starting signal (0.1‑0.3 sec reaction time)
focusReaction.drill: Start practice from blocks with sound cue – 10 reps

strategy.sprint: All‑out from start, maintain high stroke rate, perfect turn

xFactors.distance:
Pacing (negative split: second half faster than first), drafting (swim behind opponent to reduce drag) Know stroke transition – back to breast (turn on wall), breast to free (open turn)
Adjust pace based on opponents; in finals, focus on own race plan, not others When to kick harder (last 15m), when to breathe less (finish)

dailyPlan.day: Warm‑up 400m + Freestyle technique (drills: fist, catch‑up) 800m + Kick set (8x50m with board) + Cool‑down 200m
IM day: 200m drill set (each stroke) + 400m IM (broken into 50s) + Pull set (6x100m with buoy) + Cool‑down
Sprint day: warm‑up, starts & turns (20 min), 10x50m max effort (1 min rest), easy swim 300m
Endurance: warm‑up, 3x400m freestyle (descending pace), 200m kick, cool‑down
Race pace: warm‑up, 8x100m (on 1:30 base), 4x50m drills, cool‑down
Long swim (3‑5km continuous) + technique focus (video analysis)
Active recovery (light swimming 1000m, stretching, or complete rest)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Face in water, exhale bubbles, turn head to side – 10 reps
Hold board, flutter kick 4x25m (focus on straight legs, small kicks)
One arm waits for other to catch before starting pull – 4x25m
On back, practice frog kick (heels to butt, circle out, snap together) – 10 reps

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master breathing, flutter kick, basic freestyle and backstroke – 3x per week (1‑1.5km per session)
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add breaststroke and butterfly (25m non‑stop), turns (open and flip), starts – 4x per week (2km)
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: IM training, pacing, race strategy, interval sets – 5x per week (3‑4km) + dry‑land strength 2x
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine technique with video analysis, compete in local meets, maintain 4‑5 sessions/week

focusingTechniques.technique: Before race, inhale deep, exhale slowly – repeat 5 times on the block
Close eyes, imagine perfect start, turn, finish, and touchpad – 3 min before race
Same warm‑up, same cap and goggles adjustment, same lane entry pattern

pressureHandling.tip: If nervous before start, focus on your own lane and the water – ignore competitors
During race, break into segments (first 25m, turn, second 25m) – don't think of total distance
Use self‑talk: “Strong pull, fast kick, smooth breath”
After bad race, review video with coach, identify one thing to improve, move on

sportsmanship.rule: Shake hands with lane neighbours after race (if within reach)
Congratulate winner, even if you lost
No splashing or taunting

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local swim club (USA Swimming, Swim England, or municipal pool with masters or age group program)
Join beginner lessons if needed (adult learn‑to‑swim classes)
Purchase basic equipment: goggles, cap, practice suit, kickboard (approx $50‑100)
Join a competitive or recreational masters team (18+ or age group for youth)
Enter novice meets (e.g., club intrasquad, local open meet)
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Club fees: $30‑100/month; USA Swimming registration: $20‑50/year; equipment: $50‑150

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Discipline – shows up on time, completes full sets
Patience – works on technique even when tired
Resilience – recovers from disqualification or bad race
Coachability – accepts feedback and makes changes
Racing instinct – knows when to push and when to pace

sprint.strategy: Explosive block start (track start or grab start), streamline underwater dolphin kicks (max 15m), breakout powerful
50m: breath 0‑2 times total; 100m: breathe every 2‑4 strokes

distance.strategy: Swim second 50% of race faster than first (e.g., 2:00/2:00 for 400m → 1:58/1:58)
Swim directly behind or beside opponent to reduce drag (legal in pool)
Maintain constant split times, avoid sprinting early

medley.strategy: Stay relaxed, don't waste energy; backstroke leg build pace; breaststroke leg maintain efficiency; freestyle leg all out

relay.strategy: Second/third/fourth swimmers can start when teammate touches wall (anticipate, not jump early)
Fastest swimmer last (anchor), second fastest first (lead)

specialSituations.situation: Leading by body length – maintain technique, don't celebrate early
Trailing – increase kick frequency, reduce breathing, focus on turn speed

terminologies.term: Freestyle – front crawl (unrestricted, most common)
Backstroke – on back, alternating arm pull, flutter kick
Breaststroke – frog kick, simultaneous arm pull, glide
Butterfly – dolphin kick, simultaneous arm recovery over water
Individual Medley (IM) – all four strokes in order (fly, back, breast, free)
Medley relay – backstroke, breaststroke, butterfly, freestyle (order different from IM)
False start – leaving block before starting signal (DQ in FINA)
DQ – disqualification (rule violation)
Touchpad – electronic pad at wall that stops timing clock
Split – intermediate time (e.g., 50m split in 100m race)
Negative split – second half faster than first
Streamline – arms extended overhead, hands clasped, body tight after start/turn
Underwater dolphin kick – undulating body kick, allowed up to 15m after start/turn
Flip turn – somersault turn (freestyle, backstroke)
Open turn – touch wall with hand, then turn (breaststroke, butterfly)
Breakout – first stroke after surfacing from start/turn
Drafting – swimming in another swimmer's wake to reduce drag
Anchor – final swimmer in relay
Lead‑off – first swimmer in relay
Long course (LC) – 50m pool (Olympic)
Short course (SC) – 25m pool (world championships)
FINA – International Swimming Federation (now World Aquatics)
PAC – pacing (timed intervals)
Kick set – swimming with only legs (using kickboard)
Pull set – swimming with only arms (using pull buoy)
Descend – swim each repeat faster than previous
Build – gradually increase speed within a single swim

dimensions.en: 2 meters from centre (on each side) 7 meters from centre (warning area before end of piste) End of piste – fencer cannot retreat beyond

layout.lines: Centre line, en garde lines, warning lines, rear limit lines

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Metal (grounded) for electrical scoring, rubber or conductive fabric
courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor sports hall with non‑slip flooring, proper lighting, scoring apparatus

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular fitness (high‑intensity intervals)
Strengthens legs (lunges, footwork), core, and upper body (arm extension)
Burns 400‑600 calories per hour
Enhances flexibility, balance, and coordination
Develops explosive power and reaction time

mental.benefit: Sharpens tactical thinking and anticipation
Improves focus and concentration under pressure
Builds discipline and quick decision‑making
Reduces stress through controlled aggression

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: leg swings, arm circles, torso twists, walking lunges, high knees (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), footwork drills (advance/retreat, lunges without blade, 5 min), shadow fencing with blade (3 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Slow walking (3 min), static stretches: hamstring, quad, hip flexor, shoulder, wrist, forearm (30 sec each)

basicRules.rule: Two fencers compete on a piste (strip).
Objective: score touches (points) by hitting opponent with tip or side of blade (depending on weapon).
Three weapons: Foil (target torso only, right of way), Épée (target entire body, no right of way), Sabre (target above waist, right of way).
Bouts are electric – hits register via scoring box.
Fencers must stay within lateral boundaries; going off side or back line results in penalty.

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: 2 fencers (singles). Team event: 3 fencers per team (relay format).

matchTimings.directElimination: 3 periods of 3 minutes each (with 1 min break between periods) – total 9 min fencing time
matchTimings.poolRound: Bout to 5 touches (no time limit, but usually under 10 min) Each fencer fences 5 touches or 3 minutes (alternating)

breaksIntervals.medicalTimeout: Up to 10 minutes for injury (once per bout)
breaksIntervals.equipmentBreak: 2 minutes for equipment repair (e.g., body cord change)

formats.shortMatch: Pool round to 5 touches (no time limit, first to 5 wins)
formats.longMatch: Direct elimination: 3 periods of 3 min, first to 15 touches (or highest score at end)

substitutesAndRoles.substitution:
In team events, substitute fencer can replace an injured fencer (once per team). No substitutions in individual bouts.
Attacking fencer – initiates, uses speed and distance Counter‑attacking fencer – waits for opponent's attack, then ripostes Defensive fencer – parries and controls tempo

scoringSystem.foil: Touch to valid target (torso) = 1 point. Right of way determines who gets point if both hit. Touch anywhere (including mask, foot) = 1 point. If both hit within 40 ms, both get point (double touch). Touch to target above waist (mask, torso, arms) = 1 point. Right of way applies.

howToWin.directElimination: First to 15 touches, or higher score after 3 periods of 3 minutes

rulesRegulations.pool:
First to 5 touches wins the bout
Warning (no direct point loss) Opponent gets 1 point (e.g., covering target, jostling, leaving piste without permission) Disqualification (e.g., dangerous play, doping, violence)
Crossing feet (flèche) incorrectly in foil/sabre Turning back to opponent (avoiding touch) Using non‑fencing hand to parry (red card) Delaying bout (yellow then red)
Acknowledge opponent's valid touches; no arguing with referee Salute opponent and referee before and after bout (required) No celebrating excessively after touch
Makes calls on right of way (foil/sabre) and validity of touches Used in international competitions (FIE) for disputed touches Electrically registers touches and timing (light red/green for valid, white for off‑target in foil)

commonInjuries.injury: Ankle sprain (from lunges or lateral movement)
Knee pain (patellar tendinitis from repetitive lunges)
Bruises (contusions from hits, especially in sabre)

preventionMethods.method: Proper lunge technique – front knee aligned over ankle, back leg straight
Strengthen quads, glutes, and calves (squats, lunges, calf raises)
Wear proper fencing shoes (low profile, good grip)
Use protective gear (mask, jacket, plastron, glove) without exception
Warm‑up thoroughly including dynamic stretching
Do wrist and forearm strengthening (rice bucket, theraband)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, productive cough) – exertion may worsen
Uncontrolled hypertension (risk of sudden pressure spike)
Recent concussion (within 2 weeks) – balance and reaction impaired
Severe arthritis in knees or hips (lunge aggravates)
Epilepsy not well controlled (bright scoring lights may trigger)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of fainting, heart arrhythmia, or severe asthma

commonMistakes.mistake: Lunging with straight back leg (reduces reach and strains hamstring)
Crossing feet instead of advancing (loss of balance and illegal in foil/sabre)
Dropping non‑weapon hand (exposes target, may be penalized)
Watching opponent's blade instead of their shoulder/feet
Over‑committing attack without distance control
Not recovering after lunge (slow return to en garde)
Holding blade too tightly (reduces point control)

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 400‑600 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 100‑150 ml between periods (1 min break) – use small bottle
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost with water or electrolyte drink
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Excessive caffeine (jitters, dehydration), sugary sports drinks during short bouts
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dry mouth, reduced concentration – stop and hydrate, inform referee if needed

nutritionTips.preGame: 2‑3 hours before: pasta, rice, banana, oatmeal (carbs for explosive energy)
nutritionTips.preGameSnack: 30 min before: apple, energy gel, or handful of raisins
nutritionTips.duringGame: Water only (isotonic if long tournament day)
nutritionTips.postGame: Within 30 min: chocolate milk, protein shake, or chicken + rice for recovery
nutritionTips.daily: Lean protein (chicken, fish) for muscle repair; iron (spinach, red meat) for endurance; calcium (dairy) for bone density

physicalBurnout.limit: Intensive fencing (bouts + drills) max 4‑5 times per week
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days or active recovery (light swimming, yoga)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, cycling, Pilates (strengthens core, low impact)

mentalBurnout.signs: Loss of motivation, frustration after losses, avoiding practice
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, cross‑train, set fun goals (e.g., learn a new parry)

weapon.foil: Light (500g), flexible blade, tip with spring, electric. Target: torso only
weapon.epee: Heavier (770g), stiffer blade, larger guard. Target: entire body
weapon.sabre: Light (500g), blade with cutting edge, guard wraps hand. Target: above waist

mask.material: Mesh with bib (conductive for electric). FIE certified for competition

jacket.material: White cotton/nylon with conductive lame for foil/sabre (target area)

glove.material: Leather with cuff (extends over jacket sleeve)
glove.features: Palm grip, conductive cuff for sabre

shoes.features: Flat sole, reinforced heel, lateral support, thin (for feel of piste)

equipment.bodyCord: Wire connecting weapon to scoring box (two clips)

accessories.item: Weapon bag, spare blades, tip screws, spring kit, test weight (for épée/foil)

protective.item: Mask (must be in good condition – no dents, mesh intact)
Plastron (underarm protector) – mandatory, reduces bruising and puncture risk
Chest protector (women mandatory, men optional) – hard plastic
Gorget (neck protector) – optional but recommended for sabre
Fencing jacket (350N minimum, 800N for serious competition)
Glove with reinforced cuff

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, wound dressing (for minor cuts), instant cold packs

coordination.desc: Hand‑eye coordination to hit small target (foil: torso; sabre: wrist/head)
coordination.drill: Target practice on dummy – 20 extensions, 20 lunges with point control

speedAgility.desc: Quick advance/retreat, explosive lunge, fast recovery
speedAgility.drill: Footwork ladder (one step, double step, cross steps) – 5 min daily; lunge sprints (5 lunges in 10 sec)

focusReaction.desc: React to opponent's attack within 0.2‑0.3 seconds; read feints
focusReaction.drill: Partner calls "attack" – perform parry‑riposte; reaction ball drills

strategy.offensive: Flèche (running attack), feint disengage, beat attack
strategy.defensive: Parry‑riposte (circle parry, lateral parry), counter‑attack (stop hit)

xFactors.gameSense: Distance management – know when you're within lunge distance
xFactors.decisionMaking: Choose between direct attack, feint, or invitation based on opponent's habits

dailyPlan.day: Footwork (20 min) + Point control (20 min on target) + Lunge drills (15 min)
Blade work (parry combinations: 4‑6, 2‑4) (20 min) + Advance‑lunge (15 min) + Bout practice (20 min)
Strength training (squats, lunges, core, wrist curls) – 45 min
Feint drills (disengage, coulé) (20 min) + Distance exercises (15 min) + 2 bouts (15 touches each)
Tactical bouting (focus on specific parry) (30 min) + Video analysis (20 min)
Conditioning (sprints, suicide runs) + Open bouting (1 hour)
Active recovery (light footwork, stretching, yoga)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Hold proper en garde for 30 sec – repeat 5 times (builds muscle memory)
10 advances, 10 retreats without breaking stance
Advance then lunge – 10 reps, focus on back leg straightening
Without moving feet, extend arm to hit target (dummy or partner's glove) – 20 reps

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master en garde, basic footwork (advance, retreat, lunge), and extension – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add parry‑4 and parry‑6 (foil/epee) or parry‑3 and parry‑5 (sabre), basic feints – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce distance games, counter‑attacks, and tactical bouting – 4x per week + 1 tournament simulation
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine parry‑riposte combinations, video analysis, competition practice – maintain 3‑4 sessions/week

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 4 sec, exhale 6 sec – before "en garde" command
Imagine executing perfect lunge and hitting target – 2 min before competition
Same warm‑up sequence every time (e.g., 10 lunges, 5 parries, salute mirror)

pressureHandling.tip: When trailing, break bout into "one touch at a time" – don't think of final score
Use positive self‑talk: "I have the distance, I can hit"
After being hit, take an extra second before assuming en garde – breathe, reset
In close score (14‑14), rely on your strongest action (e.g., direct attack)

sportsmanship.rule: Salute opponent and referee before and after bout (mandatory, no salute = yellow card)
Acknowledge opponent's good touches (nod or tap mask)
Do not remove mask until referee says "fence" is over
After loss, congratulate opponent, shake hand (even if upset)

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local fencing club (US Fencing Association or national federation website)
Join a beginner class (usually 4‑8 weeks) – club provides equipment initially
Purchase basic equipment after 2‑3 months (mask, jacket, glove, foil) – budget $300‑500
Get an electric weapon and lame when ready for competition (additional $200‑300)
Join local tournaments (unsanctioned novice events first)
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Monthly club fees: $50‑150; beginner gear set: $150‑300; competition license (USFA): $75/year

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Discipline – practices footwork daily even without blade
Patience – waits for right moment, doesn't rush
Analytical – reviews bouts to find patterns
Adaptability – changes tactics when opponent adjusts
Calm under pressure – executes technique in close scores

offensive.strategy: Simple extension or lunge when opponent is within distance
Fake to one line, disengage around parry to hit another
Beat opponent's blade (with forte) then extend
Running attack (épée and foil) – cross feet, explosive forward thrust

defensive.strategy: Deflect opponent's blade (parry) then immediate hit (riposte)
Hit opponent as they begin attack, before their hit lands (stop hit)
Retreat just out of range, then lunge as opponent's attack falls short

weaponSpecific.foil: Control right of way – always attack or riposte; avoid simultaneous actions
weaponSpecific.epee: Use patience, hand hits, and counter‑time; double touches are acceptable
weaponSpecific.sabre: Use cut attacks, fast footwork, and distance; avoid letting opponent parry

specialSituations.situation: Leading near end – control distance, avoid risky attacks, use retreats
Trailing – take risks, use flèche, change tempo frequently

terminologies.term: En garde – starting position and command to begin
Allez – French for "fence" (command to start)
Halte – stop (referee calls to end action)
Lunge – forward thrust with front leg bent, back leg straight
Recovery – return from lunge to en garde
Advance – forward step without crossing feet
Retreat – backward step
Flèche – running attack (crossing feet, illegal in sabre)
Parry – deflection of opponent's blade (numbered: 1‑8)
Riposte – offensive action after a parry
Counter‑riposte – riposte after opponent's riposte
Feint – false attack to draw parry
Disengage – pass blade around opponent's parry
Beat – sharp tap on opponent's blade to open line
Press – gradual pressure on opponent's blade
Stop hit – counter‑attack that lands before opponent's attack
Remise – immediate second attack after first misses (without recovery)
Right of way – priority rule in foil and sabre (attacker gets point if both hit)
Target area – valid scoring surface (varies by weapon)
Lame – conductive jacket (foil/sabre)
Body cord – wire connecting weapon to scoring box
Piste – fencing strip (14m x 1.5‑2m)
Red card – penalty (opponent gets 1 point)
Black card – disqualification
Pool round – initial round where fencers fence everyone in group to 5 touches
Direct elimination – knockout bracket after pools (to 15 touches)
FIE – International Fencing Federation (Fédération Internationale d'Escrime)

dimensions.netHeight: 15.25 cm (6 in) at centre, 15.25 cm at posts

layout.lines: Baseline (end line), sideline (side line), centre line (for doubles)
layout.zones: Forehand side, backhand side; left/right halves for doubles service

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Wood (plywood) with low‑friction coating, or synthetic composite (ITTF approved)
courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor hall with non‑glare lighting, no air drafts, flooring with good grip (wood or rubber)

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (continuous rallies, quick movements)
Enhances hand‑eye coordination and reflexes
Strengthens legs (lunges, side shuffles), core, and forearm (wrist snap)
Burns 250‑400 calories per hour (moderate to intense play)
Low‑impact sport – easy on joints compared to running or tennis

mental.benefit: Sharpens focus, concentration, and anticipation
Reduces stress through rhythmic play
Improves strategic thinking (spin, placement, tactics)

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: wrist circles, arm circles, trunk twists, leg swings, ankle rotations (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (3 min), shadow strokes (forehand/backhand, 2 min), rally without spin (2 min), footwork drills (side shuffles, 2 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Static stretches: wrist flexor/extensor, shoulder stretch, hamstring, quad (30 sec each); deep breathing (2 min)

basicRules.rule: Game played between 2 (singles) or 4 (doubles) players.
Ball must bounce once on own side, then once on opponent's side (must clear net).
Serve must be tossed vertically at least 16 cm, hit so it bounces on server's side, then receiver's side.
In singles, any part of table is valid target; in doubles, serve must go from right half to opponent's right half.
Points scored on every rally (rally point system).

numberOfPlayers.doubles: 2 vs 2 (alternating shots – each player must alternate hitting)

rulesRegulations.matchTimings: Varies: best of 5 games (each game to 11 points) typically lasts 15‑30 minutes. Professional matches best of 7 games.

breaksIntervals.timeout: 1 minute timeout per player (or pair) per match (allowed once)
breaksIntervals.betweenGames: Up to 90 seconds between games (usually 60 sec)
breaksIntervals.injuryTimeout: Up to 5 minutes for medical evaluation (once per match)

substitutesAndRoles.substitution:
No substitutions in singles. In doubles, partners cannot be replaced during match.
Aggressive player – attacks early, uses loops and smashes Defensive player – uses chops, pushes, and blocks All‑rounder – balances attack and defence

rulesRegulations.scoringSystem: Each game to 11 points, win by 2. If deuce (10‑10), play until one leads by 2. Match win by winning required number of games (e.g., best of 5 = win 3 games).
rulesRegulations.howToWin: First to 11 points with 2‑point lead in a game; win majority of games in match.

faults.fault: Serve not tossed 16 cm or hit before bounce on server's side – loss of point
Ball touches net on serve but lands in – let (re‑serve, no penalty). If net serve lands out – fault, point to receiver.
Server's free hand touches playing surface during serve – point to receiver
Non‑striking hand touches table during rally – point to opponent
In doubles, hitting out of order (player hits twice in a row or wrong partner hits) – point to opponent
Ball struck before bouncing on own side (volley) – point to opponent

foulsAndPenalties.penaltyForMisconduct: Yellow card (warning), red card (point to opponent), or disqualification (for serious unsportsmanlike conduct, e.g., racket throwing)

fairPlay.rule: Acknowledge opponent's good shots (tap table or nod)
No distracting noises or movements during opponent's serve
If ball touches edge (in), raise hand to indicate it was in

judgementCriteria.umpire: Makes calls on serves, lets, and points. Assistant umpire for net cords and edge balls.
judgementCriteria.videoReview: Used in ITTF events for disputed edge balls or serve legality.

commonInjuries.injury: Wrist strain (repetitive wrist snap for spin)

preventionMethods.method: Proper grip (shakehand or penhold) – not too tight, neutral wrist
Warm‑up with easy forehand/backhand drives (no spin) for 5 minutes
Strengthen wrist extensors/flexors (wrist curls with light dumbbell, rice bucket)
Use supportive shoes with lateral stability (court shoes, not running shoes)
Take breaks between intense rallies (30 sec every 10 minutes)
Maintain correct footwork – avoid over‑reaching with straight leg

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, cough) – exertion may worsen
Uncontrolled hypertension (rapid movements may spike BP)
Recent wrist or elbow fracture (not healed)
Severe arthritis in knees or hips (lunging painful)
Active vertigo or balance disorders (quick direction changes may trigger)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of fainting, heart arrhythmia, or severe asthma

commonMistakes.mistake: Gripping racket too tightly – reduces wrist snap and causes elbow pain
Standing too close to table (no time to react to fast shots)
Incorrect serve toss (not vertical, not high enough) – fault
Using only arm, not body rotation – reduces power, strains shoulder
Not returning to ready position after each shot (centre of table)
Watching the ball instead of opponent's racket angle (to read spin)
Flat hitting everything – no spin, easy for opponent to attack

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 400‑600 ml water 2 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 100‑150 ml during breaks (between games or timeout) – keep bottle near table
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost; water or electrolyte drink if heavy sweating
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Excessive caffeine (jitters, dehydration), sugary sodas (energy crash)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dry mouth, dizziness, reduced reaction time – stop and hydrate

nutritionTips.preMatch: 2‑3 hours before: banana, oatmeal, whole grain toast (carbs) + small yogurt (protein)
nutritionTips.preMatchSnack: 30 min before: apple, handful of raisins, or energy gel
nutritionTips.duringMatch: Water only (short matches). For long tournaments, small snacks between matches (dried fruit, nuts)
nutritionTips.postMatch: Within 30 min: protein shake, chocolate milk, or eggs + toast (muscle recovery)
nutritionTips.daily: Calcium (dairy) for bone density; omega‑3 (fish) for joint health; adequate protein for muscle repair

physicalBurnout.limit: Intense training (drills + matches) max 5 days per week
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days or active recovery (light jogging, stretching)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, cycling, yoga (low impact, core strength)

mentalBurnout.signs: Loss of motivation, frustration, avoiding practice matches
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, play for fun without scoring, set new technique goals

racket.blade: Wood (5‑7 plies) or carbon fibre (stiff). Size: 15‑16 cm long, 15 cm wide.
racket.rubber: Inverted (smooth, spinny) or pimpled (short, long, or medium pips). Thickness: 1.8‑2.2 mm (competitive).

ball.material: Plastic (non‑celluloid) – matte or seamless, ITTF approved

equipment.table: Wood or synthetic, dark green or blue (ITTF approved), with 2.5 cm white lines
equipment.net: Steel wire with fabric mesh, tension adjustable, 15.25 cm high

shoes.features: Gum rubber sole (non‑marking), lateral support, low profile

equipment.apparel: Breathable polyester T‑shirt, shorts, wristbands (sweat absorption)

accessories.item: Racket case, edge tape (protects blade), cleaning sponge, ball pick‑up net, water bottle

protective.item: Wrist brace (if prone to strain)
Elbow support (for tennis elbow prevention)
Knee pads (for players who lunge frequently on hard floor)
Protective eyewear (optional – for doubles to avoid stray ball to eye)

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, blister plasters

coordination.desc: Timing to contact ball with correct spin and placement
coordination.drill: Multi‑ball drill: coach feeds balls at varying speeds – 50 balls per session

speedAgility.desc: Quick side shuffles, explosive lunges, recovery to centre
speedAgility.drill: Footwork ladder (lateral, in‑out, cross steps) – 5 min; side shuffle with cone touches – 10 reps

focusReaction.desc: React to opponent's shot (0.2‑0.3 sec), read spin from racket angle
focusReaction.drill: Random ball machine (variable spin and speed) – 10 min

strategy.offensive: Loop (heavy topspin), drive (flat fast), smash (high ball)

xFactors.gameSense: Change placement (wide angles, middle), vary spin, serve long vs short
xFactors.decisionMaking: Choose attack when ball is high or short; defend when opponent loops

dailyPlan.day: Forehand drive + backhand drive (20 min each) + Footwork (15 min) + Serve practice (15 min)
Loop vs block (20 min) + Push and attack (20 min) + Match play (3 games) (20 min)
Service receive drills (20 min) + Third ball attack (20 min) + Conditioning (sprints, lunges) (15 min)
Footwork patterns (side‑to‑side, in‑out) (20 min) + Multi‑ball (15 min) + Doubles positioning (15 min)
Spin variation drills (heavy loop vs light loop) (20 min) + Counter‑loop (20 min) + Match play (30 min)
Tournament simulation (best of 5 matches) + Video analysis (30 min)
Active recovery (light rally, stretching, or rest)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Bounce ball on racket alternately (forehand/backhand) – 50 consecutive
Hit ball against wall continuously – aim 20 hits
Toss and hit to opposite side – 20 serves, focus on height and bounce
Cross‑court forehand drive – 20 continuous shots

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master grip, basic drives (forehand/backhand), serve technique – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add push, block, footwork (side shuffle), serve receive – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce loop (topspin), chop (backspin), match tactics – 4‑5x per week + local tournament
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine spin variation, advanced footwork (Falkenberg drill), competition preparation – maintain 4x/week

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 3 sec, exhale 5 sec – before each serve
Close eyes, imagine perfect serve (toss, contact, placement) – 2 min
Same ball bounce count, same towel wipe, same deep breath

pressureHandling.tip: At deuce (10‑10), focus on your serve pattern (e.g., short backspin to middle)
If opponent gets hot streak, call timeout to break rhythm
Use self‑talk: “Watch the ball, react, don't overthink”
After losing point, bounce ball twice, reset, move on

sportsmanship.rule: If ball touches edge (in), raise hand to signal “good”
If opponent makes a great shot, tap table with racket (acknowledgment)
Shake hands after match, even if loss

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local table tennis club (USATT, TTE, or community centre)
Buy a beginner racket (pre‑assembled, $20‑40) and a few balls (3‑star quality)
Join group lessons or open play sessions (club provides tables)
After 3‑6 months, consider custom racket (blade + rubber, $50‑150)
Enter local novice tournaments (club or USATT sanctioned)

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Consistency – keeps ball on table, avoids unforced errors
Adaptability – adjusts to opponent's spin and pace
Patience – waits for high ball to attack, doesn't rush
Mental toughness – stays calm when trailing
Strategic thinking – plans 2‑3 shots ahead

serving.strategy: Catch opponent off guard, then attack their weak return

serveReceive.strategy: Return short to prevent opponent's third ball attack
Attack short serve with topspin to take initiative

rally.strategy: Use footwork to play forehand from backhand corner (more power)
Alternate wide forehand, wide backhand, middle
Heavy loop, then light loop or flat hit to disrupt timing

doubles.strategy: Use spin to force weak return for partner to attack
After serve, both players cover left/right halves

specialSituations.situation: Leading by 3+ points – play safe, keep ball on table, avoid risky shots
Trailing – serve long fast, attack immediately, change spin frequently

terminologies.term: Rally – sequence of shots from serve to point end
Let – serve that touches net but lands in (re‑serve, no point)
Fault – illegal serve (point to opponent)
Deuce – score 10‑10 (must win by 2)
Forehand – stroke on dominant side (right for right‑handers)
Backhand – stroke on non‑dominant side (across body)
Loop – heavy topspin shot (high arc, fast bounce)
Drive – flat, fast shot (less spin)
Smash – high ball hit downward with force
Chop – heavy backspin stroke (defensive)
Push – short backspin shot (often on short balls)
Block – short counter shot to opponent's loop (redirection)
Flick – wrist‑based attack on short ball (topspin)
Spin – rotation of ball (topspin, backspin, sidespin)
Third ball attack – attack after your serve + opponent's return
Pivot – step around to use forehand from backhand corner
Penhold grip – grip like holding a pen (Asian style)
Shakehand grip – grip like shaking hands (European style)
Pips – rubber nubs (short pips for less spin, long pips for reverse spin)
Inverted rubber – smooth, tacky rubber (most common for spin)
ITTF – International Table Tennis Federation
USATT – USA Table Tennis

layout.lines: Baseline, singles sideline, doubles sideline, short service line, centre line, net line
layout.serviceCourts: Left and right service courts for singles and doubles

courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor hall with non‑glare lighting, no air drafts, minimum 9m ceiling height

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (VO2max)
Strengthens legs, glutes, core, shoulders, and forearms
Burns 350–500 calories per hour
Enhances bone density and joint flexibility
Develops hand‑eye coordination and reflexes

mental.benefit: Reduces stress and anxiety through focus
Improves concentration and tactical thinking
Boosts self‑esteem when mastering skills

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles (30 sec), leg swings (30 sec), torso twists (30 sec), lunges (10 each leg)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), shadow footwork (3 min), skipping (2 min), low‑intensity clears (2 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Slow walking (3 min), static stretches: hamstring, quad, calf, shoulder stretch, wrist flexor stretch (30 sec each)

basicRules.rule: Match is won by winning 2 out of 3 games (or 1 game in short format).
Each game is to 21 points, win by 2, capped at 30 points.
Players change ends after each game, and in the third game when leading player reaches 11 points.
Shuttlecock must be hit only once before crossing the net.
If shuttle touches the net (except on serve) and lands in opponent's court, it's in play.

numberOfPlayers.doubles: 2 vs 2 (can be men's, women's, or mixed)

rulesRegulations.matchTimings: Typically 30–75 minutes depending on skill and format

breaksIntervals.at11Points: 60 seconds break when either player reaches 11 points in a game
breaksIntervals.technicalTimeouts: None in standard rules (except TV timeouts in some tournaments)

formats.shortMatch: Best of 1 game to 21 points (no cap) – used in some team events
formats.longMatch: Best of 3 games to 21 points, win by 2, max 30

rulesRegulations.substitutesAndRoles: No substitutions. In doubles, partners can switch positions but no replacement player

playersRoles.singles: One player covers entire court – requires high endurance and speed

doubles.role: Rotation system – partners rotate after each shot in advanced play

rulesRegulations.scoringSystem: Rally point system – point scored on every rally regardless of server. First to 21 wins, must lead by 2, max 30
rulesRegulations.howToWin: Win two games (or one in short format). The match ends immediately when a player/team reaches winning condition

faultDuringServe.penalty: Loss of rally, opponent gets point

conditions.cond: Shuttle above waist at point of strike
Player touches net with racket or body
Shuttle hits player's body or clothing
Double hit (two consecutive hits by same player)
Carrying or slinging shuttle on racket

fairPlay.rule: Accept line calls; if unsure, replay the rally (only if both agree)
No distracting opponents (shouting, waving racket)
Shake hands with opponent and umpire after match

judgementCriteria.umpire: Makes final decisions on faults and lets Monitors service legality (height, racket angle) Call shuttle in/out – if unsure, call "in" (shuttle is in) In professional tournaments, players can challenge line calls (limited per game)

preventionMethods.method: Wear proper badminton shoes with ankle support and non‑marking sole
Do dynamic warm‑up before every session
Strengthen wrist and shoulder with theraband exercises
Use correct grip size (too small/large causes elbow strain)
Land softly on forefoot, avoid heel strike
Take rest days – avoid playing with fatigue

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infections (fever, cough) – risk of myocarditis
Uncontrolled hypertension (high blood pressure) – sudden exertion risk
Severe asthma without pre‑exercise medication
Recent concussion (within 2 weeks)
Acute musculoskeletal injury (sprain, fracture)
Heart conditions not cleared by doctor (arrhythmias, valve disease)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Always consult doctor if you have chronic illness before starting badminton

commonMistakes.mistake: Gripping racket too tightly – reduces wrist snap and causes elbow pain
Standing flat‑footed; not staying on balls of feet
Serving too high (fault) or too low (into net)
Not returning to centre (base position) after each shot
Using only arm power for smash – should involve wrist and body rotation
Looking at the shuttle instead of opponent's position before hitting
Playing without footwork drills – leads to lunging awkwardly

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 400–600 ml water 2 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 150–200 ml every 15 minutes or at each break (60 sec at 11 points)
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost (weigh before/after). Drink 1.5 L for each kg lost, with electrolytes if heavy sweat
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Avoid sugary sodas, energy drinks (high caffeine), alcohol
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Dry mouth, dizziness, dark urine, reduced performance – stop and hydrate

nutritionTips.preGame: 2–3 hours before: complex carbs (oatmeal, banana, whole grain bread) + small protein (yogurt)
nutritionTips.preGameSnack: 30 min before: banana or energy gel (if long match)
nutritionTips.duringGame: Only water or isotonic drink (no solid food)
nutritionTips.postGame: Within 30 minutes: protein (whey, chicken, eggs) + carbs (rice, fruit) for recovery
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (leafy greens), calcium (dairy), omega‑3 (fish) for joint health

physicalBurnout.limit: Play intense matches max 3‑4 times per week
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 1‑2 full rest days per week
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, cycling, or yoga on off days to reduce impact

mentalBurnout.signs: Loss of motivation, irritability, dreading practice
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, set new small goals, play for fun without scoring

racket.types: Carbon fibre (light, stiff), aluminium (heavy, beginner), hybrid

shuttlecock.feather: Used in professional matches – faster but breaks easily
shuttlecock.nylon: Durable for beginners and recreational play – slower flight

shoes.features: Non‑marking gum sole, gel cushioning, lateral support, ankle stability

accessories.item: Grip (overgrip or replacement grip for tackiness)
Racket bag (to protect frame)
Feather shuttlecock conditioner (steamer)

protective.item: Ankle braces (if previous sprains)
Knee pads (for hard court diving)
Wrist splint (for chronic wrist pain)
Protective eyewear (recommended for doubles to avoid shuttle hit to eye)

safetyGear.firstAid: Always have ice pack, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray in kit

coordination.desc: Hand‑eye coordination to time shuttle contact precisely
coordination.drill: Wall rally – hit shuttle against wall continuously (aim for 50 hits)

speedAgility.desc: Quick direction changes, explosive lunges, split step
speedAgility.drill: Agility ladder (one foot in each square, lateral hops)

focusReaction.desc: React to opponent's smash within 0.2‑0.3 seconds
focusReaction.drill: Partner feeds random drops and smashes – defender reacts

strategy.offensive: Attack opponent's backhand, force weak lift then smash
strategy.defensive: Clear deep to push opponent to baseline, then net shot
strategy.doubles: Serve low to force lift, then front‑back attack formation

xFactors.gameSense: Anticipate shuttle trajectory based on opponent's body angle
xFactors.decisionMaking: Choose clear (safe), drop (deceptive), or smash (winner) based on position

dailyPlan.day: Footwork (20 min) + High serve practice (20 min) + Match play (30 min)
Wall rally (15 min) + Net shot drills (20 min) + Shadow badminton (15 min)
Rest or light cardio (jogging 20 min)
Smash & drop combination (25 min) + Defense drills (25 min)
Doubles positioning drills (20 min) + Service practice (15 min) + Game (30 min)
Match simulation (45 min) + Cool‑down stretching (10 min)
Active recovery (yoga or swimming)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Stand at service line, serve to back tramline – 20 reps per side
Partner feeds high shuttle – hit to opponent's baseline – 20 reps
Move to 6 corners (net left/right, mid left/right, back left/right) without shuttle – 5 min
Hit shuttle against wall continuously – goal 20 consecutive hits

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Focus on grip, service, and basic footwork – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add clear, drop, and smash technique – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Game strategy, doubles rotation, defense – 4x per week + one match
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Maintenance: drills 2x per week, matches 2x per week

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 4 sec, hold 2 sec, exhale 6 sec – repeat 10 times before match
Close eyes, imagine perfect serve or smash – 2 minutes
Watch the shuttle's seams during rally to block distractions

pressureHandling.tip: Use self‑talk: "Watch shuttle, not the score"
Between points, turn away, take deep breath, bounce on toes
Play point by point – forget previous mistakes immediately
In critical points (e.g., 20‑20), rely on your most consistent shot (clear or drop)

sportsmanship.rule: Acknowledge opponent's good shots (nod or tap racket)
Never argue with line judges; use proper challenge system if available
Help opponent if injured; don't celebrate excessively

howToJoinSport.step: Find local badminton club or community centre (search "badminton near me")
Rent racket initially or buy beginner combo (₹1000‑2000 / $15‑30)
Join beginner group lessons (usually 4‑8 sessions)
Play friendly matches with players of similar level
Enter local tournaments (D or E grade) after 6 months
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Monthly club fees: ₹500‑2000 ($6‑25) depending on city

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Consistent serve (low error rate)
Quick recovery to centre after every shot
Good shot selection – knows when to smash vs clear
Mental resilience – stays calm when trailing
Respects opponents and officials

singles.strategy: Push opponent deep to backhand corner, then net shot
Use same preparation as clear but soft touch
Steep downward drop to midcourt to catch opponent off balance

doubles.strategy: After lift, one player attacks net, other covers rear
When defending smashes, both stay in midcourt covering left/right
Aim for service line to force weak return

mixedDoubles.strategy: Female typically plays front (net kills), male covers rear (smash)

terminologies.term: Clear – high deep shot to opponent's baseline
Drop – soft shot that lands just over the net
Smash – hard downward shot hit steeply
Net shot – tight shot that barely clears the net and drops quickly
Drive – fast flat shot traveling horizontally
Fault – any violation of the rules (loss of rally)
Let – a rally that is replayed (e.g., shuttle stuck on net)
Deuce – score reaches 20‑20
Forecourt – area near the net (front third of court)
Midcourt – middle area (central third)
Rearcourt – back area (baseline third)
Split step – small jump just before opponent hits to prepare movement
Lift – high clear from net area to baseline
Kill – a winning net shot that cannot be returned
Rally – continuous sequence of shots
Service court – designated area where serve must land
Carry – illegally holding shuttle on racket (fault)
Double hit – hitting shuttle twice consecutively (fault)

layout.zones: Floor: diagonal and corner tumbling lines; Beam: centre line for acro series

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Spring floor with carpet, foam pits for landing practice, leather or synthetic covers for apparatus
courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor gymnasium with high ceiling (min 8m), padded walls, sprung subfloor

physical.benefit: Develops full‑body strength (upper, lower, core)
Improves flexibility, balance, and coordination
Enhances bone density (weight‑bearing impact)
Burns 300‑500 calories per hour (practice intensity dependent)
Builds proprioception (body awareness in space)

mental.benefit: Builds discipline, focus, and perseverance
Reduces anxiety through routine and repetition
Boosts confidence after mastering skills

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles, leg swings, trunk twists, wrist circles, ankle rotations (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), skipping (2 min), basic shapes (tuck, pike, straddle) on floor (3 min), handstand holds (2 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Static stretches: splits, bridge, shoulder stretch, hamstring, quad (30 sec each); deep breathing (2 min)

basicRules.rule: Gymnasts perform routines on each apparatus; scored by judges (D‑score difficulty + E‑score execution).
Men's Artistic (MAG): Floor, Pommel Horse, Rings, Vault, Parallel Bars, Horizontal Bar.
Women's Artistic (WAG): Vault, Uneven Bars, Balance Beam, Floor Exercise.
Routines have time limits (e.g., Floor: 70‑90 sec; Beam: 90 sec max).
Falls, steps on landing, and form breaks incur deductions.

numberOfPlayers.team: 5 gymnasts per team (4 count in finals)

breaksIntervals.betweenApparatus: 5‑10 minutes rotation time (gymnasts move as a group)
breaksIntervals.warmUpPeriod: 30‑60 seconds touch warm‑up on each apparatus before competition

formats.qualifications: Gymnasts compete on all apparatus; top teams/individuals advance to finals 3 up, 3 count (each apparatus 3 gymnasts perform, all scores count) Combined scores from all apparatus Top 8 gymnasts compete on single apparatus for medals

substitutesAndRoles.substitution: Team alternate (1‑2 per team) can replace injured gymnast before competition starts, not during

roles.role: Team captain – leads warm‑ups, communicates with judges

scoringSystem.eScore: Execution (starting 10.0, subtract deductions for form, landing, amplitude)
scoringSystem.neutralDeductions: Time violations, stepping out of bounds, extra support (0.1‑0.5)

rulesRegulations.howToWin: Highest total score on an apparatus (event final) or across all apparatus (all‑around). In team final, highest combined team score wins.

foulsAndPenalties.penalty: Neutral deduction: 0.3 for over time (floor/beam), 0.1 for stepping out of bounds, 0.5 for landing with hands on vault table
Fall: 1.0 deduction (0.5 in some codes) – from apparatus or to floor
Ineligible skill: no value, may invalidate connection bonus

fairPlay.rule: Respect judges' decisions; no arguing
Salute judges before and after routine (mandatory)
Wait for green light/signal before starting routine

judgementCriteria.ePanel: 5‑6 judges evaluate execution (highest/lowest dropped, average of middle 3)

commonInjuries.injury: Wrist sprain (from weight‑bearing on floor, vault, beam)
Knee pain (patellar tendinitis, ACL risk from landings)

preventionMethods.method: Proper landing technique (knees bent, toes to hips alignment)
Strength training: wrists (rice bucket), core (planks, leg lifts), legs (squats)
Use mats, foam pits, and spotting for new skills
Wrist supports (wrist bands/guards) during floor and vault
Adequate rest between high‑impact days (no floor/vault two days in a row)
Progressive skill development (master basics before advanced)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever) – risk of fainting
Uncontrolled asthma – exertion may trigger attack
Recent concussion (within 2‑4 weeks) – balance and spatial awareness critical
Severe osteoporosis (risk of fracture from impact)
Active joint inflammation (arthritis flare‑up)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have a heart condition, epilepsy, or chronic back pain before starting gymnastics

commonMistakes.mistake: Locking knees on landings (increases injury risk, form deduction)
Arching back in handstand or on beam (form break)
Looking down instead of ahead (on beam, floor tumbling)
Rushing skills without proper body tension
Insufficient run on vault (lack of power)
Gripping bars too tightly (reduces swing fluidity)
Skipping warm‑up – leading to pulls and strains

hydrationImportance.beforePractice: Drink 400‑600 ml water 2 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before
hydrationImportance.afterPractice: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink after intense sessions
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dry lips, reduced performance – stop and inform coach

nutritionTips.prePractice: 2‑3 hours before: oatmeal, banana, whole grain toast (carbs) + yogurt (protein)
nutritionTips.prePracticeSnack: 30 min before: apple, handful of raisins, or rice cake
nutritionTips.duringPractice: Water only; long sessions (>3h) may need sports drink
nutritionTips.postPractice: Within 30 min: protein shake, chocolate milk, or eggs + toast (repair muscle)
nutritionTips.daily: Calcium (milk, cheese) for bone density; iron (lean meat, spinach) for energy; vitamin D (sunlight or supplement)

physicalBurnout.limit: High‑intensity training max 5 days per week (elite); recreational 3‑4 days
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 1‑2 full rest days; active recovery (swimming, light stretching)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Yoga, Pilates, swimming (low impact, core strength)

mentalBurnout.signs: Loss of motivation, fear of skills, irritability, dreading practice
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, set small achievable goals, play other sports for fun

apparatus.unevenBars: Fibreglass rails with wood coating, adjustable cables
apparatus.pommelHorse: Leather handles, metal body with springs
apparatus.rings: Wood or composite rings, steel cables
apparatus.parallelBars: Wood rails on adjustable metal uprights

grips.rings: Leather grips without dowel or bare hands

equipment.shoes: Leather or canvas slippers (optional, floor/beam) or barefoot

apparel.women: Leotard (long sleeve or sleeveless) – compulsory in competition

accessories.item: Wrist bands, ankle braces, heel cups (for landing impact), tape
Gymnastics bag, water bottle, notebook for routine recording

protective.item: Wrist supports (rigid or neoprene) – for floor and vault
Ankle braces (if previous sprains) – for landings
Heel cups (gel) – reduce impact on tumbling
Grips with dowel (prevents bar burns and tears)
Padded shorts (for beam/floor practice) – reduce hip bruises
Spotting belt (for learning release moves on bars)

safetyGear.firstAid: Tape, elastic bandage, ice packs, antiseptic spray, blister plasters, instant cold packs

coordination.desc: Body awareness in air (tumbling, releases) and on apparatus
coordination.drill: Floor: forward/backward rolls, cartwheels, round‑offs (10 reps each)

speedAgility.desc: Run speed for vault, quick footwork on beam/floor
speedAgility.drill: Sprints (20m) – 5 reps; agility ladder drills (lateral, in‑out)

focusReaction.desc: React quickly to adjust landing or save balance
focusReaction.drill: Coach calls “stick” – freeze landing position; balance beam reaction drills

strategy.difficulty: Select high‑value skills within gymnast's capability (avoid over‑loading)

xFactors.execution:
Focus on straight legs, pointed toes, amplitude (height) to maximise E‑score Plan connections (e.g., acro series on beam) for bonus
Know when to play safe (e.g., dismount without fall) vs risk difficult skill Choose landing adjustments (step vs stick) based on position

dailyPlan.day: Flexibility (splits, bridges) 15 min + Floor tumbling (round‑off, back handspring) 30 min + Vault drills (15 min)
Uneven bars (glide kips, cast handstands) 30 min + Balance beam (walk series, jumps) 30 min + Conditioning (core, legs) 20 min
Rest or active recovery (light stretching, swimming)
Floor (acro series, dance elements) 30 min + Vault (full runs) 20 min + Flexibility (30 min)
Beam (full routine parts) 30 min + Bars (release moves or dismounts) 30 min + Strength (pull‑ups, leg lifts) 20 min
Full routine practice (all apparatus) + choreography (1.5 hours)
Complete rest or light stretching only

drillsForBeginners.drill: Tuck chin, round back – 10 reps on wedge mat
Hold 10 sec – 5 reps (builds shoulder strength)
Place hands and feet on same line – 10 each side
From low box, land with bent knees, arms up – 10 reps

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master basic shapes (tuck, pike, straddle), rolls, handstands, cartwheels – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Introduce round‑off, back handspring (with spot), basic bar kip, beam jumps – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Add back tuck (floor), flyaway dismount (bars), full turn on beam, vault table drills – 4x per week + strength 2x
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Routine construction, advanced tumbling (layouts, twists), competition preparation – maintain 4‑5x/week

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 4 sec, exhale 6 sec – before mounting apparatus
Close eyes, imagine perfect routine (each skill, landing) – 3 min before competition
Tap chalk, adjust grip, deep breath, then salute – same every time

pressureHandling.tip: On beam, focus on one skill at a time – not the entire routine
If you fall, get up immediately, reset, continue (judges respect recovery)
Use self‑talk: “I’ve done this a thousand times, I can do it now”
In finals, stick to your routine plan – don't add difficulty spontaneously

sportsmanship.rule: Salute judges and audience before and after routine
Quiet during other gymnasts' routines; no distracting movements
Congratulate competitors (hug or handshake) after medals ceremony

howToJoinSport.step: Find a recreational gymnastics club (USA Gymnastics or national federation directory)
Join a parent‑toddler class (ages 3‑5) or beginner school‑age class (6+). No experience needed
Club provides equipment and basic leotard/shorts initially
After 6‑12 months, consider pre‑team or advanced recreational track
For competition, join a competitive team (tryout based)
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Recreational classes: $50‑150/month; competitive team: $200‑500/month + meet fees, leotard ($50‑200), grips ($30‑60)

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Discipline – attends practice consistently, listens to coach
Body awareness – understands spatial position without looking
Resilience – gets up after falls, tries again
Focus – blocks out distractions during routine
Humility – accepts judges' scores without complaint

routineComposition.strategy: Incorporate highest difficulty elements that gymnast can execute cleanly (avoid fall risk)
On beam/floor, link acro/dance elements without pause (e.g., back handspring + layout stepout)
Stick landings to avoid 0.1‑0.3 step deductions; even small hop costs

apparatusSpecific.vault: Choose vault with appropriate difficulty – a clean 5.0 D score beats a messy 5.8
apparatusSpecific.bars: Use high‑to‑low transitions (e.g., Pak salto) for bonus; avoid extra swings
apparatusSpecific.beam: Include a mixed series (acro + dance) and a full turn (minimum 360°) to meet special requirements
apparatusSpecific.floor: Choreograph to music; emphasise expression and amplitude (height in leaps/tumbling)

competitionStrategy.qualifications: Play safe to qualify (avoid falls) – secure spot in finals
competitionStrategy.eventFinal: Take calculated risks (slightly higher difficulty) because it's medal opportunity
competitionStrategy.teamFinal: Consistency matters more than peak difficulty – every tenth counts

terminologies.term: D‑score – difficulty score (open‑ended, sum of element values)
E‑score – execution score (starting 10.0, deductions for form, landing, amplitude)
Neutral deduction – penalty (e.g., 0.3 for overtime, 0.1 for out of bounds)
Stick – landing with no steps or hops (ideal)
Fall – loss of support, touching floor or apparatus with hands/body (1.0 deduction)
Acro series – connected acrobatic elements (e.g., two back handsprings)
Connection bonus – additional D‑score points for linking skills without pause
Amplitude – height or distance of a skill (greater amplitude = higher execution)
Form – straight legs, pointed toes, tight body position
Cast – pushing away from bar to handstand (bars)
Kip – basic bar mount from hang to support
Giant – full circle swing around bar (horizontal bar/uneven bars)
Release move – letting go of bar and regrasping (e.g., Tkatchev)
