Dismount – skill to end routine and land on mat
Round‑off – 360° turn into back handspring setup (floor)
Back handspring – explosive backward flip with hands on floor
Layout – straight body position in a flip
Tuck – knees to chest in a flip
Pike – hips bent, legs straight in a flip
Split leap – leap with 180° leg separation (beam/floor)
Full turn – 360° rotation on one foot (beam/floor)
Spotting – coach assisting a skill physically to prevent injury
FIG – International Gymnastics Federation (Fédération Internationale de Gymnastique)

layout.lines: Baseline, sideline, half‑court line, centre circle, three‑point line, free‑throw line, lane lines
layout.zones: Backcourt, frontcourt, key (paint), wing, top of key, corner, low post

courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor arena (NBA, FIBA) or outdoor concrete court (street basketball)

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (increased VO2max)
Strengthens leg muscles (quads, hamstrings, calves) and core
Burns 600–900 calories per hour (high intensity)
Increases bone density and joint mobility
Enhances hand‑eye coordination and peripheral vision

mental.benefit: Reduces stress through fast decision‑making
Boosts self‑confidence and teamwork skills
Improves concentration and spatial awareness

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: leg swings, arm circles, torso twists, walking lunges, high knees (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jog (5 min), dribbling drills (3 min), form shooting (5 min), defensive slides (3 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Slow walking (5 min), static stretches: hamstring, quad, hip flexor, shoulder, lower back (30 sec each)

basicRules.rule: Game is played between two teams of 5 players each.
Objective: score by shooting ball through opponent's hoop (2 or 3 points).
Players dribble (bounce) ball while moving; cannot run with ball without dribbling (traveling).
Ball must be advanced within 8 seconds past half‑court (backcourt violation).
Shot clock: 24 seconds to attempt a shot (NBA/FIBA).

breaksIntervals.timeouts: Each team: 7 timeouts (NBA) / 5 timeouts (FIBA) – 60‑75 sec each

formats.shortMatch: Streetball (first to 11 or 16 by 1s and 2s, no shot clock) Full court, 4 quarters, shot clock, organized league rules

substitutesAndRoles.substitution: Unlimited substitutions, allowed during dead balls (after fouls, timeouts, out‑of‑bounds)

rulesRegulations.howToWin: Team with more points at end of regulation or overtime wins. If tied after regulation, play overtime periods

personalFoul.penalty: Opponent gets free throws (if shooting) or side out (if non‑shooting). After 5 fouls (NBA) / 5 fouls (FIBA) – player fouls out
personalFoul.examples: Illegal contact: blocking, charging, holding, pushing

technicalFoul.examples: Unsportsmanlike conduct, arguing with referee, delay of game

flagrantFoul.penalty: 2 free throws + possession; player ejected if flagrant 2

violations.examples: Traveling, double dribble, shot clock violation, backcourt violation, 3‑second violation (offensive/defensive)

fairPlay.rule: Respect officials' calls; no flopping (deceiving referee)
Shake hands with opponents before and after game
No taunting or trash‑talking

judgementCriteria.referees: 2 or 3 officials on court; call fouls, violations, out‑of‑bounds Used for last‑second buzzer beaters, flagrant fouls, boundary calls in NBA

preventionMethods.method: Wear high‑top basketball shoes for ankle support
Strengthen quadriceps and hamstrings (squats, lunges)
Do proprioception training (balance board, single‑leg stands)
Land softly on jumps – bend knees to absorb impact
Use mouthguard (recommended for contact leagues)
Warm‑up and cool‑down every session

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, productive cough) – risk of myocarditis
Uncontrolled hypertension or heart arrhythmia
Recent head injury/concussion (no play for at least 7‑14 days)
Severe asthma without rescue inhaler
Acute joint swelling (knee, ankle) – rest until resolved
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of heart disease, epilepsy, or severe migraines

commonMistakes.mistake: Carrying the ball (palming) during dribble
Jumping before deciding pass or shot – leads to travel or bad pass
Not boxing out on rebounds – gives opponent easy offensive boards
Reaching in instead of moving feet on defense – causes fouls
Poor shot selection (contested threes, off‑balance shots)
Overdribbling instead of passing
Not communicating on defense – leaves open cuts

hydrationImportance.beforeGame: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 250 ml 15 minutes before
hydrationImportance.duringGame: Sip 150‑200 ml every timeout or quarter break (every 10‑12 min)
hydrationImportance.afterGame: Weigh before/after – replace 150% of weight lost with water or sports drink
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Avoid carbonated drinks, excessive caffeine, alcohol
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dark urine, dizziness, dry mouth – stop play and hydrate

nutritionTips.preGame: 2‑3 hours before: pasta, rice, banana, oatmeal (carbs + moderate protein)
nutritionTips.preGameSnack: 30 min before: apple, orange, or energy gel
nutritionTips.duringGame: Water or isotonic drink (avoid heavy food)
nutritionTips.postGame: Within 30 min: chocolate milk, whey protein + banana, chicken + rice
nutritionTips.daily: Calcium (milk, yogurt) for bone health; iron (spinach, red meat) for endurance

physicalBurnout.limit: Play intense games/practices max 4‑5 times per week
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days per week or active recovery (light swimming)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, cycling, yoga to reduce impact on knees and ankles

mentalBurnout.signs: Lack of motivation, irritability, feeling of dread before practice
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks off, play pickup without pressure, set fun goals (e.g., improve left‑hand dribble)

shoes.features: High‑top for ankle support, non‑marking rubber sole, cushioning (Air, Boost, etc.)
shoes.brands: Nike, Adidas, Under Armour, Puma, New Balance

apparel.accessories: Shooting sleeve, compression tights, sweatbands, headband

accessories.item: Mouthguard (for contact leagues)
Ankle braces (if prone to sprains)
Ball pump, ball bag, gym bag

protective.item: Mouthguard (recommended for all competitive play)
Knee pads (for hard court dives)
Elbow pads (for floor burns)
Ankle braces (for chronic instability)
Protective eyewear (for players with glasses or previous eye injury)

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, instant cold packs in gym bag

coordination.desc: Hand‑eye coordination for dribbling, passing, and shooting
coordination.drill: Two‑ball dribbling (simultaneous or alternating) – 5 min daily

speedAgility.desc: Quick first step, lateral movement, change of direction
speedAgility.drill: Agility ladder (one‑in, two‑in, lateral shuffles) + cone drills (5‑10‑5 shuttle)

focusReaction.desc: React to pass, steal, or block shots within fractions of a second
focusReaction.drill: Partner throws tennis ball while dribbling – catch and continue

strategy.offensive: Pick and roll, motion offense, isolation, fast break
strategy.defensive: Man‑to‑man, zone (2‑3, 3‑2), full‑court press, help defense

xFactors.gameSense: Read opponent's defensive stance to decide drive or pass
xFactors.decisionMaking: When to shoot vs pass vs dribble – based on clock, defender position, and score

dailyPlan.day: Shooting form (100 shots from 5 spots) + dribbling drills (20 min) + 1v1 moves (20 min)
Pick & roll practice (20 min) + defensive slides (15 min) + full‑court scrimmage (30 min)
Strength training (squats, lunges, core) + plyometrics (box jumps) – 45 min
3‑point shooting (100 reps) + rebounding drills (20 min) + transition offense (20 min)
Zone offense vs defense (30 min) + free throws (50 reps under pressure) + scrimmage (30 min)
Conditioning (suicides, sprints) + team scrimmage (45 min)
Active recovery (light shooting, yoga, or rest)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Low dribble, high dribble, crossover between legs – 2 min each hand
Stand close to basket, one‑hand shot (focus on follow‑through) – 20 reps
Layups alternating left and right under basket – 10 makes each side
Chest pass, bounce pass – 20 reps each

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master basic dribbling, layups, and defensive stance – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add jump shot form, pick and roll, and rebounding technique – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce game situations, 2‑3 zone defense, fast break drills – 4x per week + 1 game
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Maintain skills with shooting drills (200‑300 shots/week) and 2 games/week

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 4 sec, hold 2 sec, exhale 6 sec – before free throws
Imagine making 10 consecutive free throws with perfect form – 2 min daily
Same pre‑free throw routine (two dribbles, spin ball, deep breath)

pressureHandling.tip: In crunch time (last 2 minutes), focus on process, not outcome – “make the right play”
Use positive self‑talk: “I’ve made this shot a thousand times”
Breathe deeply between free throws; ignore crowd noise
Forget missed shots immediately – next play mentality

sportsmanship.rule: Help opponent up after a foul
No trash‑talking or taunting (technical foul risk)
Respect referees; argue with captain only

howToJoinSport.step: Find local recreational league (YMCA, community centre, or school)
Buy a basketball (size 7 for men, 6 for women) and indoor/outdoor shoes
Join pickup games at local park or gym (ask to play next)
Attend open gym sessions or adult beginner clinics
Register for a low‑competitive league (D or C division) after 3‑6 months
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: League fees: $50‑150 per season; shoes: $50‑150; ball: $20‑50

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: High basketball IQ (knows when to pass, shoot, or cut)
Consistent free throw shooter (80%+ at advanced level)
Hustle plays (diving for loose balls, boxing out)
Unselfishness (assists over ego)
Defensive tenacity (stays low, slides feet)

offensive.strategy: Screener sets pick; ball handler drives; screener rolls to basket
Constant passing, cutting, and screening without set positions
Clear side for star player to go 1‑on‑1
Outlet pass after rebound, sprint downcourt for easy layup
Four players spaced behind three‑point line to open driving lanes

defensive.strategy: Two guards top, three forwards baseline – protects paint
Three guards top, two forwards baseline – better perimeter defense
Pressure inbound pass to force turnovers
On pick and roll, defender shows hard then returns to own man

specialSituations.situation: End of game (trailing) – foul intentionally to stop clock, shoot quick threes
End of game (leading) – hold ball, run clock, expect to be fouled

terminologies.term: Alley‑oop – pass caught mid‑air and dunked or laid in
And‑one – basket made despite foul, gets one free throw
Backcourt – team's own half (behind half‑court line)
Bank shot – shot that bounces off backboard into basket
Box out – establish position between opponent and basket to rebound
Carry – palm under ball while dribbling (violation)
Charge – offensive foul when defender is stationary
Double dribble – dribble, stop, then dribble again (violation)
Fast break – quick transition from defense to offense
Flop – exaggerated fall to draw foul (unsportsmanlike)
Free throw – unopposed shot from line after foul (1 point)
Hook shot – sweeping one‑handed shot over defender
Jump ball – ball tossed between two opponents to start game or overtime
Key / Paint – free‑throw lane area (restricted zone)
Layup – close shot off backboard using one hand
Pick – screen set to free a teammate
Post up – back‑to‑basket move near the basket
Rebound – retrieve missed shot
Screen – legal block to free a teammate
Steal – take ball from opponent without fouling
Swish – shot that goes through net without touching rim
Travel – moving without dribbling (walking violation)
Triple‑double – double‑digit stats in three categories (e.g., points, rebounds, assists)
Turnover – loss of possession due to violation or steal
Zone defense – each defender covers area, not specific player

dimensions.dangerZone: Red zone (1m wide) around competition area – warning area before out‑of‑bounds
dimensions.safetyArea: 3m wide surrounding mat (minimum) – crash mats for safety

layout.zones: Inner competition area (tatami), red danger zone, outer safety zone
layout.lines: Two white lines 4m apart for starting positions

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: PVC or vinyl tatami mats (spring‑loaded or foam core) – 4‑5 cm thick
courtFieldImage.venues: Indoor dojo or sports hall with sprung floor, padded walls, adequate lighting

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (high‑intensity intervals)
Strengthens full body: core, legs, back, grip (forearms)
Burns 500‑800 calories per hour (sparring sessions)
Enhances flexibility (hips, shoulders, spine)
Develops balance, coordination, and body awareness

mental.benefit: Builds discipline, respect, and humility (dojo etiquette)
Reduces stress through controlled physical exertion
Improves problem‑solving and adaptability (read opponent's movements)
Boosts confidence and self‑defence skills

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: neck rotations, arm circles, hip rotations, leg swings, ankle rolls (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), shrimping (ukemi breakfalls) (5 min), basic movement (tsugi ashi, tai sabaki) (5 min), grip fighting drills (3 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Static stretches: seated forward fold, butterfly stretch, shoulder stretch, neck stretch (30 sec each); deep breathing (2 min)

basicRules.rule: Two judoka (players) attempt to throw each other to the mat, hold down, or submit via joint lock/choke.
Ippon (full point) ends the match immediately: throw with force, speed, and control landing on back; or hold down for 20 seconds; or opponent submits or passes out.
Waza‑ari (half point): throw lacking one criterion, or hold down 10‑19 seconds. Two waza‑ari equal ippon.
Penalties (shido) given for passivity, false attacks, or illegal techniques. Accumulation leads to hansoku‑make (disqualification).
Prohibited: strikes, finger/toe locks, leg locks (except ashi garami restricted), touching face, head diving.

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: 2 judoka (individual). Team competition: 5 weight categories (men/women) – matches are individual then aggregate.

matchTimings.goldenScore: Unlimited overtime until score or penalty decides (no time limit)

breaksIntervals.injuryTimeout: Up to 5 minutes for medical evaluation (once per match)

formats.shortMatch: Golden score only (no time limit, first score wins) – used when regular time ends 0‑0

substitutesAndRoles.substitution:
In team events, substitute judoka can replace injured player before match (not during). Individual no substitutes.
Grip fighter – controls opponent's sleeve/lapel to dictate pace Counter specialist – waits for opponent's attack then counters Newaza specialist – excels on ground (hold downs, submissions)

scoringSystem.goldenScore: First score (any ippon or waza‑ari) or penalty (third shido) wins

rulesRegulations.howToWin: Ippon (throw/hold/submission), opponent's hansoku‑make (disqualification), or by superiority after 4 minutes if no ippon (judges decision based on attacks/penalties).

foulsAndPenalties.shido: (non‑critical violation): 1st warning (no point), 2nd warning, 3rd warning = hansoku‑make (loss). Examples: false attack, passivity (no attack for 30 sec), defensive posture (excessive stalling), illegal grip (pistol grip, cross grip without attack).
foulsAndPenalties.hansokuMakeDirect: Disqualification (loss). Examples: dangerous throws (kawazu gake, head dive), joint locks on fingers/toes, leg locks (except ashi garami with caution), punching/kicking, intentional foul to opponent's spine.
foulsAndPenalties.medicalForbidden: No blood allowed (must stop to clean); if cannot stop bleeding, loss by default.

fairPlay.rule: Bow before stepping on/off mat; bow to opponent and referee before/after match
No celebrating excessively after ippon (respect opponent)
Help opponent up after throw (good sportsmanship)

judgementCriteria.referee: One mat referee (inside) calls scores and penalties; two corner judges assist (side)
judgementCriteria.videoReview: Used for ippon/waza‑ari decisions and hansoku‑make (IJF events)
judgementCriteria.goldenScoreDecision: If no score, judges decide by activity, attacks, and penalties (fewer shido wins)

commonInjuries.injury: Knee injuries (ACL, MCL from twisting throws – tai otoshi, kata guruma)

preventionMethods.method: Master ukemi (breakfalls) – forward, backward, side rolls – before any throwing practice
Strength training: quads, hamstrings (lunges, squats), rotator cuff (external rotations), grip (farmer's walk)
Tape fingers (buddy tape) for grip protection
Wear knee pads (optional but recommended for newaza)
Proper warm‑up with neck strengthening (bridges – only under supervision)
Respect tapout – release joint locks immediately when opponent taps

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, cough) – risk of fainting or spread
Uncontrolled hypertension (blood pressure spikes during throws)
Recent concussion (no judo for at least 4‑6 weeks)
Active joint inflammation (arthritis flare‑up in knees, shoulders)
Skin infections (ringworm, impetigo, staph) – highly contagious on mats
Epilepsy not well controlled (risk during chokeholds)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of neck injury, herniated disc, or heart arrhythmia

commonMistakes.mistake: Crossing feet in standing (off‑balance, easy to throw)
Pulling with arms only instead of using hips and legs for throws
Falling flat on back without proper ukemi (head slapping mat) – increases concussion risk
Stiff‑arming (holding opponent at distance without attack) – earns shido for passivity
Looking at the ground during throws (loses posture)
Releasing grips too early before completing throw
Ignoring tapout – causing unnecessary injury (can be banned)

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (avoid heavy stomach)
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 100‑150 ml between matches (not during match – no breaks)
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost (weigh before/after); electrolyte drink if heavy sweat
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dark urine, dizziness, reduced grip strength – inform coach, stop if severe

nutritionTips.preMatch: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (rice, pasta, oatmeal) + lean protein (chicken, fish) – avoid heavy fats
nutritionTips.preMatchSnack: 30 min before: banana, apple, or energy gel (if multiple matches)
nutritionTips.duringTournament: Water and small snacks between matches (dried fruit, nuts) – maintain blood sugar
nutritionTips.postMatch: Within 30 min: protein shake, chocolate milk, or eggs + toast (muscle repair)
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (red meat, spinach) for endurance; calcium (dairy) for bone density; adequate protein for muscle recovery

physicalBurnout.limit: Intensive judo (randori + drilling) max 4‑5 days per week (competition prep)
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days or active recovery (swimming, light jogging)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, yoga, strength training (non‑impact) to reduce joint strain

mentalBurnout.signs: Loss of motivation, avoiding randori, irritability, dreading practice
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, focus on technique instead of winning, set small achievable goals

equipment.judogi: Uniform – heavy cotton jacket (uwagi), pants (zubon), belt (obi). Colour: white or blue (IJF approved).
equipment.belt: White (beginner) to black (dan ranks) and red‑and‑white (6th‑8th dan), red (9th‑10th dan)
equipment.mat: Tatami – PVC or vinyl covered foam, 4‑5 cm thick, interlocking tiles or roll‑out

protection.optional: Knee pads (volleyball style), mouthguard (recommended), finger tape, ear guards (for cauliflower ear prevention)

equipment.shoes: Not worn on mat (barefoot). Indoor slippers for off‑mat only.

accessories.item: Judo bag, water bottle, training notebook, spare gi for tournaments

protective.item: Mouthguard (highly recommended – prevents dental injury and concussion reduction)
Knee pads (prevents mat burns, cushioning for newaza)
Finger tape (prevents sprains, supports PIP joints)
Ear guards (headgear) – prevents cauliflower ear (optional but common in competitive judo)
Chest protector (women optional, men rare)

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, instant cold packs, wound closure strips, tape

coordination.desc: Timing and coordination between grips, footwork, and hip placement
coordination.drill: Shadow uchikomi (repetitive entry without throw) – 20 reps each side (seoi nage, o goshi)

speedAgility.desc: Quick direction changes (tai sabaki), explosive entry into throws
speedAgility.drill: Agility ladder (lateral, in‑out, hopscotch) – 5 min; sprint starts from lying position (ukemi to stand)

focusReaction.desc: React to opponent's attack (counter throw) or escape from hold
focusReaction.drill: Partner attacks with o uchi gari, you counter with tai otoshi – 10 reps each

strategy.offensive: Kumi‑kata (grip fighting) to dominant grips (e.g., lapel + sleeve), then attack with combination (e.g., o uchi to seoi nage)

xFactors.defensive: Use low stance, strong stiff‑arm to prevent entry (but caution for shido), counter attacks (kaeshi waza)
xFactors.gameSense: Read opponent's weight distribution – attack when they shift weight forward (e.g., seoi nage) or backward (e.g., osoto gari)
xFactors.decisionMaking: Choose throw based on grip and distance: close range (o goshi, seoi nage), long range (uchi mata, osoto gari)

dailyPlan.day: Ukemi breakfalls (10 min) + Uchikomi (20 reps each: seoi nage, o goshi) + Nage komi (throws with crash mat) 20 throws + Randori (3 rounds x 3 min)
Newaza (groundwork): hold downs (kesa gatame) transitions (15 min) + submission drills (juji gatame, okuri eri jime) (15 min) + Randori newaza (3 rounds x 2 min)
Strength & conditioning: squats, deadlifts, pull‑ups, grip strength (farmer's walk) – 45 min + light stretching
Combination drills: o uchi gari to seoi nage (20 reps) + osoto gari to sasae tsurikomi ashi (20 reps) + Randori (4 rounds x 3 min)
Defense drills: counters to common throws (e.g., tani otoshi against seoi nage) (15 min) + situational randori (starting with disadvantaged grip)
Tournament simulation: full rules (4 min matches) + video analysis of own matches (30 min)
Active recovery (swimming, yoga, or complete rest)

drillsForBeginners.drill: From squat, roll backward, slap mat with arms – 10 reps
Shuffle forward/backward without crossing feet – 2 min
Pivot on left foot, bring right hip across partner – 10 reps (static)
From side control, lock head and arm, keep hips low – hold 10 sec

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master ukemi (all directions), basic footwork (tai sabaki), 3 throws (o goshi, seoi nage, osoto gari) – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add 3 more throws (o uchi gari, sasae, de ashi harai) and basic newaza (kesa gatame, juji gatame) – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce combinations, counters, competition randori, grip fighting strategies – 4‑5x per week + local tournaments
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine tokui waza (favourite throw), video analysis, condition for competition – maintain 4x/week

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 3 sec, exhale 5 sec – during bow before match
Close eyes, imagine perfect throw (entry, off‑balance, execution) – 2 min daily
Same warm‑up sequence, same gripping pattern, same breathing

pressureHandling.tip: In golden score, do not force attack – wait for opponent's mistake (penalty or off‑balance)
If down by shido (penalty), increase attack frequency to avoid third shido
Use self‑talk: “I’ve drilled this throw 10,000 times, I can execute now”
After losing, bow to opponent, leave mat, then analyse – don't dwell emotionally

sportsmanship.rule: Bow to opponent and referee before/after match
Help opponent up after throw (except when injured)
No celebrating on mat (raise hand after ippon, then bow)

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local judo club (USA Judo, British Judo, or national federation website)
Join a beginner class (adults or children) – most clubs lend gi initially
Purchase a judogi after 2‑3 months ($50‑150 for basic white gi)
Get a club membership and accident insurance (required for competition)
Compete in local novice tournaments after 6‑12 months (white belt division)
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Monthly club fees: $30‑100; gi: $50‑150; competition entry: $20‑50; insurance: $20‑50/year

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Discipline – arrives early, helps clean mats, follows dojo etiquette
Resilience – gets thrown, gets up, tries again
Humility – respects lower ranks, learns from losses
Adaptability – changes strategy when opponent counters
Courage – attacks despite fear of being countered

offensive.strategy: Secure dominant grip (e.g., high collar + sleeve), break opponent's strong grip, then attack immediately
Attack with ashi waza (foot sweep) to unbalance, then follow with hip throw (e.g., de ashi harai to o goshi)
Linked throws: first throw fails, second immediately (e.g., seoi nage to ko uchi gari)

defensive.strategy: Bend knees, lower centre of gravity – harder to throw
Keep opponent at distance, but avoid passivity shido
Wait for opponent's throw, then redirect (e.g., tani otoshi against seoi nage)

newaza.strategy: After throw (even incomplete), immediately move to hold down (osae komi) before opponent recovers
When opponent is belly down, use techniques (e.g., sankaku jime) to roll them over

specialSituations.situation: Leading by waza‑ari with 1 minute left – play defensively, avoid risky attacks, stay centre mat
Trailing by shido (2 shido vs 1) – attack aggressively to force third shido on opponent or score

terminologies.term: Ippon – full point (match end) – throw with force, speed, back control
Waza‑ari – half point (two = ippon)
Shido – minor penalty (accumulate 3 = loss)
Hansoku‑make – disqualification (direct or cumulative shido)
Golden score – overtime (first score or penalty wins)
Ukemi – breakfall (safety roll/slap)
Randori – free practice (sparring)
Uchikomi – repetitive entry without throwing
Nage komi – full throw with crash mat
Kumi‑kata – gripping
Tai sabaki – body turning (footwork)
Tsugi ashi – shuffle step
Ashi waza – foot/leg techniques
Koshi waza – hip techniques
Te waza – hand techniques
Osae komi – hold down (20 sec for ippon)
Juji gatame – cross arm lock
Okuri eri jime – sliding collar choke
Kesa gatame – scarf hold
Seoi nage – shoulder throw
O goshi – major hip throw
Osoto gari – major outer reap
O uchi gari – major inner reap
De ashi harai – advancing foot sweep
Uchi mata – inner thigh throw
Tokui waza – favourite technique
Kaeshi waza – counter technique
Renraku waza – combination technique
Kata – formal pre‑arranged forms
Dojo – training hall
Judogi – judo uniform
IJF – International Judo Federation

layout.lines: Boundary lines (sidelines, end lines) made of flat tape, no centre line on sand
layout.zones: Attack area (no specific line; any player can attack from anywhere but must be behind the attack line if defined)

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Fine loose sand (washed, free of rocks and debris), level and raked between matches
courtFieldImage.venues: Outdoor beach or dedicated sand court facility, often with sun and wind exposure

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (jumping, sprinting in sand)
Strengthens legs (quads, hamstrings, calves, glutes) – sand adds resistance
Low impact on joints (sand absorbs shock, reduces knee/ankle stress)
Burns 400‑600 calories per hour (moderate to intense play)
Enhances agility, balance, and core stability (unstable surface)

mental.benefit: Reduces stress through outdoor, social play
Improves focus and adaptability (wind, sun, heat conditions)
Builds teamwork (only 2 players, must cover court together)

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles, torso twists, leg swings, walking lunges on sand (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging on sand (5 min), passing drills (forearm and overhead, 5 min), jumping jacks and low jumps (2 min), short sprints in sand (4 x 10m)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Light walking on sand (5 min), static stretches: hamstring, quad, calf, lower back, shoulder, triceps (30 sec each), rinse sand off with water

basicRules.rule: Two teams of 2 players each (no substitutes). Played on sand court.
Objective: ground ball on opponent's court or force opponent into error.
Maximum 3 hits per side (block touch counts as one of the three).
Same player cannot hit ball twice consecutively (except after block touch).
Ball may contact any part of body (including foot) – open hand setting must be clean (no spin, double contact called strictly).
Matches: best of 3 sets (21 points each set, win by 2, no cap in FIVB). Third set to 15 points.

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: 2 per team on court. No substitutes; if player injured, team forfeits.

breaksIntervals.technicalTimeout: 30 seconds per team per set (when leading team reaches 7 or 11 points in FIVB)
breaksIntervals.injuryTimeout: Up to 5 minutes per player per match; if cannot continue, forfeit.

formats.shortFormat: One set to 15 or 21 (win by 2, cap at 17 or 23 in some events)

rulesRegulations.substitutesAndRoles: No substitutes. Roles: blocker (at net) and defender (backcourt). Players rotate after every sideout.
rulesRegulations.scoringSystem: Rally point system – point scored on every rally regardless of serve. Win set by 2 points, no ceiling in FIVB (21‑19, etc.).
rulesRegulations.howToWin: Win 2 out of 3 sets. Third set to 15 points, win by 2.

foulsAndPenalties.fault: Double hit (same player twice in a row without block)
Four hits (more than 3 touches per side)
Lift or carry (prolonged contact with ball)
Double contact on setting (if spin detected) – stricter in beach than indoor
Reaching over net (except on follow‑through or block)
Net touch (any part of body touching net between antennae) – point to opponent
Foot fault on serve (touching baseline or court before hit)
foulsAndPenalties.penaltyUnsportsmanlike: Yellow card (warning), red card (point deduction), disqualification

fairPlay.rule: Call own touches (especially if ball hits body but you know it touched)
No arguing with referee; respect decisions
In non‑umpired games, honesty on line calls

judgementCriteria.referee: One referee (standing on a platform) calls faults, net touches, line calls.

commonInjuries.injury: Ankle sprain (landing on uneven sand or rolling over edge of court)
Knee pain (patellar tendinitis from jumping on sand)
Lower back strain (repetitive bending for digs)
Finger sprains (jammed fingers from blocking or setting)
Foot blisters and sand burns (sliding on sand)

preventionMethods.method: Warm‑up on sand before explosive movements (sand is unstable)
Strengthen ankles (balance exercises, calf raises) and knees (squats, lunges)
Use sunscreen (SPF 30+, water resistant) and reapply every 2 hours
Hydrate before, during, and after (sand play increases sweat loss)
Wear protective clothing (hat, sunglasses, rash guard for sun)
Proper landing technique: bend knees, absorb impact, avoid landing on opponent's foot
Tape fingers if prone to jamming (especially setters)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, cough) – risk of heat illness
Uncontrolled hypertension (jumping spikes BP)
Recent ankle or knee injury (sand increases instability)
Severe asthma without pre‑exercise medication (heat, sand dust triggers)
Skin infection or open wound (sand can infect)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of heatstroke, heart arrhythmia, or chronic ankle instability

commonMistakes.mistake: Setting with spin (double contact fault) – stricter in beach volleyball
Jumping too early for block (timing off)
Not communicating with partner (who takes the ball)
Standing flat‑footed instead of staying low and ready
Overhitting (aiming for lines instead of open court)
Not using enough legs in serve (weak serve)
Running into sun or wind without adjusting (poor ball tracking)

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (especially in hot sun)
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 150‑200 ml at every timeout or changeover (every 7 points) – use sports drink if high heat (>85°F/30°C)
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink if heavy sweating (sand reflects heat)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dark urine, headache, reduced jumping ability – stop, hydrate, seek shade

nutritionTips.preMatch: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (pasta, rice, oatmeal, banana) + moderate protein (egg, yogurt) – low fat and fibre to avoid GI distress
nutritionTips.preMatchSnack: 30‑60 min before: banana, energy bar, or rice cake with honey
nutritionTips.duringMatch: Between matches: small snack (dried fruit, nuts, energy gel) + water
nutritionTips.postMatch: Within 30 min: protein + carbs (chocolate milk, whey shake + banana, chicken + rice) for muscle repair and glycogen replenishment
nutritionTips.daily: Calcium (dairy) for bone density; vitamin D (sunlight or supplement); adequate protein (1.2‑1.6 g/kg body weight) for muscle recovery

physicalBurnout.limit: Competitive matches: max 3‑4 per week; training: 4‑5 days per week (sand is demanding on calves and lower back)
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days or active recovery (swimming, light stretching) per week
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, yoga, cycling (low impact, maintains fitness)

mentalBurnout.signs: Lack of motivation, frustration with partner, dreading practice in heat
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, play indoor volleyball (different surface), set small goals (e.g., improve jump float serve)

equipment.ball: Outdoor (beach) volleyball – slightly larger and softer than indoor, water resistant, dimpled surface. Circumference 66‑68 cm, weight 260‑280g.
equipment.net: Square mesh, 8m wide, 1m tall (plus band), with antennae (80cm above net).
equipment.posts: Round metal or fibreglass, adjustable height, set 8m apart.
equipment.footwear: Barefoot (standard) or beach socks / neoprene booties for cold or rough sand.

apparel.women: Bikini or tank top + shorts (competition), or one‑piece swimsuit. No large logos.
apparel.men: Shorts + tank top or bare chest (common in recreational). Competition: singlet + shorts.

equipment.accessories: Sunglasses (polarised, UV protection), hat or visor, sunscreen, towel, water bottle, line tape (for marking court), rake (for smoothing sand).

protective.item: Sunscreen (SPF 30+, water resistant, broad spectrum)
Hat and sunglasses (UV protection, reduce glare)
Lip balm with SPF
Ankle brace (if prone to sprains – sand still can cause injury)
Knee pads (optional – some players wear thin knee pads for diving)
Finger tape (for setters and blockers)

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, blister plasters, instant cold packs, eye wash (for sand in eyes), tweezers (for splinters)

coordination.desc: Hand‑eye coordination for passing, setting, hitting; footwork in sand (slower, wider stance)
coordination.drill: Passing to self (forearm and overhead) – 50 reps each; wall hitting (control)

speedAgility.desc: Quick lateral movement in sand, explosive jump for block and spike
speedAgility.drill: Sand sprints (10m x 10), lateral shuffle with resistance band, box jumps (on firm surface)

focusReaction.desc: React to opponent's hit, read body language, adapt to wind and sun
focusReaction.drill: Partner hits random balls (short, deep, left, right) – defender reacts

strategy.offensive: Target opponent's weak pass, use shot variety (roll shot, cut shot, line), serve aggressively

xFactors.defensive: Read hitter's shoulder angle, position accordingly; use hard‑driven ball defence (forearm pass)
xFactors.gameSense: Use wind: serve with wind at back; against wind, use higher trajectory. Use sun: blind opponent with high toss.
xFactors.decisionMaking: When to go for block vs drop back to cover, when to spike hard vs tip (soft shot)

dailyPlan.day: Sand conditioning (sprints, lateral shuffles, jump squats) 20 min + Passing drills (forearm and overhead) 30 min
Serving (topspin, float, jump serve) 20 min + Hitting lines (approach, spike) 30 min + Blocking footwork 15 min
Defence drills (digging hard spikes, reading hitter) 30 min + Transition from defence to offence 20 min
Set plays (high sets, shoot sets) 20 min + Communication drills (call ball, switch) 15 min + Scrimmage 30 min
Tactical game (e.g., only hit line, or only use shots) 20 min + Serving under pressure (target zones) 20 min
Full match simulation (best of 3 sets) + video analysis 30 min
Active recovery (light swimming, stretching, or complete rest)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Ball to self, maintain control – 50 consecutive
Ball to self, no spin – 50 consecutive
Hit ball against wall with forearm, keep rally – 20 hits
3‑step approach without ball (right‑left‑jump for right‑handers) – 20 reps

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master forearm pass, overhead set, basic serve (underhand or float), footwork in sand – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add spike approach, block, jump serve, basic defence – 4x per week + sand conditioning
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce advanced shots (cut, roll, line), tactical awareness, communication, match play – 4‑5x per week + tournaments
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine shot selection, partner chemistry, video analysis, maintain 4x/week with competition

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 3 sec, exhale 5 sec – between points, before serve
Close eyes, imagine perfect serve or block – 2 min before match
Same ball spin, same deep breath, same target glance

pressureHandling.tip: At match point (e.g., 14‑14 in third set), focus on making serve in (high percentage), not ace
After error, high‑five partner, reset – “Next point”
Use self‑talk: “Stay low, watch ball, move feet”
If opponent on a run, call timeout to break rhythm, discuss tactic with partner

sportsmanship.rule: Call own touches (e.g., ball touched block but went out)
Help opponent if they fall or are injured
No taunting or staring down opponent after scoring

howToJoinSport.step: Find a beach volleyball court (public parks, clubs, or resorts). Many have open play sessions.
Buy a beach volleyball (e.g., Mikasa, Wilson) and basic attire (shorts, tank top, sunglasses).
Join a beginner clinic or group lesson (often 2‑4 sessions). Learn rules and basic skills.
Attend “open gym” or “pickup” games – rotate in, play with different partners.
Enter a novice tournament (2‑player) or reverse coed (man/woman) at local level.
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Court fees: $0‑20 per session (public vs private). Ball: $30‑60. Clinic: $20‑50/session. Tournament entry: $20‑50 per team.

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Communication – constantly talks to partner (mine, yours, switch)
Resilience – bounces back from errors, doesn't blame partner
Adaptability – adjusts to wind, sun, opponent tactics
Footwork – moves well in sand, stays low
Consistency – keeps ball in play, reduces unforced errors

serving.strategy: Serve to opponent's weaker passer (often the larger player).
Low trajectory, unpredictable movement – effective in wind.
Serve just over net to force opponent forward, then attack deep.

blocking.strategy: Push ball high to allow defender to play; or hard block (penetrate) to kill.
Take away line, leave cross court to defender.

defence.strategy: Start 2‑3m from net to cover hard spikes; move forward for short balls.

offence.strategy: Mix power spikes with roll shots, cut shots, and pokey (open hand tip).
Use wind to move ball away from defender.

specialSituations.situation: Sun in eyes – wear sunglasses, use hand to shield eyes on serve receive, or switch sides at changeover.
Wind at your back – serve deep, attack with topspin; wind in face – serve short, use higher trajectory.

terminologies.term: Sideout – winning rally when receiving serve (regain serve)
Rally point – point scored on every rally regardless of server
Block touch – contact with ball at net that counts as one of three hits
Forearm pass (bump) – using forearms to pass
Overhead pass (set) – using fingertips to set
Attack (spike) – hitting ball downward into opponent's court
Cut shot – sharp angle cross‑court spike
Line shot – spike down the sideline
Roll shot – soft, spinning shot over block
Pokey – open‑hand tip (fingertips) over block
Joust – both players contact ball simultaneously above net
Double hit – same player hits twice consecutively (fault)
Lift/carry – prolonged contact (fault)
Net touch – any body part touching net between antennae (fault)
Foot fault – serve touching baseline or court before hit
Antennae – vertical rods on net marking sideline extension
Switch – players exchange positions after sideout
Peel – defender moves from net to cover tip after block
FIVB – Fédération Internationale de Volleyball (international governing body)
AVP – Association of Volleyball Professionals (US pro beach tour)

layout.lines: Touchline (sideline), goal line (end line), halfway line, centre circle, penalty area arc, corner arc (1m radius)
layout.zones: Defensive third, midfield third, attacking third, flanks (wings), channels

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Natural grass (most common), artificial turf (FIFA approved), hybrid grass (reinforced)
courtFieldImage.venues: Outdoor stadium (open or roofed), indoor arenas for futsal (smaller)

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (continuous running, intervals)
Strengthens legs (quads, hamstrings, calves), core, and upper body (shoulders for throws)
Burns 500‑900 calories per hour (depending on intensity and position)
Enhances bone density (weight‑bearing activity)
Develops agility, balance, coordination (dribbling, turning)

mental.benefit: Reduces stress through team interaction and physical exertion
Improves decision‑making, spatial awareness, and tactical thinking
Builds discipline, teamwork, and leadership

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: leg swings, high knees, butt kicks, walking lunges, torso twists, arm circles, ankle rotations (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5‑10 min), passing drills (5 min), dribbling cones (5 min), short sprints (4‑6 x 30m at 70%), shooting/heading light (5 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Light jogging or walking (5 min), static stretches: hamstring, quad, calf, hip flexor, groin, lower back (30 sec each), foam rolling if available

basicRules.rule: Two teams of 11 players each (10 outfield + 1 goalkeeper). Objective: score by putting ball into opponent's goal.
Match duration: 90 minutes (two halves of 45 minutes), plus added time (injury, substitutions, time‑wasting).

Offside rule: Attacker is offside if nearer to opponent's goal line than both the ball and second‑last opponent when ball is played (unless in own half, level with second‑last, or receiving from goal kick/corner/throw‑in).
Fouls: direct free kick (e.g., kicking, tripping, pushing, holding, handball) or indirect free kick (e.g., dangerous play, obstruction, goalkeeper handling backpass).
Penalty kick: awarded for foul inside penalty area (taken from 11m spot, only goalkeeper and kicker).

Yellow card (caution) for unsporting behaviour, dissent, persistent fouling; two yellows = red card (sent off). Red card for serious foul play, violent conduct, spitting, denying obvious goal‑scoring opportunity (DOGSO).

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: 11 per team on field. Minimum 7 to continue match (some competitions). Substitutes: typically 3‑5 (5 in most competitions since 2022).
rulesRegulations.matchTimings: 90 minutes regulation; extra time (30 minutes, 2×15) in knockout matches; penalty shootout if still tied.

breaksIntervals.injuryTimeout: Medical staff may enter; no fixed time but added to stoppage time
breaksIntervals.drinksBreak: Possible in hot conditions (referee discretion) – 1 minute at 30th and 75th minute

substitutesAndRoles.substitution: Up to 5 substitutes per match (3 substitution windows plus half‑time). Concussion substitutes allowed in some competitions. Substituted player cannot return.

roles.role: Goalkeeper – only player who can handle ball in penalty area

rulesRegulations.scoringSystem: Goal = 1 point. Team with more goals wins. If equal, draw (league) or extra time/penalties (knockout).
rulesRegulations.howToWin: Score more goals than opponent within regulation time (or extra time + penalties if applicable).

foulsAndPenalties.directFreeKick: Fouls: kicking, tripping, jumping at, charging, striking, pushing, tackling from behind, holding, spitting, handball (deliberate).
foulsAndPenalties.indirectFreeKick: Obstruction, dangerous play (high foot), goalkeeper handling backpass or holding ball >6 seconds, offside.
foulsAndPenalties.yellowCard: Caution for unsporting behaviour, dissent, persistent infringement, delaying restart, entering/leaving without permission, simulation (diving).
foulsAndPenalties.redCard: Serious foul play, violent conduct, spitting, deliberate handball to deny goal (DOGSO), denying obvious goal‑scoring opportunity with foul (DOGSO), abusive language, second yellow.
foulsAndPenalties.penaltyKick: Direct free kick foul inside penalty area.

fairPlay.rule: Respect referee decisions; no arguing
Return ball to opponent after injury stoppage (fair play)
No simulation (diving) – punishable by yellow card

judgementCriteria.referee: One centre referee; two assistant referees (linesmen) for offside and throw‑ins; fourth official for substitutions and added time.

rulesRegulations.var: Video Assistant Referee (VAR) used in top competitions for clear and obvious errors: goals, penalties, red cards, mistaken identity. Electronic system to determine if ball crossed goal line (used in top leagues).

commonInjuries.injury: Ankle sprain (inversion – landing on opponent's foot)
Groin strain (abductor – sudden change of direction)

preventionMethods.method: Proper warm‑up (FIFA 11+ programme reduces injury by 30‑50%) – includes running, strengthening, plyometrics, balance.
Strength training: hamstrings (Nordic curls), quads (squats), glutes, core (planks), calf raises.
Use appropriate footwear (studs for grass, moulded for artificial turf).
Maintain hydration and nutrition (cramps increase injury risk).
Cool‑down with static stretching after matches.
Gradual return to play after injury (no rushing).

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, cough) – risk of myocarditis, dehydration
Uncontrolled hypertension (sprinting spikes BP)
Recent concussion (no play for at least 10‑14 days, graduated return)
Severe asthma without pre‑exercise medication (cold weather trigger)
Active joint swelling (knee, ankle)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have heart murmur, arrhythmia, epilepsy, or history of ACL tear

commonMistakes.mistake: Standing still when not on the ball (lack of movement off the ball)
Ball watching instead of marking opponent (defensive error)
Poor first touch (bouncing ball away, losing possession)
Dribbling too much instead of passing
Striking ball with toe instead of laces or inside foot (poor accuracy)
Goalkeeper: poor positioning, spilling shots, weak distribution
Complaining to referee instead of playing (yellow card risk)

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (especially in warm weather)
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 150‑200 ml at breaks (halftime, drinks break) – use sports drink if >60 min in heat
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink if heavy sweating (weigh before/after training)
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Excessive caffeine (diuretic, jitters), carbonated drinks (bloating), alcohol (dehydrating)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dark urine, dizziness, headache, reduced performance – stop, inform referee, hydrate

nutritionTips.preMatch: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (pasta, rice, oatmeal, whole grain bread) + lean protein (chicken, fish, egg) – low fat and fibre (avoid GI distress)
nutritionTips.preMatchSnack: 30‑60 min before: banana, energy bar, or rice cake with jam
nutritionTips.duringMatch: Small snack if long match: orange slice, energy gel, or sports drink
nutritionTips.postMatch: Within 30 min: protein + carbs (chocolate milk, whey shake + banana, chicken + rice) for muscle repair and glycogen replenishment
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (spinach, red meat) for oxygen transport; calcium (dairy) for bone health; vitamin D (sunlight or supplement); adequate protein (1.2‑1.6 g/kg body weight)

physicalBurnout.limit: Competitive matches: max 2‑3 per week; training: 4‑5 days per week (include low‑intensity days)
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 1‑2 full rest days or active recovery (swimming, light cycling) per week
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, cycling, yoga (low impact, maintains fitness, reduces overuse injury)

mentalBurnout.signs: Loss of motivation, frustration, dreading practice, poor performance, irritability
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, play small‑sided games for fun (no pressure), set small goals (e.g., improve weak foot), vary training (technical drills instead of matches)

equipment.ball: Size 5 (circumference 68‑70 cm, weight 410‑450 g) for age 13+. Size 4 for youth. Leather or synthetic (FIFA Quality Pro).

footwear.boots: Studs (cleats): firm ground (FG) for natural grass, soft ground (SG) for wet grass (metal studs), artificial ground (AG) for turf, indoor (IC) flat sole for futsal.

equipment.shinGuards: Mandatory (covered by socks). Must be full shin coverage (plastic or fibreglass).
equipment.apparel: Jersey (numbered), shorts, socks (must cover shin guards). Goalkeeper wears different colour.
equipment.goalkeeperGloves: Latex palm for grip, with finger protection (optional).
equipment.accessories: Water bottle, captain's armband, extra laces, bag, practice cones, bibs.

protective.item: Shin guards (mandatory – reduces risk of tibial fracture/contusions)
Mouthguard (recommended, especially for youth and heading)
Goalkeeper gloves (prevent hand/finger injuries)
Headgear (soft padding) – optional, for players concerned about heading concussions
Ankle braces (if prone to sprains)

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, instant cold packs, splint, eye wash, scissors (for cutting tape/shirt), emergency blanket

coordination.desc: Ball control (first touch), dribbling, passing accuracy, shooting technique
coordination.drill: Juggling (keep ball up with feet/thighs/head – target 50); passing against wall (100 reps each foot)

speedAgility.desc: Acceleration, change of direction, sprint speed with ball
speedAgility.drill: Cone drills (zig‑zag, slalom), sprint repeats (30m x 8), agility ladder (lateral, in‑out, crossover)

focusReaction.desc: React to loose ball, opponent's move, or cross; anticipation of pass
focusReaction.drill: Reaction ball (unpredictable bounce) – catch or kick; small‑sided games (3v3) high tempo

strategy.possession: Maintain ball control, use short passes, move into space, switch play

xFactors.counterAttack:
Quick transition from defence to attack, long passes to fast forwards High pressure on opponent's defence to force mistakes
Read opponent's body shape to predict pass/dribble; position to intercept When to dribble vs pass, when to shoot vs cross, when to tackle vs jockey

dailyPlan.day: Technical: passing & receiving (20 min), dribbling (15 min), shooting (15 min), small‑sided game (20 min)
Physical: sprints (6x40m), agility ladder (10 min), strength (squats, lunges, core) – 30 min
Tactical: positional play, attacking/defensive shape (30 min), crossing & finishing (20 min)
Possession drills (rondo 5v2) (20 min), combination play (20 min), match simulation (30 min)
Set pieces (corners, free kicks) (20 min), shooting under pressure (15 min), cool‑down
Full match (11v11 or 7v7) – 60‑90 min
Active recovery (light jog, stretching, yoga) or complete rest

drillsForBeginners.drill: Two players, 5m apart, pass using inside of foot – 20 reps each foot
Slalom through 5 cones, keep ball close – 5 repetitions
From penalty spot, aim for corners – 20 shots
Drop ball on foot, kick up, catch – progress to 5 consecutive touches

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master basic passing, receiving, dribbling, shooting technique, and rules – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Introduce weak foot practice, heading, tackling, positioning, small‑sided games – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Add tactical awareness, set pieces, match fitness (intervals), 11v11 matches – 4‑5x per week + local league
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine decision‑making, video analysis, advanced skills (e.g., stepovers, knuckleball), maintain 4x/week

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 3 sec, exhale 5 sec – before taking a penalty or free kick
Close eyes, imagine scoring a goal, making a crucial tackle, or saving a penalty – 2 min before match
Same warm‑up, same music, same breathing pattern before kick‑off

pressureHandling.tip: Penalty shootout: decide spot and technique before walking; ignore goalkeeper; breathe; strike confidently.
After a mistake (e.g., own goal): reset mentally, focus on next action, apologise to team, move on.
Use self‑talk: “Next play, win the ball, keep it simple.”
If trailing by 2 goals: focus on one goal at a time, not the scoreboard.

sportsmanship.rule: Shake hands before/after match; no aggressive arguing with referee
Help opponent up if they fall (optional but good spirit)
No diving (simulation) – fair play

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local club (US Youth Soccer, FA affiliated, or recreational adult league). Many have beginner teams.
Join a recreational league or casual pickup games (meetup.com, local parks).
Buy basic equipment: boots, shin guards, socks, shorts, jersey (approx $50‑150).
Attend tryouts or open training sessions. Most clubs have multiple ability levels.
For competitive, join a travel team or academy after 1‑2 years of recreational play.
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Club fees: $50‑500/season (recreational to travel); equipment: $100‑300; league registration: $20‑100.

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Work rate – runs off the ball, tracks back defensively
Decision‑making – chooses correct pass/shot/dribble
Communication – directs teammates, calls for ball
Resilience – recovers from mistakes, doesn't give up
Coachability – listens, adapts to tactical instructions

attacking.strategy: Short passes, maintain control, tire opponent, create openings through triangles.
Quick transitions, long ball to fast forwards, exploit space behind defence.
Overlap full‑backs, cross into box, target tall striker.
Split defence with diagonal pass for striker to run onto.

defensive.strategy: Apply pressure on opponent's back line to force errors.
Defend deep, compact shape, deny space behind, absorb pressure, then counter.
Defenders step up together to catch attacker offside.

setPieces.strategy: Near post flick‑on, far post header, short corner to retain possession.
Curled around wall, power shot under/over wall, or layoff for second striker.
Long throw into penalty area (if player has strength), or short pass to retain.

specialSituations.situation: Leading by 1 goal with 10 minutes left – defend compact, waste time (goal kicks, throw‑ins), counter‑attack.
Trailing by 1 goal – increase tempo, push defenders forward, use attacking substitutes, risk offside trap.
Red card (player sent off) – sacrifice forward, reorganise to 4‑4‑1 or 5‑3‑1, defend in numbers.

terminologies.term: Pitch – playing field
Goal – scored when ball fully crosses goal line between posts and under crossbar
Offside – attacker in illegal position when ball played (see rule)
Foul – illegal contact or action (free kick awarded)
Yellow card – caution (two = red card)
Red card – sending off (team plays with 10 players)
Direct free kick – can score directly
Indirect free kick – must touch another player before goal
Penalty kick – from 11m spot, only goalkeeper
Handball – deliberate contact of ball with hand/arm (except goalkeeper in area)
Throw‑in – when ball fully crosses touchline; taken with two hands overhead
Goal kick – when ball last touched by attacker crosses goal line; taken from goal area
Corner kick – when ball last touched by defender crosses goal line; taken from corner arc
Dribbling – running while controlling ball with feet
Pass – kicking ball to teammate
Tackle – attempt to take ball from opponent (must play ball first)
Header – striking ball with head
Volley – kicking ball before it bounces
Nutmeg – passing ball through opponent's legs
Stepover – feinting by moving foot around ball
Rabona – kicking ball with leg crossed behind standing leg
Panenka – chipped penalty down the middle
VAR – Video Assistant Referee
DOGSO – Denying Obvious Goal Scoring Opportunity (red card offence)
Added time – extra minutes at end of half for stoppages
Extra time – 30 minutes additional in knockout matches
Penalty shootout – tiebreaker after extra time, alternating kicks
FIFA – Fédération Internationale de Football Association
UEFA – Union of European Football Associations

layout.zones: Infield (30‑yard circle), outfield, pitch (central strip), batting crease (1.22m in front of stumps), bowling crease (2.64m long), popping crease
layout.lines: Popping crease, bowling crease, return crease (for bowler's front foot)

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Grass pitch (rolled, compacted), outfield grass (short), synthetic pitches for some levels
courtFieldImage.venues: Outdoor oval (grass), floodlit stadiums for day‑night matches, indoor cricket centres (for practice)

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (running between wickets, fielding)
Builds explosive power (batting, bowling, throwing)
Enhances hand‑eye coordination (batting, catching)
Strengthens legs, core, shoulders, and forearms
Burns 300‑600 calories per hour (depending on role and intensity)

mental.benefit: Develops concentration and patience (long formats)
Builds decision‑making under pressure (run vs not run, shot selection)
Reduces stress through team camaraderie and outdoor activity

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles, torso twists, walking lunges, leg swings, ankle rotations (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), throwing drills (10 catches, 10 throws at stumps), batting shadow practice (20 shots), bowling run‑up (5‑10 reps)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Static stretches: hamstring, quad, hip flexor, lower back, shoulder, forearm (30 sec each); hydration and light walk (5 min)

basicRules.rule: Two teams of 11 players each. Batting team tries to score runs; bowling/fielding team tries to dismiss batters and limit runs.
Each match has a set number of overs (6 legal deliveries per over).
Batter scores runs by hitting ball and running between wickets (1,2,3 runs) or hitting boundary (4 runs if ball touches ground before boundary, 6 runs if airborne).
Dismissals: bowled, caught, LBW (leg before wicket), run out, stumped, hit wicket, handled ball, obstructing the field, timed out.
No ball (overstepping, above waist full toss) gives 1 extra run and a free hit (in limited overs). Wide ball gives 1 extra run.

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: 11 per team (on field). Substitute fielders allowed only with permission (cannot bowl, bat, or keep wicket).

matchTimings.test: 5 days, minimum 90 overs per day (approx 6‑7 hours play per day)

breaksIntervals.drinksBreak: After every hour (5 minutes) – optional in hot conditions
breaksIntervals.tea: 20 minutes (Test – between second and third sessions)
breaksIntervals.injuryTimeout: Up to 10 minutes for medical assessment; substitute fielder allowed temporarily

formats.shortFormat: T20 (20 overs), The Hundred (100 balls), T10 (10 overs)
formats.longFormat: Test (5 days, unlimited overs), First class (3‑4 days)

substitutesAndRoles.substitution: No direct substitution; concussion substitute allowed (like‑for‑like) in internationals since 2019. Otherwise, substitute fielders only.

roles.role: Batsman (specialist) – top order, middle order, lower order
Bowler (pace or spin) – opening, middle overs, death overs

scoringSystem.runs: 1,2,3,4,6 per ball. Extras: no ball (1+ free hit), wide (1), leg bye (runs if ball hits body), bye (runs without bat contact).
scoringSystem.wickets: 10 wickets end innings. In limited overs, innings ends after maximum overs or 10 wickets.

rulesRegulations.howToWin: Team scoring more runs wins. If equal runs in limited overs, tie may be broken by Super Over (one over per side) or countback of boundaries.

penaltyRuns.fault: Ball hitting helmet on field – 5 penalty runs to batting side
Time wasting (bowling side) – 5 penalty runs after warning

codeOfConduct.offence: Dissent, excessive appealing, aggressive behaviour – warning, then fine, suspension of overs (bowler), or match ban

foulsAndPenalties.illegalBowlingAction: Chucking (elbow extension >15°) – bowler suspended, must undergo biomechanical testing

fairPlay.rule: Spirit of cricket – respect umpire decisions, no ball tampering, no fake fielding, walk if you know you are out (optional but valued)

judgementCriteria.onFieldUmpires: 2 umpires (one at bowler's end, one at square leg). Third umpire (TV replays) for run outs, stumpings, catches, boundaries, no balls.

rulesRegulations.decisionReviewSystem: Each team gets limited reviews (e.g., 2 per innings in Tests, 1 in ODIs) to challenge on‑field decisions using ball‑tracking, hotspot, snicko.

commonInjuries.injury: Hamstring strain (sudden sprinting between wickets, bowling follow‑through)
Lower back stress fractures (fast bowlers – lumbar spine)
Concussion (ball hit to head – batsman or fielder)
Knee injuries (meniscus, ACL from twisting while fielding)

preventionMethods.method: Fast bowlers: limit overs per spell (max 6‑8 in a day), maintain core strength, use proper run‑up and landing technique
Strengthen hamstrings and glutes (Nordic curls, deadlifts) to prevent strains
Wear protective gear (helmet with grille, thigh pad, chest guard, arm guard, gloves)
Warm‑up with gradual intensity – no sudden sprints
Cool‑down and stretching after play, especially for bowlers
Hydrate before and during (cramps common in hot weather)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, cough) – risk of collapse or spread
Uncontrolled hypertension (rapid sprinting spikes BP)
Recent concussion (no play for at least 7‑14 days)
Severe asthma without rescue inhaler (exercise‑induced)
Active back pain with sciatica (bowling aggravates)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have heart arrhythmia, epilepsy, or history of stress fractures

commonMistakes.mistake: Batting: playing away from body (edges), not watching ball onto bat, poor footwork (stuck in crease)
Bowling: front foot no‑ball (overstepping), illegal action (throwing), inconsistent length
Fielding: fumbling easy catches, poor throwing aim, not backing up throws (overthrows)
Running between wickets: hesitation, calling for run when ball close to fielder
Wicket‑keeping: standing up to fast bowlers (dangerous), poor glove position, not anticipating edges

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (especially in hot weather)
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 150‑200 ml every drinks break (approx every hour) or between overs; use electrolyte drinks if sweating heavily
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost; drink water or sports drink; monitor urine colour (pale yellow = hydrated)
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Caffeine (diuretic, may cause jitters), carbonated drinks (bloating), alcohol (dehydrating)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dry mouth, dark urine, headache – stop play, inform umpire, hydrate immediately

nutritionTips.preMatch: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (pasta, rice, chapati, oatmeal) + lean protein (chicken, fish, eggs) – avoid heavy fats and excessive fibre (GI discomfort)
nutritionTips.preMatchSnack: 30‑60 min before: banana, energy bar, or fruit smoothie (easily digestible)
nutritionTips.duringMatch: At lunch/tea: light meal (sandwich, pasta salad, fruit); avoid heavy fried food. In drinks breaks: small snacks (dried fruit, nuts, energy gels for fast bowlers)
nutritionTips.postMatch: Within 30 min: protein + carbs (chocolate milk, whey shake + banana, chicken + rice) for muscle repair and glycogen replenishment
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (spinach, red meat) for oxygen transport; calcium (dairy) for bone health; adequate protein (1.2‑1.6 g/kg body weight)

physicalBurnout.limit: Fast bowlers: maximum 30‑40 overs per week (adult club), 10‑15 overs per day in matches; batters and spinners can handle more
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days or active recovery (swimming, light cycling) per week
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, yoga, Pilates (core strength, flexibility, low impact)

mentalBurnout.signs: Lack of motivation, frustration after failures, avoiding practice, irritability with teammates
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, focus on technique without match pressure, set small goals (e.g., improve defensive stroke), vary practice (net sessions, fielding drills)

equipment.bat: Willow (English or Kashmir), weight 1.1‑1.4 kg (adult). Size: short handle (83‑86 cm), long handle (88‑91 cm).
equipment.ball: Leather (red for Tests, white for limited overs, pink for day‑night Tests). Circumference 22.4‑22.9 cm, weight 155.9‑163 g (men).

protectiveGear.item: Helmet with grille (mandatory for batsmen vs fast bowling)
Thigh guard, chest guard, arm guard (optional but recommended)
Batting gloves (with foam or cotton padding)
Pads (leg guards) – for batsmen and wicket‑keeper

equipment.wicketKeeping: Gloves (webbed, padded), pads (lighter than batting), helmet (for standing up).
equipment.footwear: Spiked shoes (studs or rubber spikes) for grip on grass; flat‑soled for artificial pitches.
equipment.apparel: White or coloured clothing (depending on format), sun hat or cap, sunglasses (polarised recommended).
equipment.accessories: Batting grip, bat tape, ball polisher (for red ball – saliva/sweat, now banned; replaced by artificial substance), team kit bag.

protective.item: Helmet with grille (BSI or equivalent standard) – essential for facing fast bowling
Abdominal guard (box) – for all male players, recommended for females as well
Batting pads (cover knee and shin)
Gloves (prevent finger fractures)
For wicket‑keepers: helmet, chest guard (optional), padded inner gloves
For close fielders (short leg, silly point): helmet and abdominal guard recommended

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, splint (for finger fracture), antiseptic spray, instant cold packs, eye wash (for dirt in eye)

coordination.desc: Hand‑eye coordination for batting (timing, placement) and catching
coordination.drill: Throwdowns from coach (batting reaction); slip catching practice (20 catches per session)

speedAgility.desc: Quick running between wickets, lateral movement in fielding, explosive bowling run‑up
speedAgility.drill: Suicide runs (running between creases), cone drills (fielding reaction), sprint repeats (20m x 6)

focusReaction.desc: React to ball delivery (0.4‑0.6 sec for fast bowling) or sharp catches
focusReaction.drill: Batting against bowling machine at variable speed; reaction ball drills (catching unpredictable bounce)

strategy.batting: Build partnership, rotate strike, attack loose balls, protect tail‑enders

xFactors.bowling:
Set field, bowl to plan (e.g., outside off stump), change pace, use variations (slower ball, yorker, bouncer) Place fielders based on batter's strengths, cut off singles, create run‑out chances
Read pitch condition (seam, spin, bounce), assess batter's weaknesses, adjust line/length accordingly When to take a risky run, when to defend, when to attack, when to review umpire decision (DRS)

dailyPlan.day: Net batting (60‑80 balls) – focus on defence + 20 throwdowns (driving) + running between wickets (10 x 2 runs)
Footwork drills (20 min), facing spin bowling (30 balls), conditioning (sprints, core)
Rest or light fielding practice (catches, throws) + swimming
Net batting (different bowlers: pace and spin) + shot selection drill (identify scoring zones)
Scenario practice (e.g., need 30 runs in 3 overs) + video analysis
Match simulation (20‑30 overs) + cool‑down
Active recovery (yoga, light stretching) or complete rest
Run‑up work (10‑15 reps) + bowling 8 overs (focus on line/length) + strength training (deadlifts, lunges)
Skills: variations (slower ball, yorker) – 6 overs + fielding drills
Rest or light swimming (active recovery)
Bowling with field placements (tactical) – 8 overs + conditioning (sprints)
Net practice against batters (match intensity) – 6 overs + video analysis
Match simulation + cool‑down stretching
Complete rest

drillsForBeginners.drill: Coach throws underarm; batter presses forward, bat straight – 20 reps
Mark run‑up, practice no‑ball check (back foot behind crease) – 10 reps
Coach hits high catches; fielder positions under ball, soft hands – 20 reps
From 20m, hit single stump – 20 throws each side

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master grip, stance, basic forward/back defence, bowling action, catching technique – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Introduce attacking shots (drive, cut, pull), bowling variations (outswing, inswing, off‑spin), fielding positions – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Develop match awareness, scenario training, running between wickets, bowling to field – 4‑5x per week + local matches
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine technique, video analysis, mental conditioning, maintain 4x/week with match play

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 3 sec, exhale 5 sec – before facing each delivery (batter) or starting run‑up (bowler)
Close eyes, imagine playing a perfect cover drive or bowling a perfect yorker – 2 min before practice/match
Batter: tap bat, look around, deep breath, then ready. Bowler: same approach marks, same number of breaths.

pressureHandling.tip: Batting in last over of T20: focus on ball, not required runs; hit to gaps, run hard.
Bowling last over: trust your yorker; don't try too many variations.
After dropping a catch: reset, apologise to bowler, focus on next ball – don't dwell.
Use self‑talk: “One ball at a time, I can do this.”

sportsmanship.rule: Applaud opponent's good performance (century, five‑wicket haul)
Walk if you know you are out (even if umpire gives not out – optional but respected)
No appealing after umpire says not out; no aggressive send‑offs

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local cricket club (ECB, USA Cricket, or national body directory). Many have junior or adult beginner sections.
Attend introductory sessions (often free) – club provides basic equipment (bat, pads, helmet).
Buy personal equipment: bat (beginner grade), gloves, box, pads, spikes (approx $100‑200).
Join a low‑competitive league (e.g., social Sunday league) or indoor cricket for winter.
After 1‑2 seasons, consider coaching courses or try for club higher teams.
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Club membership: $50‑200/year; equipment: $150‑400; match fees: $5‑20 per game.

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Discipline – practices regularly, follows team rules
Patience – Test batting for hours, bowling dot balls
Team player – puts team before personal stats
Resilience – bounces back from duck or expensive over
Adaptability – adjusts technique to pitch/conditions

batting.strategy: Build partnerships, leave balls outside off, tire bowlers, score at 3‑4 runs per over, protect wicket.
Rotate strike (take singles), accelerate in last 10 overs (powerplay), maintain 5‑6 runs per over.
Attack from start, target boundaries, use scoop/reverse sweep, high strike rate (>140).

bowling.strategy: Swing, bowl full, attack top of off stump, try to take early wickets.
Bowling tight line, flight, vary pace, build pressure (dot balls).
Yorkers, slower balls, wide yorkers, avoid full tosses and short balls.

fielding.strategy: Close catchers (slip, gully, short leg) – for wickets.
Boundary riders – to save runs, usually in last overs.

specialSituations.situation: DRS review: only if clear error (snicko/hotspot shows edge, ball‑tracking shows hitting stumps).
Rain‑reduced match: adjust target via Duckworth‑Lewis‑Stern (DLS) method.
Super Over: send your best batter and bowler; bat first if possible (set target).

terminologies.term: Over – 6 legal deliveries bowled from one end
Maiden over – over with zero runs (no extras)
LBW – leg before wicket (batter out if ball would hit stumps and impacts in line)
No ball – illegal delivery (overstepping, above waist full toss) – 1 extra run, free hit next ball
Wide – ball too far from batter to hit – 1 extra run
Bye – runs scored when ball does not hit bat or body
Leg bye – runs off body (not bat) – only if batter attempted shot
Powerplay – fielding restrictions (e.g., only 2 fielders outside 30‑yard circle in first 10 overs of ODI)
DRS – Decision Review System (umpire review using technology)
Snicko – ultra‑edge (sound analysis for edges)
Hotspot – thermal imaging for ball contact
Ball‑tracking – predicts path of ball for LBW decisions
Duck – zero runs by a batter
Golden duck – out on first ball faced
Century – 100 runs by a batter
Five‑wicket haul – 5 wickets by a bowler in an innings
Hat‑trick – 3 wickets in 3 consecutive balls
Strike rate – runs per 100 balls (batting) or balls per wicket (bowling)
Economy rate – runs per over conceded by bowler
Yorker – full delivery aimed at batsman's toes
Bouncer – short delivery aimed at head (limited to 1 per over in limited overs)
Slower ball – off‑cutter, slower ball bouncer (to deceive batter)
Googly – leg‑spinner's delivery that turns opposite direction
Doosra – off‑spinner's delivery that turns away from right‑hander (controversial)
Carrom ball – spin delivered by flicking with middle finger
Silly point – fielding position very close to batter
Slip – catching position behind wicket (1st, 2nd, 3rd slip)
Deep square leg – boundary fielder for hook/pull shots
ICC – International Cricket Council
ECB – England and Wales Cricket Board
BCCI – Board of Control for Cricket in India

layout.trackZones: Start lines (staggered for 200m, 400m), finish line (common), exchange zones for relays (20m each)
layout.fieldEventAreas: Inside the track: infield for throws, jumps; outside: javelin runway, high jump apron

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Polyurethane or tartan track (synthetic), grass infield (or synthetic for throws), concrete or synthetic runways
courtFieldImage.venues: Outdoor stadium with 400m track, or indoor 200m track (banked)

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (distance running) and explosive power (sprints, jumps)
Strengthens lower body: quads, hamstrings, glutes, calves; core for stability
Burns 400‑1000 calories per hour (depending on intensity and event)
Increases bone density (impact events like sprints, jumps)
Enhances speed, agility, and coordination

mental.benefit: Builds discipline, goal‑setting, and resilience
Reduces stress through rhythmic running or focused throwing
Boosts confidence through measurable improvements (time, distance)

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: leg swings, walking lunges, high knees, butt kicks, arm circles, ankle rotations (10‑15 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (10 min), strides (4‑6 x 50m at 70% effort), event‑specific drills (e.g., hurdle walks, shot put medicine ball throws)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Light jogging (5‑10 min), static stretches (hamstring, quad, calf, hip flexor, lower back, 30 sec each), foam rolling if available

basicRules.rule: Track events: athletes compete by running specified distance; first to finish (or fastest time) wins.
False start: one false start allowed in some rules, second leads to disqualification (World Athletics: one false start = DQ for that athlete).
Lane discipline: sprints (up to 400m) run in assigned lanes; middle/long distance (800m+) may break after first bend.
Field events: each athlete gets 3‑6 attempts; best valid mark wins.
Relays: baton must be passed within 20m exchange zone; dropping baton = disqualification if not retrieved correctly.

matchTimings.fieldEvents: Each attempt takes 30‑60 sec; whole competition 1‑2 hours per event

breaksIntervals.betweenAttemptsField: Each athlete has 30‑60 sec between attempts (rotating order)
breaksIntervals.halftime: None, but distance races have water stations every 2‑3 km
breaksIntervals.injuryTimeout: Up to 15 minutes (medical evaluation) – if cannot continue, athlete withdraws

formats.field: Qualification round (top 12 advance to final), then final (3‑6 attempts)

substitutesAndRoles.substitution:
In relays, substitute can replace a team member before the race (not during). In individual events, no substitution.
Sprinter – explosive start, maintain top speed Distance runner – pacing, kick (final sprint) Thrower – technique, strength, release angle Jumper – approach speed, takeoff technique, landing

scoringSystem.track: Place points (e.g., 1st=8, 2nd=6, etc. in championships) – fastest time wins in finals Longest distance (throws, jumps) or highest height (high jump, pole vault) wins Points based on performance tables; highest total points wins

rulesRegulations.howToWin: In track: first across finish line in finals; in field: best valid mark after all attempts; in combined events: highest total points.

trackFouls.fault: False start – disqualification (after one illegal start in World Athletics rules)
Lane infringement (running outside lane, stepping on line) – disqualification
Obstruction – causing another athlete to lose balance – disqualification
Exchange zone violation in relay – baton passed outside 20m zone – disqualification

fieldFouls.fault: Shot put/discus/hammer/javelin: foot fault (touching top or outside circle/runway) – no mark
Javelin: tip does not land first, or thrower crosses foul line after throw – no mark
Long/triple jump: foot over takeoff board – no mark; landing outside pit – no mark
High jump/pole vault: knocking off bar with body or pole – failed attempt

foulsAndPenalties.penaltyUnsportsmanlike: Warning, disqualification, or ban (e.g., false start cheating, doping)

fairPlay.rule: No deliberate interference (blocking, pushing)
In distance races, athletes who are lapped must move to outer lanes
Respect officials' decisions; no arguing

judgementCriteria.officials: Starter, finish judges (photo finish), track referees, field judges, chief judge
judgementCriteria.electronicTiming: Photo finish (accuracy 1/1000 sec), wind gauge (for record eligibility)
judgementCriteria.videoReview: Used for false starts, exchange zones, and field event fouls

commonInjuries.injury: Hamstring strain (sprinting, especially in acceleration phase)
Shin splints (medial tibial stress syndrome – beginners, hard surfaces)
Runner's knee (patellofemoral pain – overuse, weak quads)
Plantar fasciitis (heel pain – overtraining, poor footwear)
Stress fractures (foot, tibia – distance runners, rapid mileage increase)

preventionMethods.method: Gradual mileage increase (no more than 10% per week) – for runners
Strength training: glutes, core, calves, hamstrings (squats, lunges, deadlifts, calf raises)
