Proper footwear: replace running shoes every 300‑500 miles; use spikes only on track
Dynamic warm‑up and cool‑down with static stretching
Cross‑train (swimming, cycling) to reduce impact
Technique drills: A‑skips, B‑skips, butt kicks (improve form, reduce overstriding)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, productive cough) – risk of myocarditis, dehydration
Uncontrolled hypertension (blood pressure spikes during sprint or heavy throws)
Recent concussion (within 2‑4 weeks) – balance and coordination impaired for jumps/hurdles
Severe asthma without pre‑exercise medication (cold weather or exertion trigger)
Active joint inflammation (arthritis flare‑up in knees/ankles)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of heart arrhythmia, epilepsy, or severe anemia

commonMistakes.mistake: Starting too fast in distance races (positive split) – leads to fatigue and slower finish
Overstriding (reaching foot too far forward) – braking effect, increases injury risk
Looking back at opponents (slows you down, breaks form)
Incorrect arm swing (crossing body midline) – wastes energy, disrupts balance
Poor breathing rhythm (erratic or holding breath) – reduces oxygen delivery
Field events: rushing the approach (long jump) or release (throws) without proper setup
Not practicing exchange zone in relays (dropped baton or illegal pass)

hydrationImportance.beforeEvent: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (for events >800m, more important)
hydrationImportance.duringEvent: Sprints (<400m): no need; distance (800m+): sip 100‑150 ml at water stations every 2‑3 km or between heats
hydrationImportance.afterEvent: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink if heavy sweating (marathon, hot weather)
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Excessive caffeine (diuretic, may cause GI distress), carbonated drinks (bloating)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dark urine, headache – stop, hydrate, seek medical if severe

nutritionTips.preEvent: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (pasta, rice, oatmeal) + moderate protein (chicken, egg) – low fat and fibre (to avoid GI issues)
nutritionTips.preEventSnack: 30‑60 min before: banana, energy gel, or white bread with jam (sprints: simple carbs; distance: more carbs)
nutritionTips.duringEvent: Energy gels or chews every 30‑45 min, plus water/sports drink
nutritionTips.postEvent: Within 30 min: protein + carbs (chocolate milk, whey + banana, or chicken + rice) for muscle repair and glycogen replenishment
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (red meat, spinach) for oxygen transport; calcium (dairy) for bone health; adequate protein (1.2‑1.7g/kg body weight)

physicalBurnout.limit: Sprinters: 3‑4 intensive sessions/week; distance runners: 5‑6 days but include easy days
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 1‑2 full rest days or active recovery (light walking, swimming)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Cycling, swimming, elliptical (low impact, maintains fitness)

mentalBurnout.signs: Lack of motivation, dreading practice, plateauing performance, irritability
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks low‑intensity training, set new goals (e.g., technique focus), vary workouts (fartlek, trail runs)

running.shoes: Running flats (sprints), spikes (track events), distance trainers (road/marathon), trail shoes (off‑road)
running.spikes: Pin length: 6‑9mm for synthetic track, 12‑15mm for grass
running.uniform: Lightweight singlet and shorts (tight‑fitting for sprints, loose for distance)

fieldEvents.throws: Shot put (4‑7.26kg), discus (1‑2kg), javelin (600‑800g), hammer (4‑7.26kg) – implement weight by age/gender
fieldEvents.jumps: Takeoff board (long/triple), landing pits (sand or foam), high jump/pole vault mats (thick foam)

accessories.item: Starting blocks (sprints up to 400m), baton (relay), number bib, timing chip (on shoe)
For distance: hydration belt, watch/GPS, arm band for phone

protective.item: Track spikes only on track – not on roads (dangerous)
For throws: cage (discus, hammer) – mandatory for safety of others
Javelin: keep tips down when walking; never throw backwards
High jump/pole vault: adequate landing mats (60‑80cm thick)
Sunscreen, hat, sunglasses (outdoor events)

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, blister plasters, instant cold packs, emergency blanket (for marathon)

coordination.desc: Arm‑leg synchronisation (running), takeoff timing (jumps), release angle (throws)
coordination.drill: High knees, butt kicks, A‑skip, B‑skip (running form) – 10 min daily

speedAgility.desc: Acceleration (sprints), change of pace (distance), quick steps (hurdles)
speedAgility.drill: Sprint drills: falling starts, flying 30m, accelerations; agility ladder (lateral, in‑out)

focusReaction.desc: Reaction to starting gun (0.1‑0.2 sec elite), maintaining concentration in field events
focusReaction.drill: Reaction drills: clap then sprint 10m, or light signal with random delay

strategy.sprint: Block start, drive phase, upright running, lean at finish

xFactors.distance:
Negative split (second half faster), drafting, kick (final sprint) Baton passing (upsweep, downsweep), visual or non‑visual exchange
Read opponents' moves (e.g., in 800m, position behind leader to draft, then kick) When to break from pack, when to push the pace, when to hold back (tactical races)

dailyPlan.day: Acceleration drills (30m from blocks, 8 reps) + Plyometrics (box jumps, 3x10) + Strength (squats, deadlifts)
Speed endurance (150m x 4, 80% effort) + Hurdle drills (if applicable) + Core work
Recovery (light jogging 20 min, stretching) + Technique drills (A‑skip, B‑skip)
Starts and blocks (10 x 20m) + Flying 30m (5 reps) + Weight room (power cleans)
Race simulation (200m or 400m time trial) + Video analysis
Long run (for 400m specialists) or rest (for 100m) – active recovery
Complete rest or light stretching
Easy run (5‑8 km) + Drills (high knees, strides)
Tempo run (20‑30 min at threshold pace) + Core
Recovery run (5 km very easy) + stretching
Intervals (e.g., 800m x 5 at 5K pace) + Cool‑down
Easy run (5‑6 km) + strength (bodyweight)
Long run (10‑20 km depending on event) + strides
Rest or cross‑train (swimming, cycling)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Run in place with high knees, then transition to slow jog (focus on arm swing, foot strike)
4 x 50m at 70% effort, walk back recovery (learn controlled speed)
Practice takeoff and landing (soft knees) – 5 reps
Medicine ball (2‑3 kg) to learn pushing motion

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Build base endurance (running 3‑4 days/week) + basic running form drills – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add interval training, strength training (2x/week), introduce field event technique – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Event‑specific workouts (e.g., block starts for sprints, longer intervals for distance), competition simulation – 5x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Maintain with periodised plan (base, build, peak, recovery), enter local meets

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale for 2 sec, exhale for 2 sec (sprint start); distance: rhythmic breathing (e.g., 2:2 pattern)
Close eyes, imagine perfect race (start, stride, finish) or throw (approach, release, landing) – 3 min before event
Same warm‑up, same number of strides, same mental checklist (e.g., "relax shoulders, pump arms, high knees")

pressureHandling.tip: In final 100m, focus on arm drive and form, not the finish line (prevents premature deceleration)
If you false start, reset mentally – forget it, treat as new start
Use self‑talk: “I’ve done the training, now execute”
After a poor throw/jump, take a deep breath, step back, re‑visualise, then attempt again

sportsmanship.rule: Shake hands with competitors after race (especially distance events)
Congratulate winner, even if you lost by a small margin
No taunting or celebrating excessively on track

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local athletics club (USATF, England Athletics, or masters club for adults)
Join beginner group (youth or adult). Most clubs offer introductory sessions
Buy basic equipment: running shoes, shorts, singlet, spikes (after 3 months)
For field events, club usually provides implements (shot, discus) for beginners
Compete in local open meets (unattached or club) after 6 months of practice
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Club fees: $30‑100/year; spikes: $50‑150; competition entry: $10‑30 per meet

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Discipline – follows training plan, shows up consistently
Patience – understands that progress takes months/years
Resilience – bounces back from injury or bad race
Coachability – listens to feedback, makes adjustments
Race intelligence – knows when to push, when to hold back

sprint.strategy: Set blocks correctly (front pedal 1.5‑2 shoe lengths behind line, back pedal 1.5‑2 behind front). Drive low for first 10‑15m.
Upsweep pass (receiver holds hand low, passer sweeps upward) or downsweep. Practice non‑visual (call “stick”)

middleDistance.strategy: Stay behind leader, draft, then sprint final 200‑300m
Take early lead, run even pace, force others to chase

longDistance.strategy: Run second half faster than first (requires discipline)
Unexpected acceleration to break pack, then settle into faster pace

fieldEvents.strategy: First attempt safe (guarantee mark), then build up to maximum effort
In javelin, throw against wind (increases lift), in discus, throw with crosswind (better stability)

specialSituations.situation: Headwind in sprint – lean slightly more, pump arms harder, shorten stride
Hot weather marathon – drink early, use sponges, reduce pace by 5‑10 sec/km

terminologies.term: Staggered start – lanes start at different points to equalise distance (200m, 400m)
Photo finish – camera system determining winner when times are identical to 1/1000 sec
False start – leaving blocks before starting signal (DQ after one violation in World Athletics)
Exchange zone – 20m zone for baton pass (10m pre‑zone plus 10m post‑zone in some rules)
Baton – hollow aluminium tube (28‑30cm, 50g) passed in relays
Drive phase – initial acceleration from blocks (head low, powerful strides)
Stride length – distance between foot contacts (elite sprinters: 2.2‑2.5m)
Cadence – steps per minute (sprinters: 4‑5 steps/sec; distance: 180‑200 steps/min)
Kick – final sprint at end of distance race
Drafting – running directly behind another runner to reduce air resistance (legal)
Negative split – second half of race faster than first
Positive split – first half faster than second (often results in slower overall time)
Fartlek – unstructured speed play (alternating fast and slow running)
Tempo run – steady run at “comfortably hard” pace (threshold training)
VO2max – maximal oxygen uptake (measure of aerobic capacity)
Lactate threshold – intensity where lactate accumulates rapidly (training improves)
Plyometrics – explosive jumps (box jumps, depth jumps) for power
Fosbury flop – high jump technique (arching back over bar)
Hitch kick – long jump technique (cycling legs in air)
Ortodromic step – triple jump phase (hop, step, jump)
Glide vs rotational – shot put techniques (linear vs spin)
Foul (field) – invalid attempt due to rule violation (no mark)
Mark – valid measured attempt (field event)
World Athletics – international governing body (formerly IAAF)
USATF – USA Track & Field

dimensions.finishArea: At least 100m beyond finish line for deceleration

layout.lanes: Numbered buoys (red for lane 1, orange 2, yellow 3, green 4, blue 5, white 6, pink 7, turquoise 8)

courtFieldImage.zones: Start pontoons, alignment towers, 500m markers, 1000m halfway, 1500m marker, finish tower
courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Flat water (lake, reservoir, or sheltered river) – no waves or strong current
courtFieldImage.venues: Artificial rowing lakes, natural lakes, or wide rivers with calm conditions

physical.benefit: Full‑body workout: legs (quads, hamstrings, glutes), back (lats, rhomboids), arms (biceps, forearms), core
Improves cardiovascular endurance (VO2max) – rowing uses 70‑80% muscle mass
Burns 600‑900 calories per hour (high intensity on water or ergometer)
Low impact on joints (non‑weight‑bearing, unlike running)
Enhances posture and core stability

mental.benefit: Reduces stress through rhythmic motion and outdoor setting
Builds discipline, teamwork (sweep rowing in crew boats), and focus
Improves mental resilience (pushing through pain during race)

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles, trunk twists, leg swings, hip rotations, cat‑camel (spine), wrist circles (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light ergometer rowing (5‑10 min at low intensity), dynamic stretches specific to rowing (leg drive simulation, body swing), paddling on water (5 min easy strokes)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Light rowing (5 min easy pressure), static stretches: hamstring, quad, hip flexor, lower back, latissimus, chest, triceps (30 sec each), foam rolling for back and legs

basicRules.rule: Boats race in assigned lanes over 2000m (junior/senior) or 1000m (masters). First across finish line wins.
Boat classes: single scull (1x), double scull (2x), quadruple scull (4x), pair (2‑), four (4‑), eight (8+). Sculling: two oars per rower. Sweep: one oar per rower (coxswain optional).
Starting sequence: “Attention” then “Go” (or horn). False start: one warning, then disqualification.
Lane discipline: must stay in own lane; if a boat drifts into another lane and causes interference, it may be disqualified.
Balloting: lanes assigned based on previous performance (fastest in centre lanes).

rulesRegulations.matchTimings: 2000m: elite men approx 5:20‑5:50, elite women 6:00‑6:40; masters (older) 6:30‑8:00; juniors similar to elite.

breaksIntervals.injuryTimeout: Boat may stop, but race continues; medical boat retrieves injured rower

formats.shortFormat: 500m sprints (indoor rowing, or on water for masters)
formats.longFormat: 2000m (standard Olympic), 6000m (head races – time trial, not side‑by‑side)

substitutesAndRoles.substitution: In team boats (eights, fours), a substitute rower may replace an ill/injured rower before the race (not during). Coxswain can be substituted similarly.

roles.role: Bow (seat 1) – steers (in sweep boats without cox), sets rhythm, calls moves
Stroke (seat 8 or last) – sets stroke rate and rhythm for entire boat
Coxswain (cox) – steers, calls race plan, motivates, navigates (mandatory in eights, optional in fours)

rulesRegulations.scoringSystem: Time‑based (fastest time wins). In head races (time trials), lowest time wins. In regattas, winner of each heat/progression advances.
rulesRegulations.howToWin: Cross finish line first (side‑by‑side) or have fastest time (head race).

foulsAndPenalties.fault: Lane infringement – causing interference to another boat: may be disqualified or relegated
Unsportsmanlike conduct – verbal abuse, dangerous steering – disqualification
Equipment violation – incorrect weight of coxswain (minimum 55kg for men, 50kg for women) – disqualification if underweight without added ballast

fairPlay.rule: Respect other crews, do not deliberately wash (wake) another boat
If you cause interference, stop racing and allow the affected boat to pass (sportsmanship)

judgementCriteria.officials: Starter, umpires (follow in launch), finish judges (photo finish), alignment officials
judgementCriteria.photoFinish: Camera at finish line determines winner to 1/1000 second
judgementCriteria.videoReview: Used for lane infringement and false starts (FISA events)

commonInjuries.injury: Lower back pain (most common – from poor posture or overuse)
Rib stress fractures (from repetitive loading, especially in elite sweep rowers)
Knee pain (patellar tendinitis from leg drive)
Blistering on hands (from oar handle friction)

preventionMethods.method: Proper rowing technique – maintain neutral spine, use legs first, then back, then arms (reverse on recovery)
Core strengthening (planks, bird‑dog, dead bugs) – 3x per week
Gradual volume increase (no more than 10% per week on ergometer)
Use rowing gloves or tape to prevent blisters
Warm‑up including cat‑camel stretches for spine mobility
Cross‑train (swimming, cycling) to reduce repetitive loading

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute lower back pain (herniated disc, sciatica) – rowing aggravates
Uncontrolled hypertension (leg drive spikes blood pressure)
Recent rib fracture or stress fracture – high risk of recurrence
Severe asthma without pre‑exercise medication (cold air on water triggers)
Active hand blisters with infection (risk of sepsis)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of heart disease, back surgery, or chronic wrist tendinitis

commonMistakes.mistake: Rushing the slide (recovery) – loses boat run, disrupts rhythm
Opening back too early (using back before legs are fully extended) – reduces power, strains back
Skying oar (raising blade too high during recovery) – slows boat, causes imbalance
Dropping hands at finish – slows boat, makes feathering difficult
Looking down or around – breaks body alignment, slows boat
Gripping oar too tightly – causes forearm fatigue, blisters

hydrationImportance.beforeRace: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (longer races >2000m)
hydrationImportance.duringRace: Not possible during 2000m (5‑7 min); for head races (>20 min), drink from water bottle attached to boat (use straw)
hydrationImportance.afterRace: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink if heavy sweating (hot weather)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dark urine, reduced performance – stop racing (if safe), hydrate, inform coach

nutritionTips.preRace: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (pasta, rice, oatmeal, bagel) + moderate protein (egg, yogurt) – low fat and fibre (avoid GI distress)
nutritionTips.preRaceSnack: 30‑60 min before: banana, energy bar, or rice cake with honey
nutritionTips.duringRace: 30 min)>Energy gel or small water bottle with electrolyte (using straw system)
nutritionTips.postRace: Within 30 min: protein + carbs (chocolate milk, whey shake + banana, chicken + rice) for muscle repair and glycogen replenishment
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (red meat, spinach) for oxygen transport; calcium (dairy) for bone density; adequate protein (1.5‑1.8 g/kg body weight) due to high muscle demand

physicalBurnout.limit: High intensity on‑water sessions: max 5‑6 per week (including ergometer). Limit ergometer sessions to 3‑4 per week to reduce back strain.
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 1‑2 full rest days or active recovery (swimming, light cycling) per week
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, yoga, Pilates (core and flexibility, low impact)

mentalBurnout.signs: Lack of motivation, dreading water sessions, poor performance on ergometer, irritability
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks low intensity (technical rowing only, no racing), set small goals (e.g., improve catch technique), vary training (river rowing instead of lake)

equipment.boat: Carbon fibre or fibreglass hull. Types: single scull (1x), double scull (2x), quad (4x), pair (2‑), four (4‑), eight (8+).
equipment.oars: Sculling oars (each rower holds two) – length 2.9‑3.0m. Sweep oars (each rower holds one) – length 3.7‑3.9m. Carbon fibre shaft, plastic blade.
equipment.rigger: Metal frame attached to boat holding oarlocks (swivels).
equipment.seat: Sliding seat on wheels (runs on tracks).
equipment.coxBox: Speaker and microphone system for coxswain (in eights and coxed fours).
equipment.footstretcher: Adjustable footplate with shoes attached (shoes bolted in).
equipment.apparel: Unisuit (one‑piece lycra), or separate top/shorts. Tight‑fitting to reduce drag.
equipment.accessories: Speed coach (GPS stroke rate monitor), water bottle holder, boat rack, slings, oar bag.

protective.item: Life jacket (PFD) – mandatory for beginners and in cold water, optional for competitive rowers (but recommended)
Whistle – attached to life jacket or boat (for emergencies)
Heel ties – straps to release feet from shoes if boat capsizes (mandatory)
Buoyancy bags – fixed inside bow and stern to prevent sinking
Rowing gloves (optional – prevents blisters)
Sun protection (hat, sunglasses, sunscreen)

safetyGear.firstAid: Waterproof first aid kit (bandages, antiseptic, blister plasters, ice pack, emergency blanket) – carried in launch boat

coordination.desc: Synchronise leg drive, back swing, arm pull; in sweep boats, match blade timing with teammates
coordination.drill: Pick drill (add body parts sequentially: arms only, then arms + back, then full slide) – 10 min

speedAgility.desc: Quick catch at front end, explosive leg drive, fast hands away at finish
speedAgility.drill: Rate ladders: 20 strokes at 22 spm, 20 at 26 spm, 20 at 30 spm – build stroke rate control

focusReaction.desc: React to coxswain's calls, start signal, and other boats
focusReaction.drill: Starts: practice 10 starts from dead stop to full pressure (on water or erg)

strategy.racePlan: Start (power 10 strokes), settle (race pace), move (surge at 500m, 1000m, 1500m), sprint (last 250m)

xFactors.steering:
Coxswain uses rudder (or rower in bow) to keep straight line, avoid wash
Read water conditions (wind, current) to adjust steering and blade depth When to push (move) based on opponent's position, when to hold pace to conserve energy

dailyPlan.day: On water: steady state (6‑8 km at 18‑20 spm) + technique drills (pause at catch, square blades) (60 min)
Ergometer: intervals (8 x 500m, 2 min rest) + core strength (20 min)
On water: rate pieces (4 x 1000m at 28‑30 spm, 3 min rest) + starts practice (30 min)
Weights (squats, deadlifts, pull‑ups, rows) – 45 min + light erg (15 min)
On water: full race simulation (2000m at race pace) + video analysis (30 min)
Long steady row (12‑15 km at 18‑20 spm) + cool‑down
Active recovery (swimming, light cycling, stretching) or complete rest

drillsForBeginners.drill: At full compression, pause 1 sec, then drive legs – teaches proper catch position
Row with blades square (not feathered) – teaches blade control
Arms only, then arms + back, then full slide – builds coordination
Legs only, no back swing – learn leg drive

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Learn basic stroke sequence (catch, drive, finish, recovery), boat handling, safety – 3x per week on water + erg
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add rate work (22‑26 spm), square blade rowing, basic race starts – 4x per week + strength 2x
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce race pace (30‑34 spm), interval training, side‑by‑side practice – 5x per week + on‑water racing drills
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine technique with video analysis, race strategy, peak fitness – maintain 4‑5 sessions/week + competition

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale during recovery, exhale during drive – rhythmic breathing matching stroke rate
Close eyes, imagine perfect race (start, settle, move, sprint) – 3 min before race
Same warm‑up sequence, same number of practice strokes, same coxswain call cues

pressureHandling.tip: At start, focus on first stroke (strong leg drive), not the whole race
During a “move” (surge), count strokes (e.g., 10 power strokes) – gives focus
Use self‑talk: “Legs, back, arms – smooth”
If losing, don't panic – focus on technique, find a rhythm, then push again

sportsmanship.rule: Acknowledge other crews after race (nod or wave)
If you cause wash, apologise
Help rowers from capsized boats (safety first)

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local rowing club (USRowing, British Rowing, or national federation directory). Many offer “learn to row” courses.
Take a beginner course (usually 4‑8 weeks) – club provides equipment (boat, oars, life jacket).
After course, join novice team or recreational rowing.
Buy personal equipment: unisuit, rowing shoes (if required), sunglasses, water bottle (approx $100‑200).
Compete in novice regattas (500m or 1000m) after 3‑6 months of practice.
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Club fees: $200‑800/year (varies by facility); learn‑to‑row course: $150‑400; personal equipment: $100‑300; regatta entry: $20‑50 per event.

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Discipline – arrives early, rigs boat, follows training plan
Team player (sweep) – synchronises with crew, listens to cox
Pain tolerance – pushes through lactate during race
Coachability – accepts technical feedback
Resilience – recovers from bad strokes or losing races

racePacing.strategy: Maintain consistent split time throughout 2000m (optimal for most rowers)
Start very fast, then hold on – risky, only for elite with high lactate tolerance

strategies.power10: Call for 10 strokes at maximum pressure – used to make a move, respond to opponent, or at 500m intervals.

steering.strategy: Use minimal steering to stay straight; excessive rudder slows boat
In boats without rudder (pairs, fours), rowers apply more pressure on one side to turn

headRaces.strategy: Start at target pace, hold, then sprint final 500m – no need to react to others

specialSituations.situation: Crosswind – lean boat slightly into wind, cox steers accordingly
Choppy water – shorten slide, quicker catch, blade deeper
Leading – maintain pace, don't coast; trailing – increase stroke rate (e.g., +2 spm) and power

terminologies.term: Catch – blade entry into water at front of stroke
Drive – leg push phase (blade in water)
Finish – blade extraction from water at end of drive
Recovery – slide forward to catch (hands away, body swing, slide)
Feathering – turning blade horizontal (parallel to water) during recovery
Squaring – turning blade vertical (perpendicular) before catch
Stroke rate (spm) – strokes per minute (18‑40 depending on phase)
Split time – time per 500m (e.g., 1:45/500m)
Wash – wake created by boat
Rigger – metal frame holding oarlock
Oarlock (swivel) – U‑shaped bracket where oar rests
Blade – flat part of oar that enters water
Shaft – handle part of oar
Sculling – rowing with two oars per person
Sweep – rowing with one oar per person
Coxswain (cox) – person who steers and directs the crew (mandatory in eights)
Bow – front of boat (seat 1, also steers in sweep boats without cox)
Stroke – rower at the stern (sets rhythm)
Port – left side of boat (rower's left hand oar)
Starboard – right side of boat
Ergometer (erg) – indoor rowing machine (Concept2 common)
Rating – stroke rate
Power 10 – 10 strokes at maximum effort (tactic)
Settle – transition from start to race pace
Move – acceleration (e.g., “500m move”)
Head race – time trial over longer distance (e.g., 5000m)
Regatta – side‑by‑side race event
FISA – International Rowing Federation (Fédération Internationale des Sociétés d'Aviron)
USRowing – USA Rowing governing body
British Rowing – UK governing body

dimensions.shootingLine: Line where archers stand – marked with waiting line 3m behind
dimensions.targetLine: Line where targets are placed, spaced 2.5m apart (centre to centre)

layout.zones: Shooting lane (each archer has own lane), target butts (foam or straw), safety zone behind targets (at least 50m clear area)
layout.lines: Shooting line, waiting line, 3m line (for equipment), target number markers

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Flat grass, turf, or artificial surface – must be even and well‑drained
courtFieldImage.venues: Outdoor field with clear shooting lanes, safety netting or berm behind targets, wind indicators (flags)

physical.benefit: Strengthens upper body: shoulders (deltoids), back (rhomboids, lats), arms (forearm flexors)
Improves core stability (holding bow steady)
Enhances hand‑eye coordination and fine motor control
Low‑impact sport (easy on joints)
Burns 150‑250 calories per hour (light to moderate intensity)

mental.benefit: Develops focus, concentration, and patience
Reduces stress through rhythmic breathing and repetitive motion
Boosts confidence through measurable score improvement

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles, shoulder rotations, trunk twists, wrist circles, leg swings (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), resistance band rows (simulate draw, 10 reps), draw without arrow (5 reps), stretch band (shoulder external rotation, 10 reps)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Static stretches: shoulder stretch, triceps stretch, forearm flexor/extensor, upper back (cat‑camel), deep breathing (2 min)

basicRules.rule: Archers shoot arrows at target face from specified distance. Each arrow scores based on where it lands (10‑zone scoring).
Olympic recurve: 70m, 72 arrows (qualification), then elimination rounds (set system).
Ends: 3 or 6 arrows per end (depending on competition). Time limit per end: 2 min for 3 arrows, 4 min for 6 arrows.
Compound: 50m, 72 arrows (qualification), 15‑arrow matches (head‑to‑head).
Safety: Only shoot when range is clear (whistle signal). Arrows only drawn when pointing toward target.

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: Individual (1 archer) or team (3 archers, mixed or same gender).

matchTimings.elimination: Set system: 5 sets of 3 arrows each (approx 15‑20 min per match)

breaksIntervals.betweenEnds: 30‑60 seconds to retrieve arrows or prepare
breaksIntervals.timeout: 1 minute timeout per archer per match (elimination rounds)

formats.shortFormat: Set system (best of 5 sets, 3 arrows per set) – winner of set gets 2 points, tie 1 point, first to 6 points wins
formats.longFormat: 72‑arrow qualification (ranking round) – total score determines seeding

substitutesAndRoles.substitution:
In team events, substitute archer can replace injured member before match starts (not during). In individual, no substitution.
Lead archer (team) – shoots first, sets tempo Anchor archer (team) – shoots last, handles pressure

scoringSystem.innerTen: 10 points (yellow centre ring, 12.2cm diameter for 122cm target)

rulesRegulations.rings:
9,8,7,6,5,4,3,2,1 outward from centre. Outer ring (1 point) – 122cm total diameter. Inner 10 (often used as tiebreaker) – 6.1cm diameter. 10‑ring (4cm diameter) and inner X (2cm).
Highest total score after 72 arrows wins ranking. First archer to win 6 set points (best of 5 sets). In case of tie (5‑5), one‑arrow shoot‑off (closest to centre wins).
Using illegal equipment (e.g., release aid not allowed in recurve) – disqualification Shooting before signal, drawing bow without arrow, unsafe arrow handling – warning then point deduction or disqualification Exceeding time limit for end – deduction of highest scoring arrow or loss of set Shooting at wrong target – highest scoring arrow deducted (or zero for that end) Distracting other archers, arguing with judges – yellow card (warning), red card (point deduction), black card (disqualification)
No talking while archer on shooting line (except safety commands) Acknowledge opponent's good shots (nod or tap bow) Assist in arrow pulling (if asked) and safe retrieval
Director of shooting, target judges (score verification), line judges (foot faults) Archers score own arrows, but judges verify and resolve disputes (e.g., line cutters – higher score awarded if touches line) Used for edge‑of‑ring disputes in major events (World Archery, Olympics)

commonInjuries.injury: Wrist strain (bow hand or draw hand)
Lower back pain (prolonged standing, asymmetrical posture)
Finger nerve damage (draw hand – string pressure)

preventionMethods.method: Proper draw weight – start with low poundage (20‑30 lbs) and increase gradually
Use finger tab or release aid (recurve finger tab protects nerves, compound mechanical release)
Arm guard (protects forearm from string slap)
Strengthen rotator cuff (external rotation with band, scapular retraction)
Take breaks between ends (stretch, walk)
Maintain proper posture (back straight, shoulders relaxed, not hunched)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute shoulder or elbow injury (e.g., bursitis, tendon tear)
Uncontrolled hypertension (strain of drawing may spike BP)
Recent eye surgery (focusing at distance may strain)
Severe arthritis in fingers or wrists (grip and release painful)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have history of rotator cuff surgery, herniated disc, or carpal tunnel syndrome

commonMistakes.mistake: Gripping bow too tightly (torques bow, causes inconsistent shots)
Plucking string on release (sideways movement of fingers)
Dropping bow arm after release (affects arrow flight)
Peeking at arrow impact before follow‑through complete
Inconsistent anchor point (different reference each shot)
Improper stance (leaning back or forward)
Not using back muscles – over‑relying on arm strength

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 400‑600 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (long outdoor sessions)
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 150‑200 ml between ends (every 10‑15 min) – especially in hot weather
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink if heavy sweating (sun exposure)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Dry mouth, muscle cramps (draw arm), dizziness, blurred focus – stop, hydrate, inform judge if needed

nutritionTips.preMatch: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (oatmeal, whole grain bread, banana) + moderate protein (yogurt, egg) – avoid heavy fats
nutritionTips.preMatchSnack: 30 min before: apple, handful of almonds, or energy bar
nutritionTips.duringMatch: Water and small snacks between ends (dried fruit, nuts) – maintain steady blood sugar
nutritionTips.postMatch: Within 30 min: protein shake, chocolate milk, or chicken + rice (muscle recovery)
nutritionTips.daily: Calcium (dairy) for bone density; vitamin D (sunlight or supplement) for bone health; omega‑3 (fish) for joint health

physicalBurnout.limit: Shooting max 4‑5 days per week (elite); recreational 3‑4 days. Limit arrows per session: beginners 60‑100, advanced 120‑150.
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days or active recovery (light walking, stretching)
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, yoga, rowing (strengthens back without overloading shoulder)

mentalBurnout.signs: Lack of focus, frustration, plateauing scores, avoiding practice
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, set new goals (e.g., blank bale shooting for form, not score), try different bow type (barebow, compound)

bow.recurve: Olympic style – riser (aluminium/carbon), limbs (wood/carbon), string. No mechanical release.
bow.compound: Pulley system (80‑90% let‑off), mechanical release aid, peep sight, stabilisers.
bow.barebow: Recurve without sights, stabilisers, or clicker – traditional style.

arrows.material: Carbon, aluminium, or carbon/aluminium composite. Spine (stiffness) matched to draw weight.
arrows.points: Bullet or field points (target), 100‑120 grain weight.
arrows.fletching: Three feathers or plastic vanes (helical or straight).

accessories.item: Sight (recurve/compound) – adjustable pin or scope
Stabiliser rod (reduces bow vibration, improves balance)
Clicker (recurve – audible draw length indicator)
Bow stand, arrow rest, plunger button

equipment.apparel: Lightweight, close‑fitting clothing (no loose sleeves), flat‑soled shoes (athletic or archery specific), hat or visor (sun protection)

protective.item: Arm guard (mandatory for beginners – prevents string slap bruising)
Finger tab (prevents nerve damage and blisters)
Chest guard (optional – prevents string interference)
Safety glasses (recommended for compound shooters – peep sight may snap)

rangeSafety.item: Arrow puller (rubber grip for removing arrows from target)

safetyGear.firstAid: Antiseptic wipes, bandages (for string slap cuts or arrow nicks), ice packs, tweezers (splinter removal from carbon arrows)

coordination.desc: Hand‑eye coordination to align sight pin with target centre
coordination.drill: Blank bale shooting (no target face) – focus on form, 20 arrows

speedAgility.desc: Not primary in archery; more about consistent tempo and smooth execution
speedAgility.drill: Shot routine rehearsal – same timing for every arrow (e.g., 3 sec draw, 2 sec aim, release)

focusReaction.desc: Block out distractions (wind, crowd, other archers) and release at subconscious level
focusReaction.drill: Shoot with eyes closed after aiming (feel the shot) – 10 arrows

strategy.windCompensation: Aim off to upwind side; use sight ring to estimate drift

xFactors.shootingOrder:
In team matches, shoot quickly to put pressure on opponents
Read wind flags before each end; adjust sight if needed (e.g., 2 clicks left for crosswind) When to accept a 9 (safe) vs risk a 10 (if leading); in shoot‑off, aim for centre mass, not risky edge

dailyPlan.day: Form shooting (blank bale, 60 arrows) + Strength (resistance band rows, 3x10) + Stretching
Distance practice (70m, 72 arrows, record scores) + Wind condition drills (20 arrows with variable aim)
Rest or light shooting (30 arrows at 30m, focus on release) + Core strength (planks, bird‑dog)
Competition simulation (6 ends of 6 arrows, time limit) + Video analysis of form (30 min)
Mental training (blind bale) + Short distance (50m, 60 arrows) + Shot routine rehearsal
Mock match (set system against partner or recorded pace) – 3 matches + Cool‑down
Active recovery (walking, light stretching, or complete rest)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Practice stance (feet shoulder‑width, shoulders square) – 5 min without bow
Shoot at blank target from 5m (no scoring) – 30 arrows (focus on draw, anchor, release)
Use mirror to check string alignment to bow riser – 10 draws
Shoot one arrow, retrieve, repeat (30 times) – builds routine consistency

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Learn proper stance, nocking, draw, anchor, release, follow‑through – 3x per week (60 arrows/session)
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add sight picture, grouping at 30m, basic tuning (plunger, rest) – 4x per week (80 arrows)
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce distance (70m), wind reading, set system matches – 4‑5x per week (100 arrows) + local tournament
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine shot routine, competition psychology, advanced tuning – maintain 4x/week (80‑120 arrows)

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale during raise, exhale slowly during draw and hold, natural pause before release
Close eyes, imagine perfect shot sequence (from stance to follow‑through) – 3 min before practice
Same steps: nock arrow, set feet, hook string, raise bow, draw, anchor, aim, release, follow‑through – every arrow identical

pressureHandling.tip: In shoot‑off, focus on process (form), not outcome (centre). Trust your shot routine.
If you feel rushed, take an extra breath before drawing
Use self‑talk: “Back tension, smooth release, follow through”
After a bad arrow (e.g., 7), reset – don't analyse, just repeat routine for next arrow

sportsmanship.rule: Wait for your turn on shooting line; do not cross in front of another archer
Congratulate opponent after match (handshake or fist bump)
If you notice a scoring error (over‑score), correct it even if it reduces your own score

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local archery club (USA Archery, Archery GB, or World Archery affiliated)
Take a beginner safety course (usually 2‑4 hours, club provides equipment)
Rent club equipment for first 2‑3 months (bow, arrows, arm guard, finger tab)
After consistent interest, buy a beginner recurve setup (bow, arrows, accessories) – approx $200‑400
Join club tournaments (indoor/outdoor) after 6 months of practice
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Club fees: $50‑150/year; beginner bow kit: $200‑400; arrows (6): $60‑150; tournament entry: $10‑30

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Patience – does not rush, accepts that archery is a game of consistency
Self‑awareness – can feel small form errors and correct them
Resilience – recovers from a bad end without spiralling
Analytical – reviews scores and grouping to identify problems
Calm under pressure – executes same routine regardless of match situation

wind.strategy: In crosswind, aim to the upwind side of the gold (e.g., 1 ring left for 10 mph wind)
In gusty conditions, draw and hold until wind subsides (but stay within time limit)

qualification.strategy: Shoot 6 arrows per end, maintain same tempo throughout 72 arrows (avoid rushing early)
Between ends, drink and eat small snack to maintain energy for 2+ hours

elimination.strategy: If you win first set, shoot for centre (safe 9s) to force opponent to chase 10s
Practice one‑arrow shoot‑offs in training (simulate sudden death)

team.strategy: All three archers shoot within 10 seconds of each other to apply psychological pressure
Strongest archer shoots last in each set to handle pressure

specialSituations.situation: Leading by 4+ points in final end – play safe (aim for 9, avoid risky 10 attempts)
Trailing – take extra time, focus on centre, accept that one 10 may tie

terminologies.term: End – group of arrows shot before scoring (3 or 6 arrows)
Set – in elimination: 3 arrows per set, winner gets 2 set points
Gold – centre yellow rings (10 and 9 points)
X – innermost 10 ring (used as tiebreaker)
Line cutter – arrow touching line between scoring zones (awarded higher score)
Robin Hood – arrow splitting previous arrow (rare, but scores highest possible)
Grouping – cluster of arrows on target (indicates consistency)
Anchor point – consistent reference on face (e.g., under chin, finger to corner of mouth)
Back tension – using back muscles (rhomboids) to draw and hold, not arm strength
Clicker – device on recurve that clicks when at full draw (consistent draw length)
Plunger – button inside arrow rest that centres arrow and absorbs paradox
Paradox – arrow flexing during release (corrected by plunger and spine selection)
Spine – stiffness of arrow (too stiff = arrows left, too weak = arrows right)
Fletching – vanes or feathers on arrow (steer arrow in flight)
Nock – plastic piece at arrow rear that snaps onto string
Let‑off – compound bow's reduced holding weight at full draw (e.g., 80% let‑off means 50lb draw holds at 10lb)
Peep sight – small aperture on compound string for rear sight alignment
Stabiliser – rod with weights (reduces bow torque and vibration)
Bow sling – strap preventing bow from falling after release
String slap – bowstring hitting forearm (prevented by arm guard and proper elbow rotation)
World Archery – international governing body
USA Archery – US national federation

dimensions.transitionArea: Approx 20‑50m long, marked racks for bikes, numbered spots
dimensions.swimCourse: Rectangular or point‑to‑point, with buoys (turning markers) every 200‑400m
dimensions.bikeCourse: Paved roads, closed to traffic, with aid stations every 15‑20km
dimensions.runCourse: Paved or trail, with aid stations every 2‑3km

layout.zones: Swim start (beach or pontoon), transition 1 (swim to bike), bike course, transition 2 (bike to run), run course, finish chute

courtFieldImage.surfaceTypes: Open water (lake, river, ocean) for swim; asphalt for bike and run
courtFieldImage.venues: Outdoor: lake or ocean swim, closed roads for bike, park or urban run course

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (three sports, long duration)
Strengthens full body: swimming (upper), cycling (legs), running (legs & core)
Burns 800‑1500 calories per hour (depending on intensity and distance)
Low impact on joints (swimming) combined with weight‑bearing (running) for bone density
Enhances metabolic efficiency and fat utilisation

mental.benefit: Builds discipline, goal‑setting, and resilience (long training hours)
Reduces stress through varied, outdoor exercise
Improves mental toughness (pushing through fatigue in later stages)

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles, leg swings, torso twists, walking lunges, ankle rotations (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), swim specific: easy 100‑200m in water, bike: 5‑10 min easy spinning (if trainer available), run: strides (4 x 50m)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Light walking or jogging (5‑10 min), static stretches (hamstring, quad, calf, hip flexor, shoulder, back) 30 sec each, foam rolling for legs and back

basicRules.rule: Race order: swim → bike → run, with transitions in between. Timing includes transitions.
Wetsuits allowed (if water temperature below certain threshold, e.g., 24.5°C / 76°F for elite). No wetsuit above that.
Drafting (following another bike closely) is prohibited in non‑drafting races (most age‑group). Penalty time or stop‑and‑go.
Helmet must be worn and fastened before touching bike, and removed after racking bike. Chin strap must be fastened.
No headphones or music devices during race (safety).
Outside assistance (except from official aid stations) prohibited.

rulesRegulations.numberOfPlayers: Individual (age group or elite). Relay teams (2‑3 people, each doing one or two legs) allowed in many races.

breaksIntervals.aidStations: On bike: every 15‑20km; on run: every 2‑3km. Athletes take water/food while moving. For drafting or other violations – stop‑and‑go (30 sec to 5 min depending on race). Race continues; athlete may stop and receive medical aid, but cannot re‑enter unless cleared (rare).

substitutesAndRoles.substitution: In relay teams, each member does one leg; substitution before race only (not during). Individual no substitute.

rulesRegulations.scoringSystem: Time‑based (lowest total time wins). In age groups, ranks by finish time.
rulesRegulations.howToWin: Complete swim, bike, run (including transitions) in shortest total time.

foulsAndPenalties.drafting: Following within 7m of another bike for more than 15‑20 seconds (depending on race). Penalty: 2‑5 minutes time penalty or stop‑and‑go.
foulsAndPenalties.helmetViolation: Not wearing or unfastened while on bike – disqualification.
foulsAndPenalties.transitionViolation: Racking bike in wrong spot, helmet off before racking – time penalty.
foulsAndPenalties.littering: Throwing trash outside aid stations – penalty (e.g., 2 min).

fairPlay.rule: No deliberate obstruction; give way to faster athletes
Respect volunteers and officials

judgementCriteria.officials: Referees on motorcycles (bike), at transition, on run course. Penalty tent for stop‑and‑go.

rulesRegulations.timing: Electronic chip (on ankle or wrist) records swim exit, bike start/end, run finish.

commonInjuries.injury: Swimmer's shoulder (rotator cuff impingement) – from overuse in swim
Cyclist's knee (patellar tendinitis) – from poor bike fit or overtraining
Runner's knee (iliotibial band syndrome) – common in run leg
Lower back pain – from bike position or weak core
Stress fractures (foot, tibia) – rapid increase in run mileage

preventionMethods.method: Proper bike fit (saddle height, reach, cleat position) – crucial to avoid knee/back pain
Gradual build in weekly volume (no more than 10% per week in each discipline)
Strength training (core, glutes, hamstrings, rotator cuff) – 2‑3x per week
Warm‑up each discipline before hard sessions
Use proper running shoes (replace every 500‑800 km)
Incorporate rest days and active recovery (swimming, yoga)

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute respiratory infection (fever, cough) – risk of myocarditis, dehydration
Uncontrolled hypertension (sprinting on bike/run spikes BP)
Recent concussion (no play for 2‑4 weeks, graduated return)
Severe asthma without pre‑exercise medication (cold water or pollen trigger)
Active joint swelling (knee, ankle, shoulder)
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have heart murmur, arrhythmia, epilepsy, or history of heatstroke

commonMistakes.mistake: Starting swim too fast (adrenaline) – leads to hyperventilation, early fatigue
Poor sighting in swim (swimming off course, adding distance)
Forgetting to remove wetsuit fully in T1 (lost time)
Not having bike shoes clipped in before mounting (fumbling)
Starting bike too hard (burning matches before run)
Not hydrating or fuelling enough on bike/run (bonking)
Running too fast off bike (brick legs – muscle confusion)

hydrationImportance.beforeRace: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200‑300 ml 30 min before (with electrolytes if hot)

duringRace.bike: Sip 400‑800 ml per hour (depending on temperature), from aero bottle or aid stations. Use sports drink for electrolytes and carbs.
duringRace.run: Drink at every aid station (100‑150 ml water or sports drink). For half/full Ironman, take electrolyte tablets as needed.

hydrationImportance.afterRace: Replace 150% of weight lost (weigh before/after training to estimate). Use recovery drink with electrolytes, protein, carbs.
hydrationImportance.warningAvoid: Excessive plain water without electrolytes (risk of hyponatremia in long races), caffeine (diuretic, may cause GI issues)
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dark urine, headache, reduced performance – slow down, drink at next aid station, seek medical if severe

nutritionTips.preRace: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (pasta, rice, oatmeal, bagel) + moderate protein (egg, yogurt) – low fat and fibre to avoid GI distress
nutritionTips.preRaceSnack: 30‑60 min before: banana, energy bar, or rice cake with honey

duringRace.bike: Main fuel source: 60‑90g carbs per hour (energy gels, bars, chews). Practice race nutrition in training.
duringRace.run: 30‑60g carbs per hour (gels with water). Aid stations provide water, sports drink, sometimes cola, bananas, gels.

nutritionTips.postRace: Within 30 min: protein + carbs (recovery shake, chocolate milk, or whey + banana) for muscle repair and glycogen replenishment. Then solid meal within 2 hours (chicken + rice, pasta, vegetables).
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (red meat, spinach) for oxygen transport; calcium (dairy) for bone health; adequate protein (1.4‑1.8 g/kg body weight) for recovery

physicalBurnout.limit: Training: 5‑6 days per week with periodisation (hard weeks, easy weeks). Long course athletes: 10‑20 hours/week.
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 1‑2 full rest days or active recovery (walking, light swimming) per week
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Strength training, yoga, Pilates (core and flexibility, reduces overuse)

mentalBurnout.signs: Lack of motivation, dreading workouts, poor performance, irritability, sleep disturbances
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks easy (reduce volume by 50‑70%), focus on fun sessions (group rides, open water swim), set new small goals (e.g., improve swim technique), vary routes

swim.wetsuit: Neoprene (full sleeve or sleeveless), legal up to certain water temperature. Buoyancy aids hip position.
swim.goggles: Anti‑fog, UV protection, comfortable fit. Spare pair in transition bag.
swim.swimCap: Provided by race (colour coded by wave). Silicone or latex.
swim.earplugs: Optional – prevent ear infections from open water.

bike.bicycle: Road bike (clip‑on aero bars) or triathlon specific (TT) bike. Must have working brakes, bar end plugs.
bike.helmet: Aero or road helmet, must be CPSC or CE certified. Chin strap mandatory.
bike.shoes: Cycling shoes with cleats (Look, SPD‑SL, or Speedplay). For T1: shoes already clipped or put on after mount.
bike.bottles: At least 1‑2 bottle cages, aero bottle between aero bars.
bike.spares: Tubular or clincher tyres, CO2 inflator, spare tube, mini pump (or rely on race support).

run.shoes: Running shoes (lighter than trainers, but enough cushion for distance). Elastic laces (quick transition).
run.raceBelt: Holds race number, easy to clip on in T2.
run.hat: Visor or cap (sun protection, sweat management).

transition.bag: Transition bag holds all gear: towel, helmet, shoes, nutrition, race belt, sunglasses, sunscreen.

equipment.apparel: Tri suit (one‑piece) – quick drying, padded for bike (thin chamois), worn for swim/bike/run.
equipment.accessories: Garmin or GPS watch (multisport mode), sunglasses (polarised), body glide (anti‑chafe), sunscreen (water resistant).

protective.item: Helmet (mandatory on bike, must be fastened before mounting)
Bike lights (front white, rear red) – required in low light or some races
Wetsuit (for cold water – also provides buoyancy and thermal protection)
Safety whistle (attached to wetsuit or race belt) – for emergency
ID bracelet (emergency contact, medical info)
Sunscreen (SPF 30+) – apply before race and reapply in T2 if long course

safetyGear.firstAid: Antiseptic wipes, bandages (for cuts from wetsuit or crashes), blister plasters, ice packs (in race medical tents)

coordination.desc: Swim bilateral breathing, sighting; bike handling (cornering, drinking while riding); run form after bike (brick transition)
coordination.drill: Brick workouts: bike then run immediately (e.g., 30 min bike + 15 min run) to adapt to dead legs

speedAgility.desc: Quick transitions (T1 and T2), smooth mounting/dismounting bike
speedAgility.drill: Practice transition at home: set up mock transition, go swim (or simulate) → bike (trainer) → run. Time each transition.

focusReaction.desc: Stay aware of surroundings (other swimmers, traffic on bike, aid station placement)
focusReaction.drill: Open water sighting every 6‑8 strokes; practice drinking from bottle while riding (no‑hands)

strategy.pacing: Even effort across three disciplines; avoid starting too hard. For Olympic: 80% perceived exertion first half, build.

xFactors.nutrition:
Practice race nutrition: 60‑90g carbs/hour on bike, 30‑60g on run. Hydrate with electrolytes.
Read wind direction on bike (aero position saves watts); on run, use aid stations strategically (walk through to drink). When to push (on bike climbs, final 5km run) vs conserve (early swim, downhill sections).

dailyPlan.day: Swim technique (2000m: drills, intervals) + strength (core, legs) – 60 min
Bike (60‑90km endurance, zone 2) + brick run (15 min off bike)
Run (10‑15km tempo or intervals) + swim (1500m easy)
Bike intervals (6 x 8 min threshold, 2 min recovery) + transition practice
Run (long, 15‑20km steady) + swim (2000m open water if possible)
Long bike (100‑150km) + short brick run (10‑15 min)
Active recovery (light swim 1500m, walking) or complete rest

drillsForBeginners.drill: Ride 15 min on stationary bike, then run 5 min immediately – repeat 3 times (learn leg adaptation)
In pool: every 6 strokes lift head to spot a target on deck
Practice running with bike, swinging leg over, pedalling; and dismounting before rack
During long bike, take gel every 30 min with water; simulate race feeding

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Build base fitness in each discipline: swim 2x, bike 2x, run 2x per week, plus 1 brick. Total 6‑8 hours/week.
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add intensity (intervals, threshold work) and longer weekend sessions. Practice transitions and nutrition. 8‑12 hours/week.
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Race‑specific training: long bricks, open water swims, race pace efforts. Simulate race day conditions. 10‑15 hours/week.
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Maintain with periodised plan (3 weeks build, 1 week recovery). Enter sprint or Olympic races to gain experience.

focusingTechniques.technique: Swim: bilateral breathing rhythm; bike/run: 2:2 or 3:3 inhale:exhale pattern
Night before: imagine each segment (swim start, T1, bike, T2, run, finish). Positive outcome.
Same breakfast, same warm‑up, same pre‑swim activation (jumping jacks, arm swings)

pressureHandling.tip: In swim start (mass start), stay to the side to avoid kicks; focus on your pace, not others
If you feel overwhelmed, break race into segments: “just get to first buoy”, “just get to T1”, etc.
Use self‑talk: “Smooth and steady, I’ve done the training”
When suffering on run, count steps (1‑100) or focus on aid station to aid station

sportsmanship.rule: No drafting (intentionally); if you pass, move ahead and drop back within 15 seconds
Thank volunteers at aid stations
Encourage fellow athletes (especially in long races)

howToJoinSport.step: Join a local triathlon club or find a beginner training group (USAT, Triathlon England, etc.)
Enter a sprint distance event (most beginner friendly). Many offer novice waves.
Acquire basic equipment: swim goggles, road bike (or borrow), helmet, running shoes. Tri suit optional (wear swim trunks + shirt).
Practice open water swim (with safety buoy) before race.
After first sprint, decide on investment (tri bike, wetsuit, coach) for longer distances.
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Race entry: $50‑150 (sprint) to $300‑800 (Ironman). Equipment: beginner $500‑1500 (bike, helmet, shoes, wetsuit). Club fees: $50‑150/year. USAT license: $15‑50/year.

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Consistency – trains regularly, doesn't skip sessions
Patience – builds volume gradually, respects rest days
Resilience – bounces back from bad races or injuries
Self‑sufficiency – handles flats, nutrition, pacing without external help
Mental endurance – stays positive during long, tough races

pacing.strategy: Maintain same relative perceived exertion (RPE) across swim, bike, run. Avoid “blowing up” on bike.
Run second half of run leg faster than first (requires discipline on early run).

drafting.strategy: Stay in bike pack to save energy (20‑30% less effort). But need to be comfortable in close quarters.

transitions.strategy: Run with bike, swing leg over, start pedalling with shoes already clipped in (advanced).
Spread towel, place shoes, helmet upside down, sunglasses inside helmet.

nutrition.strategy: Start taking gels at 15‑20 minutes, then every 30‑40 min. Practice in training.
Even if not thirsty, take small sips to stay ahead of dehydration.

specialSituations.situation: Flat tyre on bike – know how to change (practice at home). Use CO2 inflator, carry spare tube.
Hot race day – wear white tri suit, pour water over head at aid stations, take salt tablets.
Cold or rainy – wear arm warmers, disposable poncho for bike, change into dry socks in T2.

terminologies.term: T1 – first transition (swim to bike)
T2 – second transition (bike to run)
Brick – workout combining bike then run immediately
Drafting – following closely behind another bike to reduce air resistance (illegal in age‑group triathlon)
Wetsuit legal – water temperature below 24.5°C / 76°F (elite) or lower (age‑group) allowing neoprene
Buoy – floating marker in swim course (turn, intermediate)
Sighting – lifting head to see buoys in open water swim
Transition bag – bag holding all gear for T1/T2
Race belt – elastic belt with race number, quick to clip on
Aero bars – extensions on handlebars allowing forward crouch (reduces drag)
TT bike – triathlon/time trial bike (aggressive geometry, aero)
Bonking – hitting the wall (glycogen depletion) – severe fatigue
Age group – amateur athletes grouped by age (e.g., 35‑39)
Elite – professional triathletes
Wave start – groups starting at intervals (e.g., by age or swim ability)
Mass start – all athletes start together (common in Ironman)
Cut‑off time – maximum time allowed to complete each segment (e.g., swim 2h20m for Ironman)
Penalty box – designated area where athlete serves stop‑and‑go penalty
USAT – USA Triathlon (governing body)
Ironman – brand (full distance 3.8‑180‑42.2), also generic for long distance
ITU – International Triathlon Union (now World Triathlon)
Challenge Family – another full distance triathlon series

dimensions.alleyWidth: 1.37 meters (4.5 ft) – doubles sideline to singles sideline

layout.lines: Baseline, singles sideline, doubles sideline, service line, centre service line, centre mark (baseline)
layout.zones: Deuce court (right side), advantage court (left side), forecourt (net area), midcourt, backcourt

surfaceTypes.surface: Clay (red or green) – slow, high bounce
Hard court (acrylic, concrete) – medium pace, consistent bounce

courtFieldImage.venues: Outdoor stadium (open or retractable roof), indoor courts (for cold/wet climates)

physical.benefit: Improves cardiovascular endurance (intermittent sprinting, long rallies)
Strengthens legs (quads, hamstrings, calves), core, shoulders, and forearms
Burns 400‑600 calories per hour (singles, moderate to high intensity)
Enhances bone density (weight‑bearing activity)
Develops agility, balance, coordination, and reaction time

mental.benefit: Reduces stress through focus and physical exertion
Improves strategic thinking, problem‑solving, and mental toughness
Builds discipline and emotional control (handling pressure points)

warmup.stretching: Dynamic: arm circles, trunk twists, leg swings, walking lunges, high knees, ankle rotations (10 min)
warmup.pregameExercises: Light jogging (5 min), mini‑tennis at net (5 min), groundstroke rally from baseline (5 min), serves (5‑10 at 50% pace), overheads (2 min)

cooldown.postgameRecovery: Light walking (3‑5 min), static stretches: hamstring, quad, calf, hip flexor, shoulder, triceps, wrist (30 sec each), foam rolling if available

basicRules.rule: Players (or teams in doubles) hit ball over net into opponent's court. Ball must bounce once before hitting (except serve).
Scoring: points (15,30,40, game), games (6 to win set by 2), sets (best of 3 for women, best of 5 for men in Grand Slams).
Serve must land in diagonally opposite service box; two attempts allowed (fault). Foot fault if foot touches baseline before hit.
Ball landing on line is in. Ball touching any part of line is good.
Let serve: ball touches net but lands in service box – re‑serve (no penalty).
Hitting ball on the fly (volley) allowed except on serve return.

rulesRegulations.matchTimings: Singles: 1‑3 hours (best of 3); Grand Slam men's 3‑5 hours; tiebreaks shorten time.

breaksIntervals.changeover: 90 seconds after odd games (e.g., 1,3,5), 2 minutes at set break
breaksIntervals.timeout: Medical timeout (3 minutes) for injury; bathroom break (one per match) – 3 minutes

formats.shortFormat: No‑ad scoring (next point wins game at deuce), super‑tiebreak (first to 10 points) instead of final set
formats.longFormat: Advantage scoring (deuce, advantage, game), best of 5 sets, tiebreak at 6‑6 (except final set in some tournaments)

substitutesAndRoles.substitution:
No substitutions in singles. In doubles, partners cannot be replaced during match (except injury retirement).
Baseline player – rallies from backcourt, consistent groundstrokes Serve‑and‑volley player – attacks net after serve All‑court player – mixes baseline and net play Doubles: net player (poacher), baseline player (returns, covers lobs)

scoringSystem.tiebreak: First to 7 points, win by 2 (at 6‑6 games)
scoringSystem.superTiebreak: First to 10 points, win by 2 (used instead of final set in many events)

rulesRegulations.howToWin: Win 2 out of 3 sets (women) or 3 out of 5 (men's Grand Slams). Each set won by winning 6 games with 2‑game lead, or winning tiebreak at 6‑6.

foulsAndPenalties.footFault: Serve: foot touches baseline or court before hitting – fault (no point loss unless second serve)
foulsAndPenalties.timeViolation: Exceeding time between points (25 sec) – warning, then point penalty
foulsAndPenalties.codeViolation: Unsportsmanlike conduct (racquet abuse, audible obscenity) – warning, then point, then game, then default
foulsAndPenalties.ballAbuse: Hitting ball out of court in anger – code violation
foulsAndPenalties.coaching: Receiving coaching during match (not allowed in most events) – warning then penalty

fairPlay.rule: Call own lines (in non‑umpired matches); if unsure, give benefit to opponent
No distracting opponent (shouting, waving, grunting excessively)
Shake hands at net after match

judgementCriteria.chairUmpire: Makes line calls, overrules, time violations, code violations

rulesRegulations.lineJudges: Call lines (in/out) – used in professional events Hawk‑Eye or automated system (used on ATP/WTA tours) – players can challenge limited calls

commonInjuries.injury: Tennis elbow (lateral epicondylitis) – overuse of wrist extensors
Wrist tendinitis (extensor carpi ulnaris) – from heavy topspin
Ankle sprain (lateral) – sudden direction changes on court
Patellar tendinitis (jumper's knee) – repetitive loading from lunges
Lower back pain – from serving rotation and bending

preventionMethods.method: Proper grip size (too small/large increases elbow strain); use softer strings (multifilament/natural gut) to reduce vibration
Strengthen forearm extensors (wrist curls reverse) and rotator cuff (external rotation with band)
Warm‑up with mini‑tennis and gradual intensity – no full serves cold
Use appropriate court shoes with lateral support; replace every 300‑500 hours
Cool‑down and stretch after play, especially shoulders and wrists
Take rest days – avoid playing with elbow or shoulder pain

diseasesToAvoidPlaying.condition: Acute elbow or shoulder tendinitis (acute flare‑up) – rest until pain‑free
Uncontrolled hypertension (sprinting spikes BP)
Recent concussion (no play for 7‑14 days, graduated return)
Severe asthma without pre‑exercise medication (cold or hot weather trigger)
Active wrist or ankle fracture
diseasesToAvoidPlaying.advice: Consult doctor if you have heart arrhythmia, epilepsy, or history of rotator cuff surgery

commonMistakes.mistake: Gripping racquet too tightly (tennis elbow, loss of feel)
Arching back excessively on serve (lower back strain)
Not watching ball onto strings (off‑centre hits)
Poor footwork – standing flat‑footed, not split‑stepping before opponent hits
Overhitting instead of placing ball (unforced errors)
Hitting on the run without setting feet (loss of control)
Double faulting under pressure (second serve too conservative or too risky)

hydrationImportance.beforeMatch: Drink 500‑700 ml water 2‑3 hours before; 200 ml 15 minutes before (especially in hot weather)
hydrationImportance.duringMatch: Sip 150‑200 ml at changeovers (every 2 games, approx 10‑15 min) – use sports drink if >1 hour in heat
hydrationImportance.afterMatch: Replace 150% of weight lost; electrolyte drink if heavy sweating
hydrationImportance.signsOfDehydration: Muscle cramps, dizziness, dark urine, headache, reduced concentration – take medical timeout, hydrate

nutritionTips.preMatch: 2‑3 hours before: complex carbs (pasta, rice, oatmeal, whole grain bread) + lean protein (chicken, egg, yogurt) – low fat and fibre
nutritionTips.preMatchSnack: 30‑60 min before: banana, energy bar, or rice cake with honey
nutritionTips.duringMatch: At changeovers: small bites of banana, orange slice, or energy gel (if >2 hours)
nutritionTips.postMatch: Within 30 min: protein + carbs (chocolate milk, whey shake + banana, chicken + rice) for muscle repair and glycogen replenishment
nutritionTips.daily: Iron (spinach, red meat) for endurance; calcium (dairy) for bone health; omega‑3 (fish) for joint health

physicalBurnout.limit: Competitive matches: max 3‑4 per week (singles); training: 4‑5 days per week with variety (drills, points, fitness)
physicalBurnout.restDays: At least 2 rest days or active recovery (swimming, light cycling) per week
physicalBurnout.crossTrain: Swimming, cycling, yoga (low impact, maintains fitness, reduces overuse)

mentalBurnout.signs: Loss of motivation, frustration after errors, dreading practice, poor shot selection
mentalBurnout.solution: Take 1‑2 weeks break, play for fun (no scoring), set small goals (e.g., improve slice backhand), vary practice (clay vs hard court)

equipment.racquet: Weight 260‑340g, head size 95‑110 sq in (MP to OS). Materials: graphite, carbon fibre, aluminium (beginners). String pattern 16x19 or 18x20.

strings.polyester: Low power, high spin, stiff – arm risk
strings.multifilament: Comfortable, moderate power, good for arm health

equipment.ball: Pressurised rubber, felt covered. Type 1 (fast), Type 2 (medium – standard), Type 3 (slow).
equipment.grip: Overgrip (tacky or absorbent) – replace regularly (every 5‑10 hours).
equipment.shoes: Court shoes with lateral support, non‑marking sole, herringbone tread (for clay) or modified herringbone (hard court).
equipment.apparel: Breathable polyester T‑shirt or polo, shorts or skirt (with pockets for balls), sweatbands (wrist, head).
equipment.accessories: Dampener (reduces string vibration), racquet bag, ball hopper (for practice), water bottle, towel.

protective.item: Elbow brace (counterforce brace) – for tennis elbow prevention/support
Wrist brace (if prone to tendinitis)
Ankle braces (for chronic instability)
Sunglasses (polarised, UV protection) – reduces glare, protects eyes
Sun hat or visor, sunscreen (SPF 30+)
Protective eyewear (optional, for doubles – prevents ball to eye)

safetyGear.firstAid: Ice packs, elastic bandage, antiseptic spray, blister plasters, instant cold packs, tape (for finger sprains)

coordination.desc: Hand‑eye coordination to hit ball cleanly, control spin and placement
coordination.drill: Ball bounce on racquet (forehand/backhand sides) – 100 consecutive; wall hitting (alternate forehand/backhand)

speedAgility.desc: Quick first step, lateral movement, explosive sprints, split step
speedAgility.drill: Cone drills (zig‑zag, figure 8), ladder drills (side shuffle, in‑out), sprint from baseline to net (10 reps)

focusReaction.desc: React to opponent's shot (0.3‑0.5 sec), anticipate direction from racquet angle
focusReaction.drill: Partner feeds random balls (deep, short, left, right) – react and hit; reaction ball drills

strategy.offensive: Aggressive baseline (heavy topspin, deep shots), serve‑and‑volley, attack net

xFactors.defensive: Lob, slice, moonball, counter‑punch (use opponent's pace)
xFactors.neutral: Consistent rally, wait for short ball, change direction
xFactors.gameSense: Read opponent's weaknesses (e.g., backhand, movement, net play), adjust shot selection
xFactors.decisionMaking: When to go for winner vs rally, when to approach net, when to lob

dailyPlan.day: Groundstroke drills (20 min cross‑court, 20 min down‑line) + footwork (15 min) + serves (15 min)
Volleys & overheads (20 min) + approach shot & net finish (15 min) + match play (30 min tiebreaks)
Fitness: sprints (6x40m), agility ladder (10 min), core (planks, Russian twists) – 30 min
Serve practice (first and second serves, slice/kick) (20 min) + return practice (20 min) + baseline rally (20 min)
Point play (serve + 1, rally to 4) (30 min) + video analysis (20 min)
Full match simulation (best of 3 sets) + cool‑down
Active recovery (light hitting, stretching, or complete rest)

drillsForBeginners.drill: Drop ball, hit over net – 20 reps each side
Toss ball without hitting – 30 tosses (consistent height and placement)
Rally from service line (or closer) – focus on control, not power
Hit against wall continuously – aim for 20 consecutive shots

skillImprovementProgram.phase1: Master basic grips (continental, eastern, semi‑western), forehand/backhand groundstrokes, serve motion – 3x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase2: Add volleys, overheads, slice backhand, approach shots, basic footwork – 4x per week
skillImprovementProgram.phase3: Introduce topspin (forehand/backhand), kick serve, tactical patterns (serve +1, rally + approach) – 4‑5x per week + local tournaments
skillImprovementProgram.phase4: Refine strategy, match play, fitness, video analysis – maintain 4x/week with competition

focusingTechniques.technique: Inhale 3 sec, exhale 5 sec – between points, before serve, or after long rally
Close eyes, imagine perfect serve (toss, contact, placement) or passing shot – 2 min before match
Same ball bounce count (e.g., 4 bounces), same deep breath, same glance at target

pressureHandling.tip: At deuce or break point, focus on making first serve (high percentage), not hitting an ace
After double fault, reset – bounce ball 5 times, self‑talk: “Next point”
Use self‑talk: “Watch ball, split step, early preparation”
When opponent has momentum, slow down (tie shoelace, towel off) to break rhythm

sportsmanship.rule: Call own lines honestly; if uncertain, opponent's favour
No shouting or racquet throwing
Acknowledge opponent's good shot (tap racquet strings or nod)
Shake hands at net (or wave if distance) after match

howToJoinSport.step: Find a local tennis club or public courts (USTA, LTA, or national federation directory). Many offer group lessons.
Buy a beginner racquet (pre‑strung, oversized head, 270‑280g) and can of balls (approx $50‑100).
Take group lessons (beginner level) – 4‑8 weeks. Club often provides balls.
Practice with a hitting partner (same level) or use ball machine.
Join a recreational league or enter novice tournaments (e.g., USTA NTRP 2.5‑3.0).
howToJoinSport.costEstimate: Club fees: $30‑100/month; group lessons: $15‑40/session; racquet: $50‑150; tournament entry: $20‑50.

qualitiesOfGoodPlayer.quality: Consistency – reduces unforced errors, keeps ball in play
Mental toughness – stays focused after losing points, handles pressure
Adaptability – changes tactics when plan not working
Footwork – always moving, split‑stepping, recovering to centre
Discipline – practices serves and fitness regularly

singles.strategy: Aim cross‑court (over lower part of net), target 2‑3 feet inside lines, use depth to push opponent back
If weak backhand, hit high topspin to backhand; if slow movement, use drop shots and wide angles
Serve wide, follow to net, cut off return with volley (effective on fast surfaces)
High deep topspin lobs to push opponent back, then short slice to draw error

doubles.strategy: Net player crosses to intercept return; communication with partner
Both players start on same side of centre line to confuse returner
Server's partner stands on same side as server (deuce court) – covers middle

surfaceSpecific.clay: Long rallies, heavy topspin, use drop shots, movement important (sliding)
surfaceSpecific.grass: Serve and volley, slice low, attack net, shorter points
surfaceSpecific.hardCourt: All‑court game, consistent bounce, good for aggressive baseliners

specialSituations.situation: Leading 40‑0 on serve – go for bigger serve or winner; leading 30‑40 (break point) – play safe, high percentage first serve
Trailing 0‑40 – focus on making first serve, use kick serve to backhand, play defensive
Tiebreak – stay aggressive, avoid double faults, aim for opponent's weaker side

terminologies.term: Love – zero points
Deuce – score 40‑40 (need 2 clear points to win game)
Advantage – point after deuce (Ad‑in for server, Ad‑out for receiver)
Break point – receiver's chance to win game on opponent's serve
Set point – point to win set
Match point – point to win match
Tiebreak – first to 7 points (win by 2) at 6‑6 games
Super tiebreak – first to 10 points (win by 2) instead of final set
Let – serve touching net but landing in service box (re‑serve)
Fault – serve landing outside service box or foot fault
Double fault – two consecutive faults (lose point)
Ace – serve untouched by opponent
Winner – shot that opponent cannot reach
Unforced error – mistake not forced by opponent's shot
Forced error – opponent hits good shot, you miss
Topspin – forward rotation of ball (dips down after bounce)
Slice – backspin (low bounce, stays low)
Kick serve – topspin serve (high bounce)
Slice serve – sidespin serve (curves wide)
Flat serve – no spin (fast, straight)
Volley – hit ball before it bounces
Half‑volley – hit ball just after it bounces (at feet)
Lob – high shot over opponent's head
Drop shot – soft shot that lands just over net
Approach shot – shot hit while moving forward to net
Passing shot – shot past net player
Moonball – very high deep lob (defensive)
Rally – sequence of shots
Split step – small hop as opponent hits (prepares movement)
Open stance – body facing net when hitting (modern forehand)
Closed stance – sideways to net (classic)
Hawkeye – electronic line calling system
ATP – Association of Tennis Professionals (men's tour)
WTA – Women's Tennis Association
ITF – International Tennis Federation
US Open, Australian Open, French Open (Roland Garros), Wimbledon – Grand Slams
